column2: Weights and Measures Dataset Version 1 Data-Envelope 1
column6: Weights and Measures Dataset Version 1 Data-Envelope 1
column18: This dataset was made to provide information on weights and measures used by the Dutch East India Company (Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie or VOC). The dataset is meant to be integrated into the GLOBALISE project as a whole, to serve as reference data for annotation of named entities, and to be used in future query expansion in the archives of the VOC. This dataset consists of data on weights and measures which were used by the VOC. It contextualizes units of weight and measure which were drawn from the eighteenth century work, the Memoriën van Munten, Maaten, en Gewigten als dezelve op Batavia en op de respective Comptoiren van Indië in gebruik zijn. The dataset consists of three different files: File 1: a glossary of weights and measures File 2: labels for weights and measures File 3: conversions drawn from the Memoriën.
column35: Parts of Indonesia, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, China, India, Yemen, Japan
column80: There are four areas in which the dataset will be improved for the next version. (1) Standardization of terms for commodities and locations (2) Adding of new definitions (3) Enriching conversions with other archival material and (4) Adding new units from secondary sources. See the documentation for more information about the proposed updates. Standardization 1. We will add URI’s for commodities 2. We will standardize locations and add location ID’s Definitions 1. We will add definitions from the Woordenboek der Nederlandsche Taal 2. We will add definitions from secondary literature for non-European weights and measures, from sources which are concerned with Asia 3. We will create our own GLOBALISE definitions, based on the conversions from the first version of the dataset Poolparty 1. We will add all the units from the VOC Glossary and the Boekhouder Generaal Batavia, including definitions and alternative labels Enrichment 1. We will add further conversions from primary material. Possibilities for this are lists from the archive from the 17th Century.

column2: Bookkeeper-General Batavia Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Bookkeeper-General Batavia Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The database Bookkeeper-General Batavia provides information about the circulation of commodities and the voyages of the Verenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie (Dutch East India Company) in the eighteenth century between the Dutch Republic and Asia. It contains information of more than 18.000 voyages and 250.000 commodities. Different aspects of the voyages are described, such as the name of the ship, the place and date of departure and arrival and the products and their values. The information was taken from the administration of the Beekkeeper-General located in Batavia, where only 55 years of the general journals have survived.
column34: Two series of books from the archive of the Bookkeeper-General are used in this database. These are 10751-10801 which covers the years 1700-1790, and 10802-10809 which covers the years 1771-1801. Together they cover a total of 55 years within the range 1700-1801. Each book year began on the 1st of September and was closed on the 31st of August of the next year, so a single calendar year is split over two books. From the series 1700-1790 the following book years are missing: 1704-1705, 1705-1706, 1707-1708, 1709-1710, 1713-1714, 1715-1716, 1716-1717, 1717-1718, 1718-1719, 1719-1720, 1720-1721, 1724-1725, 1727-1728, 1729-1730, 1732-1733, 1733-1734, 1735-1736, 1736-1737, 1739-1740, 1743-1744, 1744-1745, 1745-1746, 1746-1747, 1747-1748, 1748-1749, 1749-1750, 1755-1756, 1757-1758, 1764-1765, 1767-1768, 1769-1770, 1770-1771, 1772-1773, 1773-1774, 1778-1779, 1783-1784, 1785-1786, 1787-1788, 1788-1789. From the series 1771-1801, the following book years are missing: 1775-1776, 1779-1800

column2: 2023 Ground Truth Document Classification CABR Version 1 Data-Envelope Version 1
column6: 2023 Ground Truth Document Classification CABR Version 1 Data-Envelope Version 1
column18: This dataset contains ground truth (GT) that was created in order to train a document classification model for the Central Archives of Special Jurisdiction (CABR, 2.09.09), as part of the project War in Court (Oorlog voor de Rechter) [1]. ABOUT THE ARCHIVE This archive is held within the National Archives of the Netherlands. It contains files of around 425,000 people that were investigated within the Special Jurisdiction. Among them were people who had been members of the National Socialist Movement (NSB), or who were suspected of crimes such as betrayal or fighting in the German army. But also people who were investigated and found to be innocent. The archive contains around 30 million pages. Digitisation is ongoing and will finish in 2027. The archive contains a lot of different types of documents. They are also not grouped together within the files, and users are mostly interested in specific documents, such as verdicts and police reports. Furthermore, they have trouble reconstructing the judicial timeline, which document classification can facilitate. We therefore created this GT set to train a document classification model. ABOUT THIS GROUND TRUTH SET This is the first GT that was created. We classified on the level of one scan. We were able to look at scans before and after the one that we were classifying, in order to provide more context and thus a more accurate classification. In total, we classified 7,472 pages. The GT gives an insight in what type of documents the files in this archive consists of, although it is skewed towards cases that were treated in the Supreme Court of the Special Jurisdiction. The underlying documents are not part of the GT due to legal restrictions. We started creating the ground truth mid-October 2023, and finished the process late November 2023. We will create and share more ground truth as a separate data set. [1] You can find more information about the project at https://oorlogvoorderechter.nl/about-the-project/.
column28: Law|Criminal law and Criminology|Modern and contemporary history
column34: Scans of Dutch documents from the 1940’s and 1950’s make up the bulk of the ground truth. These documents are mostly evidence from before and during WW2, and legal documents from directly after WW2. Therefore, the ground truth would not be ideal to train a set of documents from outside this period and context.

column87: This spreadsheet lists ID's of the scans, with the categories and subcategories of document types which were assigned to them as ground truth by hand. The scan numbers themselves have been replaced by ID's, because the scan numbers could be used to find out which person or persons the classified scans relate to. BASIS OF THE GROUND TRUTH The set of scans used for the ground truth (GT) is not representative for the entire CABR. At the time of the GT creation, the vast majority of scans were of the ‘Bijzondere Raad van Cassatie’, comparable to the Supreme Court. There are collections of many other organisations in the CABR, which also contain different documents not available (enough) in the set we worked with. The set consists of a random sample of two datasets: 4,755 scans taken from a set of the ‘Bijzondere Raad van Cassatie’. 2,100 scans come from the TRIADO [1] project (the War in Court predecessor). The TRIADO set contains a sample from the entire CABR. At the time of sampling, there were 181,580 scans in the ‘Bijzondere Raad van Cassatie’ set, and 155,775 TRIADO scans. The random sample was related to the scanning timeframe of the CABR. The TRIADO dataset was already created and the project itself was completed. The ‘Bijzondere Raad van Cassatie’ scans only represent a subset of the total part of this part of the archive. There are files of many other organisations in the CABR. In other words: the random sample was created based on the scans available at the time. However, the CABR contains an estimated 30 million pages, so in order to have a fully representative sample of the documents in the archive, we have created more ground truth after finishing this first set. Scanning is ongoing and will not be completed until 2027. CREATING THE GROUND TRUTH Each scan in the dataset was manually annotated by the ground truth creators. We annotated individual scans of documents in a file. This file is related to a person. Each row in the dataset represents information related to one scan. The scans themselves are not part of the dataset, due to legal reasons. Each annotated scan (instance) is a row in a CSV file. The metadata fields are described the next section. There are some relations between instances. They can belong to the same file, or to the same person in cases where there are multiple files about that person. This is not made explicit in the data. The annotators had access to the scans in our closed web environment, in order for everyone working on the ground truth creation had access to the scans. The scans are not publicly available due to legal restrictions. Each scan was assigned to at least one top-level category (max. 3) and up a subcategory. The categories used are listed in the tab 'Document types - overview', and the hierarchical structure in '2023 Document classification GT anonymised - Hierarchical structure document'. The GT follows this structure. An example of a top-level category is Printed document (Gedrukt document), and an example of a subcategory is Verdict (Uitspraak). In some cases, it was not possible to determine a document subcategory. In those cases, all of the columns ‘Subcategorie 1’, ‘Subcategorie 2’, ‘ Subcategorie 3’ are empty. We supplemented the random GT with key documents that we wanted the document classification model to focus on. This is documented in 'GT addendum key documents'. It follows the same structure as random GT in the tab 'GT document classification'. There is a link for document types that are described in the WW2-Thesaurus. The thesaurus contains terms relating to events, places, concepts, and objects. column125: The archive contains evidence related to the Nazi occupation in the Netherlands and the roles Dutch individuals played in it. The dataset contains photos from World War II that depict swastikas and people giving the nazi salute. There are terms and slurs that will no longer be considered acceptable. There is also a lot of information about victims of the Nazi persecution. Furthermore, there are reports by coroners, pictures of dead bodies and descriptions of executions in the dataset. This data is however not present in this Ground Truth dataset.

column128: The archive itself contains files of around 425,000 people suspected of collaboration after World War II. Around 13% appeared in court, and another 19% did not have to appear in court but received sanctions from a special public prosecutor. In about 329,000 cases, the person was not punished. For example, if a person’s whereabouts were unknown, if someone had passed away, if the offenses were too minor for a punishment, or if the person was innocent. The reasons why people were suspected in the first place are not always known, and could be rooted in personal feuds. The scans that were annotated for this ground truth were mostly sampled from files of people that appeared in front of a court. This does not represent the majority of the archive, since only 13% of the suspects appeared in front of a court.
column129: We didn't take steps to mitigate biases in this round of ground truth. We will however in the future work with more representative samples of the archive, once more has been digitised.
column131: The CABR by default contains personal information, since the archive consists of files about people suspected of collaboration with the Nazis during World War II. This information is not present in this dataset, but in the archive this dataset is based on.

column157: This dataset is ground truth that was created in order to train a document classification model for the Central Archives of the Special Jurisdiction (CABR). This archive is held within the National Archives of the Netherlands. It contains files of around 425,000 people were investigated within the Special Jurisdiction. Among them were people who had been members of the National Socialist Movement (NSB), or who were suspected of crimes such as betrayal or fighting in the German army. But also people who were investigated and found to be innocent. The archive contains around 30 million pages. Digitisation is ongoing and will finish in 2027. The archive contains a lot of different types of documents. They are also not grouped together within the files, and users are mostly interested in specific documents, such as verdicts and police reports. Furthermore, they have trouble reconstructing the judicial timeline, which document classification can facilitate. We therefore created this ground truth set to train a document classification model.

column18: Deze onderzoeksgids biedt door institutionele beschrijvingen van de betrokken actoren inzicht in het functioneren van het Nederlandse emigratiebestel. Het ontsluit hun archiefmateriaal voor zover het betrekking heeft op het onderwerp emigratie. Om de onderzoeker op het spoor te zetten van archivalia over Nederlandse emigratie en emigranten in buitenlandse archieven, is verkennend onderzoek gedaan in Australië (2003) en Canada (2005). Kwantitatief zijn dit de twee grootste bestemmingslanden voor Nederlanders in de onderzoeksperiode. De gids pretendeert geen volledigheid, maar wil door een combinatie van breedte- en diepteontsluiting vooral een handreiking bieden om zoekstrategieën of nieuwe vraagstellingen te ontwikkelen en zo verder wetenschappelijk onderzoek stimuleren.

column18: This is the overarching collection maintained by The Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision (NISV), NISV is one of the most important Dutch audiovisual archives. It collects, looks after and provides access to over 70% of the Dutch audio-visual heritage. This collection is a combination of the items directly owned by NISV, and items that are stored by NISV on behalf of other organisations ('tenants'). The collection consists of both the media (where available) and the descriptive metadata. Access varies for media and metadata so check the Access section. This collection excludes items stored at NISV whose metadata is not allowed to be published. It also excludes items from collections that have yet to be integrated into the metadata asset management system at the NISV (e.g. Press collection, computer games, Muziekweb) The collection primarily contains audiovisual items, but also paper documents (e.g. TV guides), photos (e.g. production photographs) and objects (e.g. props). The items in the collection have been chosen according to the selection policies of NISV and of institutions and individuals who originally created collections stored at NISV. Policies have changed considerably over time. For more information about the impact of selection on the completeness of the collection, see the data-envelopes that describe specific sub-collections of Sound & Vision.

column87: The metadata describes the items in the Sound & Vision collection. There are 200-300 metadata fields in total. Metadata fields fulfil three purposes. Fields such as description, title and genre describe the content of an item (technical and semantic). Fields such as collection and heritage value describe the provenance and value of an item. Fields such as material type and sort date provide context that is helpful in finding the item. The most important and most frequently filled-in metadata fields are described in the Data Fields section. See data-envelopes of specific sub-collections for data fields of particular relevance to those sub-collections. Archive items are described as the type Program. There may also be descriptions of parts of the archive item, called Scene Descriptions. Programs may be grouped together into Seasons and/or Series. The carriers on which archive items are stored are described as the type Asset Item. In addition, there may be timed information about audiovisual objects, such as subtitles or recognised faces. Metadata can be filled in at all these levels, with different fields being relevant at different levels. Information about the content of the item and the related persons/locations/organisations is mostly stored at Series, Season, Program or Scene Description level. Some metadata originally comes from institutes that merged to form Sound & Vision. Some metadata comes to Sound & Vision together with the archive item itself. Other metadata is added by Sound & Vision. Metadata comes from both manual human input and technical sources such as automatic face recognition. The amount of metadata filled in for an item varies according to the type of item (as not all metadata fields are relevant for all types) the priority it has been given by archival metadata policies, and the metadata techniques in use at the time the item was described. As a consequence, the metadata varies greatly, and you should be aware that this will affect the items that you can find when searching, and the quality of any analysis you do on the metadata. We recommend carefully reading the documentation of the sub-collection you use. Refer also to https://data.beeldengeluid.nl/nl/showcases/data-fractures for large changes in the archival systems that affect all collections, such as the introduction of a new metadata system, the use of a thesaurus for certain metadata fields or the strong reduction in manual annotation. The metadata is available in the open access and restricted access portals listed in Section 2.4. Note that the exact names of metadata fields may differ slightly between portals. Metadata may not be downloaded. To download metadata either see the Open Catalog collection or contact Sound & Vision to see if special arrangements can be made.|These are the actual media stored by Sound & Vision. These vary greatly, including digitised films, digitised TV and Radio broadcasts, photographs of props, scans of documents etc. Some are available for anyone to browse via the open access public portal. Others are restricted, and are only available to view via the restricted access portals. See Section 2.4 for a list of portals. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis.

Because of these underlying questions, the focus for constructing the dataset was on annotating aspects of use and reuse of research designs, data and infrastructure, which were not specific categories in the original classification scheme for citation motivation that we adapted.

column16: Baptism registers of Amsterdam 1546-1811|Doopregisters van Amsterdam 1546-1811
column18: In the 'Baptism registers of Amsterdam 1546-1811' dataset are details taken from baptism registers (or from similar registers of births) of various church communities and the Register of the Regiment Oranje-Nassau. Regulations for keeping baptism registers were laid down during the Council of Trent (1545-1563). The oldest baptism register of Amsterdam dates from one year after the council. On 26th January 1714 the mayors decided that the municipality of Amsterdam was required to keep birth registers. In July 1792 the Dutch states formed a resolution to record the birth date and birth place in the registers. In 1807 the reformed church council took the decision to add ‘son’ or ‘daughter’ to the registration. In the registers are mostly the first names of the children and the full names of the parents and witnesses, along with the date of the baptism. The date of birth was initially not registered. In almost all registers the name of the cleric who carried out the baptism is noted. In the margins of the registers there are sometimes comments about e.g. changes of name, the issuing of birth certificates, or important functions of the parents or witnesses in the town council. In 1810 the Netherlands was absorbed by the French Empire and the civil registry was introduced, a register of the population according to the French model. In 1811 the churches were required to hand over their registers to the civil registry. After 20th October 1892, following a decision of the Mayor and Aldermen, all baptism registers from before 1811 were moved to the Amsterdam City Archives. This dataset consists of: - a digital index for the baptism registers, this can be searched and downloaded - digital scans of the baptism registers, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly These are described in further detail in the Resources in Level 3. The dataset does not describe paper indices or other non-digital resources available only in the reading room. For more details about this dataset read the links in section 2.4
column34: The registers are from different church communities who started keeping registers at different moments. For example, the earliest Catholic baptism registered is in 1628. For more details, see the links in section 2.4

column87: The index of the baptism registers contains selected key details from the baptism records, including the child's name, birth date, baptism date and names of parents. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find baptism records. The index is downloadable as a zip archive of xml files. Each file contains a field indexRecords, which contains a number of fields called indexRecord. Each indexRecord represents one baptism record. For the entry of the details from the period 1564-1701 a paper index was used that was created in the 1950s. This index was digitised in ca. 2008. The digitised index records were manually entered to create a digital index. In this index only the names of the baptised child and the parents were included, and not other information such as the names of the witnesses and the pastor. So unfortunately it is not possible to search on the names of the witnesses or pastor prior to 1701. For the period 1701-1811 all details from the original registers were entered in the index (even the marginal notes). The list of Data Fields in 3.2 indicates which information is available in the index. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk|The digital scans of the baptism registers show all the details of the baptism records. Individual scans of register pages can be viewed by searching the baptism index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page, and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. Note that this scan is for the complete page of the register on which the record in the search result can be seen. The scans can also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The original pages of the baptism registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2008. The scans contain more information than contained in the index. For example, in 1807 the reformed church council took the decision to add ‘son’ or ‘daughter’ to the registration.

column111: The registration of twins. In the Lutheran church the baptisms were written per year in one register, ordered alphabetically on the first letter of the name of the child. So you first find, in time order, all the names starting with an 'a', then a 'b', then 'c' and so on. The names of twins naturally often didn’t start with the same letter. To keep the registrations of twins together they were registered twice, once alphabetically on the name of one child, then once alphabetically on the name of the other child.
column113: The various church communities introduced registers at various moments. For more details see the links in section 2.4. Very occasionally a book can be missing. The registration in the register was done by the cleric that performed the baptism. He wrote the name as he heard it and there was no check on completeness or spelling. Therefore names can be written in very different ways. The sextons at first didn’t always keep to the instructions about registration. Sometimes the names of the mother and the witnesses are missing. It took until 1611 before the church council discussed checking the books. When entering the information from the digital scans of the index records into the digital index, errors can have been made in transferring or interpreting the details, in particular with special characters and unclear first or last names. For some names you would expect a J but see an IJ. The letters I and J were frequently interchanged previously and also used together. For example: De Bruijn, Kuijper, but also IJan de IJong. Sometimes there is a ‘ in a name. This means that the name was abbreviated. For example, Jo’es means Johannes, W’m means Willem and Fred’k means Frederik.|Very occasionally a book can be missing.

column18: Muziekweb.nl (previously called Stichting Centrale Discotheek Rotterdam, CDR) offers a collection of audio carriers (CDs, LPs, phonograph cylinders etc.) that have been collected since 1961. The audio carriers contain classical, popular and non-western music. The collection consists of the physical carriers, the digitised audio and the descriptive metadata. Muziekweb.nl has CDs, DVDs, LPs, 78 RPM records and phonographic cylinders in its collection. The CDs and DVDs are intended for lending, the other carriers cannot be borrowed and are also not consistently or completely catalogued, with the exception of the phonographic cylinders, which can be found on muziekweb.nl and listened to in their entirety.

column87: The metadata that describes the music in the collection can be searched via the Muziekweb website. It is also possible to listen to audio fragments (or when access via a library, the entire audio). Digital photos of the front and back of the covers have been made of all CDs and almost all LPs in the collection.Digital photos have been made of the lids of the storing tubes of the phonographic cylinders. Certain parts of the collection can be borrowed via the website, see Data section on CDs and DVDs. To work with the metadata directly, see the related datasets section. The structure and form of the metadata is mainly based on the Pica cataloguing format, using the international ISBD regulations. There are approximately four hundred metadata fields in the Muziekweb system, but these are not all necessarily in (frequent) use. There are all different types of metadata, from technical metadata about the audio, to barcodes on the CD cases and the text of a CD description. The metadata available on muziekweb.nl describes the content of the music CDs and DVDs. It consists of information about the CDs (label, label number, barcode, title, main artist etc.) and information about the tracks that can be found on the CD (track number, duration, titles, performer(s), composer (for classical music) etc.). The most important metadata fields are those of 1) the artist names and 2) the classical work descriptions. The artist names (persons and organisations) are enriched with various metadata, such as ISNI numbers and links to Wikidata. Wikidata links are automatically created during ingest and checked if a problem is reported. The classical music descriptions contain more than 250,000 uniform titles, systematically constructed according to the rules of Muziekweb, linked to the composer (artist name), tagged with a genre keyword and available in 4 languages (NL, EN, DE, FR). These metadata fields are described in the Data Fields section. A full overview of all metadata fields is available on request. Links to External Resources (see Section 3.5) have been generated, mostly automatically. These can be edited manually where necessary. All CDs in the collection are ripped with an application developed in-house. The audio is stored in multiple formats (.mp3, .wav, .flac). Audio fragments from muziekweb can be shown on other sites (wee website for instructions). The website is availble in Dutch, English and French. Part of the metadata is available in these languages. Metadata, audio and photos should not be copied from the website. For users with an account, metadata can be accessed via an API (see Access in Section 2). Muziekweb retains all rights to the data.|The CDs and DVDs in the Muziekweb collection can be borrowed via the Dutch libraries. Borrowing can be done via the website.

column185: Cataloguing the metadata of the music carriers
column189: Since 1961 tens of cataloguers have described and catalogued LPs and CDs for muziekweb.nl . As of Spring 2025 there are eight cataloguers (3 women and 5 men, all with Dutch nationality and Dutch speakers) working on creating and maintaining the (meta)data in Muziekweb. Four of them are musicologists, who work mainly on classical music. The other four concentrate on the CDs with popular music (including jazz) and non-western music. In the past, the number of cataloguers was substantially larger, particularly in the pop department. The classical department was more stable, with a core of 4 employees, sometimes supplemented by a fifth for a few years (mostly men, one woman) Some of the cataloguers also look after the ordering, delivery and processing (including the ingest of basic metadata such as barcodes) of newly appeared CDs and DVDs, and as a result maintain contact with various suppliers of commercially available audio carriers. The employees more and more make a selection of what should be acquired for the collection, whereas up until a few years ago pretty much everything that appeared in the Netherlands was acquired. Partly because of this, the departure of a colleague with thorough jass knowledge has had consequences for the purchase of CDs and DVDs with this repertoire (so there are fewer). Most cataloguers also write reviews for a selection of newly published CDs and DVDs. Annotation is literally what is on the CD case, so this is independent of influence from the annotators.

column16: Dutch Prize Papers - Bills of lading
column18: The Dutch Prize Papers Bills of Lading dataset is a subset of the Dutch Prize Papers database, in which Prize Papers are digitally available. The subset contains bills of lading. Prize Papers are documents that were confiscated by the British Navy and privateers from enemy ships in the period between 1652 and 1815. In total 35,000 ships were captured, from which a large number of documents were retrieved as they were required as evidence to prove the legality of the capture. For more details about the Dutch Prize Papers in general, see the data-envelope for the Dutch Prize Papers dataset. Bills of lading - in Dutch 'cognossementen' or 'ladingbriefjes' - describe the goods being transported and obtained a legal significance. These documents were usually thrown away upon arrival after transaction, which makes this collection special.

column87: The bills of lading were selected from the Dutch Prize Papers collection using custom Python scripts written in Jupyter Notebooks (see link in section 2.4) that detected a number of characteristic words and phrases in the automatic transcription, using fuzzy matching, to identify a document as a bill of lading. These words and phrases can be found clearly listed in the Jupyter Notebook in the code. As these words are Dutch, the collection only contains Dutch bills of lading. As the automatic transcripts contain errors, some bills of lading may be missed. The scans contained in this dataset are copies of the original scans held by the National Archives in The Hague, The Netherlands, and are stored at the Huygens Institute. The scans can be searched and viewed via the Dutch Prize Papers website, see the link in Section 2.4.|The metadata and transcriptions come from the original Dutch Prize Papers collection. The metadata includes the ship's name, ship's master, capture date and language of the document. The automatic transcription was produced by the Loghi Handwritten Text Recognition tool. The metadata and the automatic transcriptions can be searched and viewed via the Dutch Prize Papers website, see the link in Section 2.4.

column142: This source is the collection of Dutch Prize Papers of all types. This was used as the basis to make the selection of bills of lading. The Dutch Prize Papers database makes digitally available a selection of mainly Dutch documents from the Prize Papers. Prize Papers are documents that were confiscated by the British Navy and privateers from enemy ships in the period between 1652 and 1815. In total 35,000 ships were captured, from which a large number of documents were retrieved as they were required as evidence to prove the legality of the capture. The Dutch collection consists of more than 38,000 letters and other papers. It contains various types of documents: ships logs, cargo manifests, bills, plantation lists, letters of recommmendation, interrogations of crew members but also letters from and to sailors, merchants and their families. The letters come from all sections of society; most are written by women and most never reached their destination. The database (see link in section 2.4) can be searched on ships name, captain, date of capture and language. For each document scans of the original pages are available, and also the automatically generated transcription.|The National Archives in Kew, London, possesses the metadata of the original Prize Papers collection. This was used to create the selected metadata for the Dutch Prize Papers, of which the Bills of Lading are a subset

column149: This is a historical dataset and as such the content of the scans reflects the social and economic structures of the time. Only documents from ships that were taken over by British ships, and for which this takeover was judged to be justified by the High Court of Admiralty (HCA), came into this collection. Documents may have been lost over time. Being a selection, this dataset may not contain all relevant papers to Dutch ships that are present in the original Prize Papers collection. Also, some selected papers could not be preserved in time to be scanned, and some large documents could not be scanned.

column18: The Dialectology and Ethnology departments made (about 5,000) recordings of dialect speakers and storytellers, among others. Transcriptions have been made of the recorded texts. The collection is digital and consists of scans of a selection of transcriptions of the free conversations (soundbites) recorded in the Netherlands and Flanders. There is both an anonymised version of the transcriptions and a non-anonymised one. In total, more than 11,000 scans are involved. The transcripts are available as JPEG (anonymised) and as TIFF. The anonymised version can be consulted web-based (just like the audio tapes) via the Meertens Institute website. As part of the KNAW project Stimulating Open Access (2012), the scans have also been stored at DANS (both anonymised and non-anonymised).

column18: The dataset is part of the output of the REPUBLIC-project, which provides access to the handwritten and printed resolutions of the Dutch States General between 1576 and 1796. The resolutions of the States General in the Dutch Republic are a key resource to the political history of this period as they contain all decisions made by the States General (SG), the central ruling body in the Republic. The dataset consists of multiple data resources, each containing a specific type of entity that has been extracted from the resolutions. The motivation of creating the dataset is to facilitate searching the entities within the resolutions. The entity types are: -committees (COM) -dates (DAT) -roles (HOE) -locations (LOC) -organisations (ORG) -persons (PER). Per entity type, except for dates, there is a resource that contains the sections of text detected as referring to this entity type (e.g. annotations-layer_PER), a resource that contains a list of the standardized entities produced by curating these sections of text (e.g. PER entities), and a resource containing the occurrences of these entities in the text of the resolutions (e.g. PER annotations). The entity type 'dates' does not have a list of standardised entities associated with it.

column87: Through an automated process, nearly two million text elements in the resolutions were identified as personal names. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_PER' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the same name were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'PER-annotations' (see below). The PER-entities data resource described here contains the standardized surnames to which the 'PER-annotations' data resource refers. The over 8,000 names in this resource together account for about half of the name mentions appearing in the resolutions. The remaining name mentions concern names that appear only once or a few times. These are not part of this dataset. For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|Through an automated process, almost 140,000 text elements in the resolutions were identified as committees. Committees consisted of a number of delegates to the States General to handle specific matters. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_COM' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the same committee were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'COM-annotations' (see below). The COM-entities data resource described here contains the standardized committee names to which the 'COM-annotations' data resource refers. For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|Through an automated process, more than two million text elements in the resolutions were identified as 'role'-entities. A role can be an occupation (e.g. carpenter), an office (e.g. burgomaster), a judicial status (e.g. widow) et cetera. These role designations are typically found in the resolutions near the names of individuals. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_HOE' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the same name were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'HOE-annotations' (see below). The HOE-entities data resource described here contains the standardized roles to which the 'HOE-annotations' data resource refers. For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|The data resource contains location-entities whose occurrences are mentioned in the text of the resolutions. Through an automated process, more than two million text elements in the resolutions were identified as locations. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_LOC' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the same location were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'LOC-annotations' (see below). The LOC-entities data resource described here contains the standardized location names to which the 'LOC-annotations' data resource refers. This resource contains all (found) locations that appear more than once in the resolutions and is therefore fairly complete: an identified location was assigned to more than 90% of the text elements that were recognised as a location reference. Text elements where this was not possible were omitted from this dataset. For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|Through an automated process, nearly 600,000 text elements in the resolutions were identified as organisations. 'Organisation' refers to an official or less official association of individuals that the compilers have considered as an organisation, e.g. a government agency, a military unit, or a religious institution. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_ORG' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the organisation were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'ORG-annotations' (see below). The ORG-entities data resource described here contains the standardized organisation names to which the 'ORG-annotations' data resource refers. Approximately 80% of the text elements that refer to some form of organisation could be identified as a known organisation. The remainder that could not be identified was not included in this dataset. For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|This data resource contains the occurrences of the person-entities in the text of the resolutions. It contains an identification number that refers to the standardised form in the PER-entities data resource (see above). For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|This data resource contains the occurrences of the committee-entities in the text of the resolutions. It contains an identification number that refers to the standardised form in the COM-entities data resource (see above). For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|This data resource contains the occurrences of the role-entities in the text of the resolutions. It contains an identification number that refers to the standardised form in the HOE-entities data resource (see above). For the method of entity extraction, see https://github.com/huygensING/republic-project/|Through an automated process, nearly 160,000 text elements in the resolutions were identified as mentions of dates. The raw output of this process is included in data resource 'annotations-layer_DAT' (see below). This initial output was then curated. Different variants of the same date were linked to a single standard form. The result is included in the data resource 'DAT-annotations' which is described here. The format of the dates is YYYY-DD-MM. This dataset excludes both dates that do not refer to another resolution and date mentions that cannot be linked to a specific calendar date. column6: The Mondrian Papers Version-0.1 Data-Envelope Version-1.0
column18: The Mondrian Papers is a scholarly digital edition of the correspondence and theoretical writings of the Dutch artist Piet Mondrian (1872-1944), developed in cooperation with the Netherlands Institute for Art History (RKD), The Hague. The edition consists of digital facsimiles end XML/TEI based transcriptions, English translations and critical annotations of the letters and wirings. Entities such as mentioned people and artworks are tagged, described and linked to the RKD archive for further information. The edition aims to be an aid to research into Mondrian's life and work and his living and working environment. The edition is a work in progress and will be published in parts. Currently available materials cover letters from 28 February 1892 to 20 June 1919 and three texts Mondrian (co-)published in the journal De Stijl between 1917 and 1919.

column157: The Mondrian Papers aims to fill a gap in (art) historical knowledge about Piet Mondrian and contemporaries. Not before have all the letters by and to Mondrian been published. A small number of editions of individual correspondences is available, only a few dozen out of more than 1,700, moreover, many of them, are published only in Dutch. In the Mondrian Edition Project, all letters will be published in the source language (Dutch, French, English and a few in German) and, if missing, will be provided with an English translation. The edition provides a new and complete picture of Piet Mondrian's views, personality and network.

column18: Muziekweb.nl (previously called Stichting Centrale Discotheek Rotterdam, CDR) offers a collection of audio carriers (CDs, LPs, phonograph cylinders etc.) containing music released in the Netherlands, which have been collected since 1961. The audio carriers contain classical, popular and non-western music. A subset of the descriptive metadata for these carriers is available as Linked Data. The collection has approximately 600,000 CDs, 300,000 LPs and 30,000 DVDs, with altogether more than 17 million tracks. The linked data includes: - information about pop, classical, jazz and world music - links to streaming services to listen to music - objective and reliable descriptions of albums, artists and music styles

column86: Muziekweb Linked Data
column87: The Muziekweb linked data is available to browse on the data website (see Path) and can be also accessed via the SPARQL endpoint (https://data.muziekweb.nl/MuziekwebOrganization/Muziekweb/sparql/Muziekweb), which permits querying the data. A number of example queries are supplied on the website. The data can also be downloaded (https://data.muziekweb.nl/MuziekwebOrganization/Muziekweb/assets/5e96feb56ac1c3036f26eaa4). The linked data uses the Muziekweb vocabulary (https://data.muziekweb.nl/MuziekwebOrganization/Muziekweb/)to model the metadata. This is an extension of the widely used schema.org (http://schema.org) vocabulary. Entities include Albums, MusicAlbums, MediaObjects, and CreativeWorks. Artists are described with the entities Performer, Artist and MusicGroup. Ordering information is contained in the entities Offer and OrderInformation. The most important properties of these entities are described in the Data Fields in Section 3.2. The Muziekweb Linked Data is generated from the Muziekweb metadata. The model (schema) and the mapping from the Muziekweb data to Linked Data following the schema were developed by Triply in 2019, in consultation with Muziekweb and according to their wishes and requirements. The model and mapping were formally accepted by Muziekweb on 9th September 2019. The model is published. The mapping is property of Muziekweb and is currently not published. Triply uses the mapping to create Linked Data from the Muziekweb data. This is done daily. Triply hosts the data in its own system.

column16: Administrative documents of the Amsterdam College of Mayor and Aldermen 2015 - present day
column18: The administrative documents comprise the agendas, decisions, executive proposals and attached documents of the meetings of the Amsterdam College of Mayor and Aldermen, the Amsterdam districts and the Amsterdam municipality of Weesp. The agendas and decisions are made available under the terms of the WOO (Wet Openbaar Overheid - Open Government law) that took effect on 1st May 2022. The executive proposals and attachments are made available by the City of Amsterdam voluntarily. This dataset contains the agendas and decisions of the College since 2015, and the executive proposals and attachments of the College since February 2024. It contains the agendas and decisions of the Amsterdam districts and the Amsterdam municipality of Weesp since July 2023, and the executive proposals and attachments from their meetings since February 2024. (Note: The complete archive of the college is also stored at the Amsterdam City Archives. After enough time has elapsed this will also be made public. In the meantime it is possible to request access to this archive by contacting the City Archives of Amsterdam.)
column34: This dataset contains the agendas and decisions of the College since 2015, and the executive proposals and attachments of the College since February 2024. It contains the agendas and decisions of the Amsterdam districts and the Amsterdam municipality of Weesp since July 2023, and the executive proposals and attachments from their meetings since February 2024. Some documents between 2015 and 2024 have not yet been migrated to the new system and so are missing from this dataset. New documents continue to be added from current meetings.

column86: Open administrative documents of the Amsterdam College of Mayor and Aldermen 2015 - present day
column87: The administrative documents of the college are created for the meetings by civil servants in agreement with the mayor or the alderman who is responsible for the relevant policy area. Before being discussed in the meeting, they are checked by the legal department or the secretarial office. The agendas and decisions are published after the meeting. The executive proposals and attachments are published if the college decides that they should be shared. They may not be published if they contain sensitive or confidential information. The administrative documents of the districts and municipalities are prepared by civil servants from the relevant district, in agreement with the daily management. The civil servants check the content and legal aspects before they are discussed in the meeting. After the meeting, the daily management decides whether the document will be published or not, following the WOO (Wet Openbaar Overheid - Open Government law) rules. They may not be published if they contain sensitive or confidential information. The documents are stored in the administrative decision-making application of the Amsterdam local authority. When the publish checkbox is ticked they are transferred to the City Archives. Names and contact addresses and other private information is blacked out automatically. Publishing is done weekly. The agenda is delivered prior to the meeting. The other documents follow after the meeting. Documents are automatically checked for completeness and integrity. Problems are flagged for a manual check. The documents can be searched, browsed and read online in the City Archives of Amsterdam or OpenAmsterdam websites. The documents can be searched for particular words, and/or filtered on the dossier name, document category or dossier date. The document name, dossier, date and document category are shown for each document. Note that the metadata field names differ slightly between Open Amsterdam and the City Archives. Both names are shown in the list of data fields in Section 3.2. Note also that the publication date of a meeting and the number of the meeting dossier can be found by clicking on the Dossier link on the City Archives website. An individual document or the set of documents relating to a given meeting (dossier) can be downloaded if the user enters their email address. The email address is deleted after the download link is sent. Note that downloading individual documents can lead to loss of context. The inventory of all administrative documents can be downloaded from the City Archives page.

column6: MORE Moral Residue Reading List Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: This reading list contains the metadata, including title and author, of selected stories intended to help medical practitioners through moral residue. This arises when they are confronted by situations of unavoidable moral failure, where it is impossible to make the right choice. They may experience self-directed negative feelings, such as self-blame, regret or unease, after the failure of a medical decision with unavoidable negative outcomes. The books are originally in a variety of languages, but have been translated to English for the reading list. The reading list was created during the MORE project. For more details, see the project link in Section 2.4. For details of the metadata included in the reading list, see Section 3.2
column34: The publication dates of the books range from 1838 to 2022, with the exception of Greek Tragedy (approx 405BC)

column87: The list was created during the MORE project, see link in Section 2.4. The list consisted initially of 3 extracts of moral residue stories selected by the dataset creators, namely an extract of (1) Atonement, by Ian McEwan (2001); (2) Amok, by Stefan Zweig, originally published in German in 1922; and (3) The Grasshopper (also translated as The Butterfly), a short story by Anton Chekhov, originally published in Russian in 1892. These were added to by fiction books/stories and/or poems that medical practitioners interviewed in the MORE project have read and that have resonated with their own experiences of “moral residue". See Section 3.2 for an explanation of the metadata available for each work in the list.

column201: The creators were members of the MORE project: Olivia Fialho (Senior Researcher in the MORE project) is Senior Researcher at the Huygens Institute (KNAW, Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences) and Assistant Professor in Comparative Literature at Utrecht University. She has published in empirical, cognitive, and computational literary studies, and in literature education, including Scientific Approaches to Literature in Learning Environments (2016) and Transformative Reading (2024). In her work, she seeks insights from interdisciplinary domains to study the personal and social impact of reading and promote its benefits to people’s lives. She is the creator of Transformative Reading as an evidence-based program of literature teaching that impacts readers’ cognitive and emotional capacities, such as reflection on self and others, empathy, compassion, moral understanding, and motivation to read literature, among other benefits. The program is applied in education (primary, secondary, higher) and business (leadership). Her work at MORE entails adapting Transformative Reading to medical ethics. She is Associate Researcher in LCE (Literature, Cognition and Emotions), University of Oslo, and coordinates the IGEL coalition group Openess, Intensive Reflection and Self-Altering Literary Reading. Jan Helge Solbakk (PI in the MORE project) is a medical doctor and theologian, and he holds a PhD in Ancient Greek Philosophy. Since 1996, he has been Professor of medical ethics at Center for Ethics, University of Oslo. Between 1996 and 2011, he was Adjunct Professor of medical ethics at the Center for International Health, University of Bergen. In 2007 and 2008, he was Head of the UNESCO Bioethics Programme, UNESCO Headquarters, Paris. His current research focus is on irresolvable moral conflicts and the nature of moral learning. Solbakk has coordinated and been involved in various international research and capacity building projects relating to bioethics teaching, research ethics and integrity in research, research biobanking, personalised medicine, nanomedicine, stem cell research and the ethics of organoid research. Additional literature for the reading list was suggested by medical practitioners who were interviewed about their experiences with the reading list as part of the MORE project. The interviewees were healthcare professionals who had expressed interest in research on moral residue and inescapable moral failure and who had lived through moral residue situations in the workplace. They were a mix of male and female, and included Dutch, South Africans, Norwegians, 1 Kenyan, and 1 Iraqi. Among these participants, four were published writers on moral residue-related topics. For information on the interview participants, see the data-envelope for the MORE interview transcripts.

column2: Dutch Prize Papers Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Dutch Prize Papers Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The Dutch Prize Papers database makes digitally available a selection of mainly Dutch documents from the Prize Papers. Prize Papers are documents that were confiscated by the British Navy and privateers from enemy ships in the period between 1652 and 1815. In total 35,000 ships were captured, from which a large number of documents were retrieved as they were required as evidence to prove the legality of the capture. The Dutch collection contains various types of documents: ships logs, cargo manifests, bills, plantation lists, letters of recommendation, interrogations of crew members but also letters from and to sailors, merchants and their families. The letters come from all sections of society; most are written by women and most never reached their destination. The database (see link in section 2.4) can be searched on ship's name, captain's name, date of capture and language. For each document scans of the original pages are available, and also the automatically generated transcription.

column86: Dutch Prize Papers - Scans|Dutch Prize Papers - metadata and transcriptions
column87: The Prize Papers are kept in the Archive of the High Court of Admiralty (HCA) in The National Archives in Kew, London. Approximately a quarter of these come from Dutch ships. Two series have been selected: HCA 30 series consists of miscellaneous Admiralty papers. HCA 32 series consists of papers relating to cases concerning ships captured. The Prize Papers were examined at the National Archives in Kew, London. Collection experts made a selection of boxes of papers that contained large amounts of Dutch papers. These represented a spread both in time and in geographical location. As the boxes contained mixes of documents, some papers not pertaining to Dutch ships (and also in other languages) were also included. The papers from the selected boxes were preserved and then scanned on location in London, and the scans were checked. Due to the need for preservation prior to scanning, some selected papers were not scanned when preservation could not be completed on time. Also, some large documents could not be scanned. Approximately 60m of the estimated 750m of Dutch documents in the Prize Papers collection were scanned. The scans are stored by the National Archives of the Netherlands. The scans contained in this dataset are copies of the original scans, and are stored at the Huygens Institute. The scans can be searched, viewed and individually downloaded via the Dutch Prize Papers website, see the link in Section 2.4 Images have been reproduced by permission of The National Archives, London, England. The National Archives give no warranty as to the accuracy, completeness or fitness for the purpose of the information provided. Images may be used only for purposes of research, private study or education. Applications for any other use should be made to The National Archives Image Library, Kew, Richmond, Surrey TW9 4DU, see: www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/imagelibrary/.|This is the metadata that pertains to the papers that were scanned, and the automatic transcription of each scan. The metadata for each scan was retrieved from The National Archives (UK) website and translated to Dutch or, where the metadata for the scan was absent, the relevant details were obtained by reading the scan. The metadata includes the ship's name, ship's master, capture date and language of the document. Scans are grouped into inventories. The 72000 scans are distributed over 87 inventory numbers. To aid search, the scans were processed by the Loghi Handwritten Text Recognition tool to produce automatic transcriptions. https://github.com/knaw-huc/loghi. 100 scans were manually annotated with the ground truth of the text they contained, using the Transkribus platform (https://www.transkribus.org/). These scans and their ground truth were used to train the Loghi model before it was applied to produce automatic transcriptions for all scans. The metadata and the automatic transcriptions can be searched and viewed via the Dutch Prize Papers website, see the link in Section 2.4.

column16: MORE Moral Residue Reading Interview Transcripts
column18: This dataset contains transcripts of thirty interviews conducted with health care practitioners on the topic of moral residue, based on their experiences with a reading list developed for the purpose of helping practitioners live with and through moral residue situations. Moral residue is the anguished sense of responsibility that individuals may experience in response to a perceived moral failure, even though others would be wrong to hold the individual responsible. Moral residue can arise when health care practitioners are confronted by situations of unavoidable moral failure, where it is impossible to make the right choice.

column87: The dataset consists of transcripts of interviews conducted with thirty health care practitioners on the topic of moral residue, based on their experiences with a reading list developed for the purpose of assisting practitioners with moral residue situations. The recruitment process was lengthy due to health care practitioners having very little time. Recruitment was via Amsterdam UMC, KNAW and Utrecht University. Each participant filled in a consent form. Prior to the interviews, each participant was given three text extracts from the reading list to read. The extracts were in English. The participants were also free to suggest their own books. The time between receiving the reading list and the interview varied due to the need to plan interviews to fit the participant's schedule. Participants in the Netherlands were interviewed in person at a location of their preference, those in Norway, Kenya, South Africa and the UK were interviewed online. The participants took part in two interview sessions of 60 minutes each. Only the interviewer (dataset creator) and the participant were present. Participants were asked to select three to five fiction books/stories and/or poems that they have read and that have resonated with their own experiences of “moral residue. If they could not think of any title, then 3 extracts of moral residue stories were offered to them, namely an extract of (1) Atonement, by Ian McEwan (2001); (2) Amok, by Stefan Zweig, originally published in German in 1922; and (3) The Grasshopper (also translated as The Butterfly), a short story by Anton Chekhov, originally published in Russian in 1892. In the first session, the participants reported their most meaningful reading experiences of the moral residue stories. In the second session, they selected five evocative passages from the book that had the most impact on their understanding of moral residue. Participants were instructed to describe aloud any thoughts, feelings, images, or memories evoked by each passage. After reading, they were asked to describe aloud any thoughts, feelings, images, or memories evoked by the text overall. To acquaint them with the procedures, participants rehearsed answering the interview questions by referring to the most recent fiction book they had read. The interviews were conducted according to a specifically designed interview schedule, which is described in the paper linked in Section 4. Audio recordings were made of the interviews. These were transcribed manually, in full, by a Native English speaker (hired) or a Native Dutch speaker (research assistant) depending on the language spoken. Most interviewees spoke English. Dutch participants were free to speak Dutch if they chose. After transcription, the audio recordings were destroyed. ***Metadata for the transcripts?***

column201: Olivia Fialho (Senior Researcher in the MORE project) is Senior Researcher at the Huygens Institute (KNAW, Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences) and Assistant Professor in Comparative Literature at Utrecht University. She has published in empirical, cognitive, and computational literary studies, and in literature education, including Scientific Approaches to Literature in Learning Environments (2016) and Transformative Reading (2024). In her work, she seeks insights from interdisciplinary domains to study the personal and social impact of reading and promote its benefits to people’s lives. She is the creator of Transformative Reading as an evidence-based program of literature teaching that impacts readers’ cognitive and emotional capacities, such as reflection on self and others, empathy, compassion, moral understanding, and motivation to read literature, among other benefits. The program is applied in education (primary, secondary, higher) and business (leadership). Her work at MORE entails adapting Transformative Reading to medical ethics. She is Associate Researcher in LCE (Literature, Cognition and Emotions), University of Oslo, and coordinates the IGEL coalition group Openess, Intensive Reflection and Self-Altering Literary Reading. The interviewees were healthcare professionals who had expressed interest in research on moral residue and inescapable moral failure and who had lived through moral residue situations in the workplace. They were recruited during the period of September 2022 - June 2025 via the networks of Amsterdam UMC (12 participants), the Medical Humanities Program at Utrecht University (2 participants), the Centre for Medical Ethics at the University of Oslo (2 participants), and via one research assistant at the Huygens Institute (KNAW, 9 participants).They were reimbursed any travel and parking costs, and received 10 euros per hour for participating in the interviews. From the total of 25 participants who took part in this study, 16 self-identified as female and 9, as male. The average age was 44 years-old (SD=16,4 Range 21-77). 17 were Dutch, 4 South Africans, 2 Norwegians, 1 Kenyan, and 1 Iraqi. Regarding their profession, 12 were medical doctors (11 of which, specialists – 2 anestheologists, 2 pediatricians, 1 vascular medicine, 1 urologist, 1 reproductive medicine, 1 obstetrician, 1 hematologist, 1 acute medicine, 1 forensic psychiatrist), 6 were nurses, 2 ethicists (1 working with ethics of care; 1 Humanistic counsellor at the Justice Department or prisons), 1 medical practitioner (working on emergency), 1 physiotherapist (specialized in chronic pain), 1 therapist, and 1 was a retired lawyer in the health care sector. Among these participants, four were published writers on moral residue-related topics. The sample had a general high level of educational achievement (48% with a PhD degree and/or specialization). For information on the literary experience and preference of the participants, see the paper linked in Section 4

column18: The 'Repertorium van Ambtsdragers en Ambtenaren' is an online, searchable database containing information about the members and highest-ranking officials of the most important representative, administrative, and judicial institutions in the Netherlands. This systematic overview, covering more than 12,000 individuals and approximately 25,000 appointments, is intended as a general reference work and also serves as a foundation for analytical research into the actual functioning of institutions and politics. With the Repertorium researchers can investigate who was involved in politics, administration, and justice, the extent of continuity in institutional staffing, and how careers progressed.

column18: This is the Public Broadcaster Television collection maintained by the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision (NISV). It contains recordings of Dutch public broadcast television. The coverage of the archive has varied over time, see Section 3 for more details. The very first Dutch TV broadcast has not been preserved. The earliest recording is from 1951. At present all Dutch-made TV productions on public service broadcasters are recorded. Twice a year, an entire week of Dutch TV is recorded, including non-Dutch productions, programme trailers and adverts. The collection consists of both the audiovisual TV recordings (where available) and the descriptive metadata. Access varies for media and metadata so check the Access section. Standard access is via portals. Download of metadata, e.g. for computational analysis, requires special arrangements with NISV. Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy of the media may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis. Initially, only recordings deposited at NISV were preserved. With the introduction of magnetic tape in 1970 the selection became more representative. In 1987, NISV started to periodically digest a whole week of Dutch television. In 1997 the policy changed from archival of deposited material to active selection of TV broadcasts, which NISV recorded themselves. From 2006 onwards NISV was hooked up to public broadcaster production systems and all Dutch-made public service broadcast TV was stored.

column87: Metadata was originally entered by Sound & Vision archivists. Increasingly, metadata was imported from the production systems of programme makers until manual annotation ceased almost completely. Currently, production metadata is enriched by detecting faces and voices. The Sound & Vision Television collection uses the general Sound & Vision metadata model, described in the data-envelope ***link***. See this data-envelope for information about the general metadata fields available and about significant changes to metadata models and systems over time. In Section 3.2 we describe the metadata fields that are of specific interest to the Television collection. The metadata is available in the open access and restricted access portals listed in Section 2.4. Note that the exact names of metadata fields may differ slightly between portals. Note that the portals contain additional television, such as incidental recordings of commercial broadcasters or international news exchange programmes. Use the portal filters to filter these out if you wish. Very few programmes are preserved from the early years of TV, and these are often fragments. The numbers increase over time, but archival was dependent on voluntary deposits until 1997. From that point onwards, Sound & Vision actively selected programmes for recording. From 2006 all Dutch-made productions (that were not repeats) were stored. From 2010 onwards subtitles were stored. From 2014 onwards speakers were detected, and from 2020 onwards faces. Between 2014 and 2019 subject terms were extracted from subtitles. From 2020 onwards 'clean feeds' - audiovisual material without titles or captions - are also stored for news programmes. Some programmes have been run through a speech recogniser to generate a speech transcript. Metadata fields are not filled in for all programs. To get an idea of the coverage of a given metadata field, you can use the Inspect tool in the Media Suite|These are the actual TV recordings stored by NISV. Some are available for anyone to browse via the open access public portal. Others are restricted, and are only available to view via the restricted access portals. See Section 2.4 for a list of portals. Recordings may not be downloaded from restricted access portals. Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis.

column201: Selection of archival items was performed by Sound & Vision archivists according to the institutional selection policy in effect at that moment in time. The aim was to preserve Dutch cultural heritage from the audiovisual media. Metadata policy determined which of the selected items were described, in how much detail and with which techniques. Attitudes about which media count as cultural heritage and how valuable certain types of archive items are have changed over time, affecting these policies. These policies were also affected by the interest of the broader media community in the reuse of archival material. Within the policies, selection and description were influenced by the particular interests, expertise and biases of the archivists. Archivists were predominantly Dutch nationals and native Dutch speakers.

column16: Archive of Cold Case Team Anne Frank
column18: This dataset contains a portion of the archives of the Cold Case Team (CCT) Anne Frank, including copies of archived documents, interview reports and investigative reports. The dataset is a mix of sources and interpretations. In 2017 the company Proditione Investigative Media BV (Proditione) set up the CCT international investigation team, led by former FBI agent Vince Pankoke. The team researched the arrest of Anne Frank in the style of a ‘cold case’ investigation, not as a purely historical investigation. The team searched multiple archives, including both public and restricted information. Interviews were held with researchers who had previously conducted research on this topic, and with descendants of investigated persons. The results of this investigation were published in the book 'The Betrayal of Anne Frank', by Rosemary Sullivan. The book describes twelve of the thirty scenarios investigated by the team. Publisher AmboAnthos removed this book from circulation in the Netherlands on 22nd March 2022 after a group of researchers, led by Professor of Jewish Studies B. Wallet from the University of Amsterdam presented a report refuting the allegations of the Cold Case Team against their chief suspect. More information about the research, the reactions to it and opinions on the value of this dataset is available via the links in Section 2.4. The interview and contact reports, research reports and the names of the (sub)folders and files are in English. The collected archival material from Dutch archives is in Dutch.

column87: A portion of the CCT archive, containing 8170 documents, was transferred to the City Archives of Amsterdam in 2023. After transferral a number of files were removed, as these didn't directly refer to the investigation. A number of duplicate files that were not saved in logical locations were removed. 7500 documents remained. The dataset consists of: - Reports of interviews with researchers and the descendants of investigated persons - Reports of investigations of the CCT in the various archives - Collected research data, including copies of archival material from various archives, researchers, descendants and the internet These files were saved in a folder structure, stored in an Apple environment. The City Archives transformed this to a Windows environment, and converted approximately 150 files from an Apple format to a Microsoft format. The original (sub)folder names and filenames were retained. Some documents are stored multiple times in different (sub)folders. The main structure contains the following folders: 1. Inbox 2. Staff 3. Tasking 4. Information and Evidence 5. Last scenarios There is a folder per person. Persons include interviewees, suspects, next-of-kin and historical figures. The researchers used Microsoft Azure to transcribe video and audio files, automatically translate documents and to answer questions such as ‘who is connected to this address?’. Neither these algorithms, nor the questions asked and answers given by the algorithms, are included in the dataset. The CCT did not ask interviewees to approve their interview reports. The City Archives of Amsterdam did this, where possible, in the end of 2023 / start of 2024. There are no or few documents available from the period 2016-2017 or 2020-2021 The dataset does not contain the organisational details or contracts of the team, nor any correspondence with the author of the book. It also does not contain filmed interviews and their transcripts, as Proditione reserved these for a potential documentary. Researcher N. Gerson, one of the people given access to the dataset prior to publication, delivered some important missing documents. Some interviewees supplied the City Archives with an annotated version of their interview reports. The missing documents and annotated reports each have their own section in the dataset. The dataset is accessed via two inventories, in HTML. One of these contains the folder/file structure, which can be expanded, and is useful to get to know the folder structure and/or to search in a structured way. The other contains the expanded version of the folder/file structure, allowing the folder/filenames to be searched. Searching in the content of the files is currently not possible. Publicly available documents can be viewed by clicking on them. For other documents only the filename is visible. For some of these documents, information about when the document will become publicly available is shown after the filename. It is possible that some large files (larger than 9 Mb) can’t be opened. Ask the City Archives customer service for help. For more details on the dataset, such as frequently used abbreviations, see the links in Section 2.4

column6: Marriage banns, proclamation and marriage registers of Amsterdam 1565-1811 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Ondertrouw-, proclamatie- en trouwregisters van Amsterdam 1565-1811 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column16: Marriage banns, proclamation and marriage registers of Amsterdam 1565-1811|Ondertrouw-, proclamatie- en trouwregisters van Amsterdam 1565-1811
column18: In the 'Marriage banns, proclamation and marriage registers of Amsterdam 1565-1811' dataset there are details of banns, proclamations and marriages in Amsterdam, taken from the church and civil registers. Regulations for keeping marriage registers were laid down for the Roman Catholic Church during the Council of Trent, (1545-1563). To avoid clandestine marriages the Council decided that a marriage was only valid when it was blessed by the pastor or priest in the presence of two or more witnesses. Such marriages had to be registered. After 1578 the Protestants in Amsterdam took on the rules of the Council of Trent. In the same year, it was decided that everyone wishing to marry, regardless of faith, would be required to follow the same marriage banns procedure (ondertrouw). On 28th August 1586 Amsterdam created an ordinance requiring couples planning to marry to appear in front of the magistrate. Intended marriages were registered in the marriage banns register, including details such as name, place of birth, age etc. The banns were proclaimed and noted in the proclamation registers. Once the banns had been proclaimed without hindrance, and all necessary documents and proofs had been submitted, the betrothal was recorded as successful, and the marriage could take place. The marriage itself was recorded in the marriage registers, which simply contained the names of the bride and bridegroom, and the location and time of the wedding. More marriage banns were registered than actual marriages performed. In 1810 the Netherlands was absorbed by the French Empire and the civil registry was introduced, a register of the population according to the French model. In 1811 the churches were required to hand over their registers to the civil registry. After 20th October 1892, following a decision of the Mayor and Aldermen, all marriage registers from before 1811 were moved to the Amsterdam City Archives. The marriage registers are divided into the marriage banns registers (church, civil and extraordinaris); the proclamation registers (individual churches and town hall); and the marriage registers (individual churches and town hall). This dataset consists of: - a digital index for the Marriage banns registers, this can be searched and downloaded - digital scans of the Marriage banns registers, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly - digital scans of the Marriage proclamations register, these can be browsed but not searched - digital scans of the Marriage registers, these can be browsed but not searched. These are described in further detail in the Resources in Level 3. The dataset does not describe paper indices or other non-digital resources available only in the reading room. For more details about this dataset read the links in section 2.4
