column87: The index of the marriage banns register contains selected key details from the marriage banns records, including the names of the couple, any previous spouse, and the date. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find marriage banns records. The index is downloadable as a zip archive of xml files. Each file contains a field indexRecords, which contains a number of fields called indexRecord. Each indexRecord represents one marriage banns record. Intended marriages were initially registered in a single marriage banns register. From 28th January 1581 onwards two registers were maintained, a church register for members of reformed churches and a civil register for 'dissenters'. Where one partner was from a reformed church and the other a dissenter, the marriage was registered in both registers. Between 1578 and 1636 a third marriage banns register was maintained, for marriages where one partner did not live in Amsterdam, the 'extraordinarisregister'. From June 1795 up until February 1811 the dual register system ceased to exist and all marriage banns were registered in a single register. The marriage banns registers contain information about the couples who registered their intention to marry. In the 1950s, the City Archives of Amsterdam manually created a paper index to the registers. This index was digitised in ca. 2007/2008. The digitised index records were manually entered to create a digital index. The index covers the following inventories: 406-660 - the Church marriage banns register 661-761 - the Civil marriage banns register 762-765 - the 'Extraordinaris' marriage banns register for persons living outside of Amsterdam 988 - the marriage register of the Oude Kerk between Easter 1565 and August 1578 (as in this period there are no marriage banns registers) 1008 - marriage register for church marriages registered afterwards (e.g. because they occurred elsewhere) The list of Data Fields in 3.2 indicates which information is available in the index. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk|The digital scans of the marriage registers show all the details of the marriage records. The scans can be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. Marriages of couples where both were members of reformed churches were performed in the church. Marriages of dissenters had to be performed at the town hall. The church marriages were registered in marriage registers by the sexton. From 2nd June 1604 onwards, following a new ordinance, the marriages in the town hall were also registered. The original pages of the marriage registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2008. The marriage registers only contain the names of the bride and bridegroom and the date and location of the marriage. For this reason, they have not been indexed and so can't be searched. The marriage registers are divided into two groups: - Trouwregisters van het Stadhuis (Civil marriage registers), inventories 766 - 816 - Trouwregisters van de Kerken (Church marriage registers), inventories 969 - 1038 To find the marriage record of a bride and bridegroom, you should first search the marriage banns registers (see the Resource ‘Marriage banns registers index 1565-1811' in Section 3). Look up the marriage register that fits the religion of the bride and groom (if the marriage banns are in the civil banns register then use the civil marriage register, if the banns are in the church banns register then use the church marriage register), and browse a period of about two to five weeks after the date of the marriage banns.|The digital scans of the proclamations registers show all the details of the proclamation records. The scans can be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The registers have not been indexed and so cannot be searched. If there was no impediment found when registering in the marriage banns register, then the couple received permission to proclaim the banns. This proclamation was recorded, in one of two different proclamation registers depending on the faith of the couple. If both were from a Reformed church, then the proclamation was recorded in the church register, otherwise it was recorded in the civil register. Three stripes in the register indicate that the banns had been successfully proclaimed three times. Once this was done, then a couple was formally bound, and required to officially get married within a month. The original pages of the proclamation registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2008. The registers are divided into the following inventories: 766 - 816 - The civil proclamations register 817 - 941 - The church proclamations registers, per Reformed church|The digital scans of the marriage banns registers show all the details of the marriage banns records. Individual scans of register pages can be viewed by searching the marriage banns index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page, and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. Note that this scan is for the complete page of the register on which the record in the search result can be seen. The scans can also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The original pages of the marriage banns registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2008. Intended marriages were initially registered in a single marriage banns register. From 28th January 1581 onwards two registers were maintained, a church register for members of reformed churches and a civil register for 'dissenters'. Where one partner was from a reformed church and the other a dissenter, the marriage was registered in both registers. Between 1578 and 1636 a third marriage banns register was maintained, for marriages where one partner did not live in Amsterdam, the 'extraordinarisregister'. From June 1795 up until February 1811 the dual register system ceased to exist and all marriage banns were registered in a single register. The registers are divided into the following inventories: 406-660 - the Church marriage banns register 661-761 - the Civil marriage banns register 762-765 - the 'Extraordinaris' marriage banns register for persons living outside of Amsterdam The scans contain more information than the index. They may contain the following information: - Birthplace - The birthplace of the person. Amsterdam is often shown as A or Amst - Age in years - Only for a first marriage - Marital status - 'j(onge)m(an)' and 'j(onge)d(ochter)' if not previously married, 'wed(uwnaa)r', 'wed(uw)e' for widow(ers) and 'gesch(eiden) man/vrouw' for divorced persons for a second or later marriage. The word 'gedissolveerd' is often used for divorced - Address – in Amsterdam (the street name) or another place where someone lived - Witness - For the first marriage it was required to bring a witness to either give permission for the marriage (one parent or guardian) or to confirm the person’s identity. This would be expected to be the father, or otherwise the mother. If both were dead, ill or absent, then someone else could take their place, but this was not necessary. The term used for witnesses in the register is ‘geass(isteerd) door’ with, where possible, the relationship with the person. - Profession of the bridegroom – until October 1714 - Religion - from February 1755 onwards - Marginal notes – Additional information, such as that the person was required to bring evidence of their previous spouse’s death, or proof of divorce. An important note is an eventual reference to the 'huwelijks-krakeelregister' (see inventory numbers 3053-3077 of the archive '5061 Archieven van de Schout en Schepenen, van de Schepenen en van de Subalterne Rechtbanken' at the City Archives of Amsterdam, https://archief.amsterdam/inventarissen/details/5061/path/4) in which records were kept if there were objections to the marriage. See the links in Section 2.4 for more examples of information in marginal notes. In the oldest marriage banns registers, if persons originally came from outside of Amsterdam then the number of years for which they had lived in Amsterdam is also noted.

column113: Very occasionally a book can be missing. Names may occur more than once in the index (aside of multiple marriages) for many reasons. The first reason is if the signature was different to the name filled in by the official, in that case both names are included in the index. The second reason may be that the betrothed couple did not turn up to confirm their registration, but then registered again later. The third reason can be a mixed marriage between a Reformer and a dissenter, in which case the banns were registered in both the civil and Church register. The marriage banns register is missing for the period between Easter 1565 and August 1578. The index uses the marriage register in its place.|Very occasionally a book can be missing. Mennonites and Jews often married in their own circle. Between 1621 and 1630 both groups were given the chance to register their marriage. Even after this time, Jewish marriages were often not registered. There will certainly be cases of church marriages that were clandestinely conducted before the legal marriage (marriage banns). There are also known cases of dissenters who married in the church but who never arranged marriage banns and who therefore were not legally married. The way that place names and countries are written varies and can be difficult to interpret, sometimes because the details were phonetically noted when they were unfamiliar to the clerk. More people in Amsterdam were registered in the marriage banns register than in the marriage registers. Reasons for this are: - There was an impediment to the marriage - Couples who were marrying elsewhere were also entered into the marriage banns registers in Amsterdam if one or both partners lived in Amsterdam - Couples who decided not to get married after all (they may later have registered in the marriage banns register again and then actually married)|Mennonites and Jews often married in their own circle. Between 1621 and 1630 both groups were given the chance to register their marriage. Even after this time, Jewish marriages were often not registered. There will certainly be cases of church marriages that were clandestinely conducted before the legal marriage (marriage banns). There are also known cases of dissenters who married in the church but who never arranged marriage banns and who therefore were not legally married. The way that place names and countries are written varies and can be difficult to interpret, sometimes because the details were phonetically noted when they were unfamiliar to the clerk. More people in Amsterdam were registered in the marriage banns register than in the marriage registers. Reasons for this are: - There was an impediment to the marriage - Couples who were marrying elsewhere were also entered into the marriage banns registers in Amsterdam if one or both partners lived in Amsterdam - Couples who decided not to get married after all (they may later have registered in the marriage banns register again and then actually married) The marriage banns register is missing for the period between Easter 1565 and August 1578. The details in the marriage banns registers were given by the bride and groom themselves. Ages are therefore not always exactly correct. Birthplaces may also be inaccurate, for example someone may indicate they were born in Amsterdam when they actually moved there as a small child. Birthplaces abroad may not be the exact birthplace, for example Hamburg may be used when the birthplace was a village near Hamburg. Parents were sometimes indicated as being deceased so that parents who lived far away didn't need to travel, or to avoid problems with unwilling parents. The details in the record vary through the centuries, depending on customs but also on the clerk.

column128: Mennonites and Jews often married in their own circle. Between 1621 and 1630 both groups were given the chance to register their marriage. Even after this time, Jewish marriages were often not registered. Dissenters who were required to marry at the town hall also often married in their church. Those marriages are not recorded in these registers. They can sometimes be found in the archives of the church community, for example records of marriages in the Evangelical-Lutheran churches were kept from 1591 onwards, the marriage records from the “Doopsgezinde Gemeente bij het Lam” are available for the period 1621-1668 and those of the Portugese Jews from 1673 onwards. For some church communities, no marriage registers have been preserved. For Roman Catholics, it is not always apparent in which church building they married, and the marriage records have not been preserved for all church buildings.

column18: In the 'Burial registers of Amsterdam 1553-1811' dataset there are details of burials and deaths in Amsterdam, taken from church, graveyard and civil registers. The oldest burial register of Amsterdam is the burial book of the Oude Kerk (Old Church) from 1553. There are few known regulations for burials. The Reformed Church decided during the Provincial Synod in Dordrecht in 1574 to record the names of all deceased church members for whom a grave was dug. In general, registers were kept by gravediggers, who were appointed by the city authority. These registers were mainly financial, to account their costs to the church warden. In addition to these registers, grave books were kept, in which the owners of the graves were recorded. On 26th October 1695 the Dutch States introduced a tax on burials. At first, this was collected by the sextons and gravediggers, who kept registers. After 3rd December 1695 the responsibility for both collection and registration was handed over to the city secretary. Bodies that were found in the city, on the street or in the water, were taken for autopsy and then buried. These so-called 'haaldoden' were recorded in a separate register. Deceased persons who were housed in the city institutions Gasthuis, Pesthuis, Werkhuis and Spinhuis were recorded in separate registers. In 1810 the Netherlands was absorbed by the French Empire and the civil registry was introduced, a register of the population according to the French model. In 1811 the churches were required to hand over their registers to the civil registry. The churches strongly resisted this as they regarded the registers as their private property, but eventually complied on 22nd August 1811. The Portuguese-Jewish community requested to submit a Dutch copy instead and this was permitted. The Ashkenazi community handed over their register, which was in Hebrew. After 20th October 1892, following a decision of the Mayor and Aldermen, all burial registers from before 1811 were moved to the Amsterdam City Archives. This dataset consists of: - a digital index for the burial registers, this can be searched and downloaded - a digital index for the registers of deaths in institutions, this can be searched and downloaded - digital scans of the burial registers, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly - digital scans of the registers of deaths in institutions, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly - digital scans of the 'haaldoden' register of bodies found in the city, these can be browsed but not searched. These are described in further detail in the Resources in Level 3. The dataset does not describe paper indices or other non-digital resources available only in the reading room. For more details about this dataset read the links in section 2.4

column87: The index of the burial registers contains selected key details from the burial records, including the name of the deceased and the date and place of burial. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find burial records. The index is downloadable as a zip archive of xml files. Each file contains a field indexRecords, which contains a number of fields called indexRecord. Each indexRecord represents one burial record. In the 1950s, the City Archives of Amsterdam manually created a paper index to the registers. This index was digitised in ca. 2010. The digitised index records were manually entered to create a digital index. The index covers all the burial registers except for the 'Haaldoden' (those found dead on the street) and those who died in institutions (see the separate Resource 'Index of registers of deaths in Gasthuis, Pesthuis, Werkhuis and Spinhuis institutions 1739-1812'). The list of Data Fields in 3.2 indicates which information is available in the index. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk|The digital scans of the burial registers show all the details of the burial records. Individual scans of register pages can be viewed by searching the burial index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page, and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. Note that this scan is for the complete page of the register on which the record in the search result can be seen. The scans can be also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The original pages of the burial registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2010. A burial record contains at least the name of the deceased and their location. The day of the burial is usually recorded in the lefthand margin. Instead of a number, there can be the abbreviation 'do', which means 'on the same day'. The price that was to be paid was recorded in the righthand margin. However, there are many variations on this standard way of recording the burial. For an explanation of the abbreviations used in the registers, see the Begraafregisters voor 1811 in the Repository links in Section 2.4. Examples of records are explained on the same page.|The index of the registers of deaths in institutions contains selected key details from the death records, including the name of the deceased, their birthplace and the date of death. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find death records. The index is downloadable as a zip archive of xml files. Each file contains a field indexRecords, which contains a number of fields called indexRecord. Each indexRecord represents one death record. The Gasthuis and Pesthuis institutions cared for the sick, poor and homeless. The Werkhuis and Spinhuis were workhouse and correctional institutions. Those who died in the Gasthuis, Pesthuis, Werkhuis and Spinhuis institutions had their deaths recorded in the registers of those institutions. If their family collected their bodies for burial then they may also appear in the burial registers. In 2013, a digital index of these registers was created by volunteers at the City Archives of Amsterdam. The list of Data Fields in 3.2 indicates which information is available in the index. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk|The digital scans of the registers of deaths in institutions show all the details of the death records. Individual scans of register pages can be viewed by searching the death index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page, and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. Note that this scan is for the complete page of the register on which the record in the search result can be seen. The scans can also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The original pages of the death registers were digitally scanned at some point prior to 2010. The Gasthuis and Pesthuis institutions cared for the sick, poor and homeless. The Werkhuis and Spinhuis were workhouse and correctional institutions. Those who died in the Gasthuis, Pesthuis, Werkhuis and Spinhuis institutions had their deaths recorded in the registers of those institutions. If their family collected their bodies for burial then they may also appear in the burial registers. The scans may contain more information than the index records. This may include: - name - place - date of death - cause of death (only for deaths in Spinhuis and Werkhuis) - marital status (only for deaths in the Gasthuis and Pesthuis; not always noted) - very rarely: profession or military rank - very rarely: extra annotations such as 'criminelen' (criminal), 'door de politie gezonden' (sent by the police) - very rarely: the graveyard where someone was buried - references to registers with more details about the residents (only Spinhuis and Werkhuis; these registers have mostly been lost)|The digital scans of the 'haaldoden' registers show all the details of the death records. The scans can be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website for births, marriages and deaths, see the link in Path. The register has not been indexed, so it cannot be searched. Bodies that were found in the city, on the street or in the water, were taken for autopsy and then buried. These so-called 'haaldoden' were recorded in a separate register. The original pages of the death registers were digitally scanned in ca. 2008. The following details may be in the register: - name of the person - place where the body was found - date - possible cause of death Some bodies could not be identified, but had initials embroidered into their clothing. This part of the cloth was sometimes cut out and pinned to the register page.

column113: Only Amsterdammers buried in Amsterdam are included. Relatives of Amsterdammers who were buried outside of Amsterdam had to pay a fine. These fines were recorded in the register of fines related to marriages and burials. Persons whose burial can't be found in the normal registers may therefore possibly be found in the register of fines. A number of burial registers from various churches and graveyards are missing. Some books were not kept or handed over, for unknown reasons. There are also people for whom the burial record is missing. An example is the period in which Amsterdam was in the grip of the plague. Thousands of citizens died each month and many were buried at the same time, making it impossible to register everyone. Important plague years were 1656, 1663 and 1664. The registers of deaths in the town institutions Gasthuis, Pesthuis, Spinhuis and Werkhuis, and the ‘haaldoden’ (bodies found in the city) are not included in this index. There is a separate index for deaths in the institutions. There is no index for the ‘haaldoden’. Until 1810, the Engelse Kerk had no church wardens of their own, but fell under the church authority of the Nieuwe Kerk. For this reason, burials in the Engelse Kerk were recorded in the register of the Nieuwe Kerk, with the annotation ‘E.K.’. Citizens of Amsterdam who were buried outside the city are not recorded in this index. Many poor children’s burials were recorded in the Children’s registers of the respective graveyards. However, these registers are not complete. In the years for which there was no children’s register, the children were registered anonymously. Names can appear twice in this index. This is for one of two reasons: - The deceased had a widow or widower. The death is then indexed using both the name of the person and the name of the widow/widower, so the name of the person will be indexed both for their own death and for the death of their partner. - A burial in the Westerkerkhof was registered both in the registers of the Westerkerkhof and the Westerkerk. The gravedigger often wrote the name of the deceased as it had been passed on to him. There was no check on completeness or spelling. As a result, people’s names can be written in very different ways.|A number of burial registers from various churches and graveyards are missing. Some books were not kept or handed over, for unknown reasons. There are also people for whom the burial record is missing. An example is the period in which Amsterdam was in the grip of the plague. Thousands of citizens died each month and many were buried at the same time, making it impossible to register everyone. Important plague years were 1656, 1663 and 1664. Until 1810, the Engelse Kerk had no church wardens of their own, but fell under the church authority of the Nieuwe Kerk. For this reason, burials in the Engelse Kerk were recorded in the register of the Nieuwe Kerk, with the annotation ‘E.K.’. Citizens of Amsterdam who were buried outside the city are not recorded in the registers. Many poor children’s burials were recorded in the Children’s registers of the respective graveyards. However, these registers are not complete. In the years for which there was no children’s register, the children were registered anonymously. The gravedigger often wrote the name of the deceased as it had been passed on to him. There was no check on completeness or spelling. As a result, people’s names can be written in very different ways.|Many nameless infants and unborn children died in the various institutions. In that case the name of the mother is recorded in the index with the annotation 'kind van' (child of) or 'kraamkind van' (infant of).

column18: This is the Amateur film collection maintained by the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision (NISV). It contains films recorded from the 1910s until now. It consists of family films, travelogues, fiction films and animation. These were intended for screenings at home or at the filmclubs in the Netherlands. Nowadays these films are loved by historians and filmmakers. The films show a history of domestic life in the Netherlands and its former colonies. These are often images that were never captured with a professional camera and now prove so valuable to our history. The collection also includes films made by film hobbyists. These are films that have been recorded with a script and often provided with titles or music. These filmmakers were usually affiliated with one of the many film clubs in the country. The collection was created by the Stichting Smalfilmmuseum, which was set up specifically to preserve amateur films. The founder of the museum, Henk Verheul, actively searched the market for amateur films and also put out calls for material in film magazines, regional and national television and his netwerk of local film clubs. In 2006 the collection was handed over to the Netherlands Institute of Sound & Vision. Since then, the collection has mainly been added to by films donated by individuals. The collection contains a few thousand amateur films and also a number of videos. The films are available on various portals belonging to Sound & Vision, see Section 2.4 for a list. Some of these are Open Access, others are Restricted. The entire collection of films and metadata is available under Restricted Access. The metadata and some films are available under Open Access. Some portals make it possible to specifically search the Amateur film collection, others do not. Note that the genre 'amateurfilm' in the Sound & Vision collection covers a broader range than the Amateur film collection. The dataset consists of: - the metadata describing the films. Part of this is available in the Open Access portals; all of the metadata is available in the Restricted Access portals - a subset of the metadata describing the films that has been transformed to Linked Data. This is available via the Linked Data endpoint - the films themselves. Many films are available in the Open Access portals, all are available in the Restricted Access portals. See the links in Section 2.4 for more information.

column201: In the early years of amateur film the equipment was expensive, so only privileged people could create such films. From the 1970s onwards equipment became cheaper and it became possible for 'ordinary' people to film. Most amateur film makers were men, only a handful were women. Prior to 2006, films were selected and metadata was created by Henk Verheul at the Stichting Smalfilmmuseum, a professional TV archivist. Since 2006, selection and metadata are done by employees of the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision with expertise in amateur film.

column2: Sound & Vision Linked Open Data Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Sound & Vision Linked Open Data Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The Sound & Vision Linked Open Data is a selection of the structured metadata for the audiovisual collection maintained by the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision (NISV), NISV is one of the most important Dutch audiovisual archives. It collects, looks after and provides access to over 70% of the Dutch audio-visual heritage. For more information about the NISV collection, see the data-envelope for 'Sound & Vision collection' ***link***. The Linked Open Data includes all items in the Sound & Vision collection for which the metadata is permitted to be made publicly available. This is the vast majority of the collection. For these items the metadata fields that are not protected by copyright, or to which Sound & Vision itself is the rights holder, are selected for publication as Linked Open Data. The Linked Open Data contains factual information about the archive items, such as title, broadcast date, persons, locations and subjects. Textual descriptions of the items are not available, as these are under copyright, with the exception of certain collections for which the rights have been cleared, such as the Amateur film collection. The audiovisual content itself is not available, but for some items there are links to portals where the content can be viewed. Licensing information for the audiovisual content is included in the metadata. The Linked Open Data is available online for browsing and querying. See Section 3 for more details.

column86: Sound & Vision Linked Open Data Online
column87: To create the Linked Open Data, all items in the Sound & Vision collection that are publicly available are selected. The metadata of these is mapped to Linked Open Data using a mapping and software created by Sound & Vision. The metadata fields that are allowed to be published are selected and mapped to schema.org https://schema.org/. During this mapping the metadata is simplified. For example, both crew and creator fields in the original metadata are mapped to the property sdo:creator. Similarly, subjects, geographical names, organisations and persons discussed are all mapped to the property sdo:about. The detailed information on legal, ethical and privacy considerations contained in the original metadata is mapped to rights and licensing information from rightsstatement.org and creativecommons.org. Note: this license applies to the audiovisual content itself. The metadata is made available under a Creative Commons CC0 licence. The hierarchical structure of series, seasons, programmes and scenes is preserved in the Linked Open Data. For more information on this hierarchy, see the data-envelope for ‘Sound & Vision collection’. For more information on the LOD model, browse the schema at https://beeldengeluid.github.io/beng-lod-ontospy/. The mapped metadata is enriched with alignments to external sources. For example, many persons have been linked to Wikidata, many musical artists have been linked to the Discogs and Muziekweb databases and recording locations for concerts have been linked to Wikidata. Some alignments have been created manually, some automatically with crowdsourced checks. The Linked Open Data provides links from the items to the Sound & Vision General Public Portal, where materials can be ordered and may also be viewed, depending on copyright restrictions. If an item is also available on the Sound & Vision Open Images platform (approximately 7000 items), then this link is included in the metadata. Using this link media can be downloaded in various formats. (Note: The Open Images platform will be discontinued in the future). Each item has a coolURI (https://www.w3.org/TR/cooluris/), of the form http://data.beeldengeluid.nl/id/{cat_type}/{item_id }. ‘Cat_type’ is one of the following values: 'series', 'season', 'program', 'scene'. ‘Item_id’ is a unique number given to the archive item. Opening this URI in a browser shows a selection of the Linked Data fields for the item. The dataset is queryable via a SPARQL endpoint (see Path). A number of sample queries are offered to help users familiarise themselves with the model and the data. They can modify these queries for their own use or build their own queries. The Linked Open Data schema can also be queried via the SPARQL endpoint. (Note that the endpoint also gives access to the GTAA thesaurus. See the data-envelope on the GTAA thesaurus for more details. )

column16: Biographical lexicon for the history of Dutch protestantism
column18: This dataset describes the digitised 'Biographical lexicon for the history of Dutch protestantism'. The lexicon contains 2200 biographies of persons who played a role in the history of Dutch Protestantism. Earlier parts only contain people who were already deceased in 1968, the last book extends the scope to people who were already deceased in 2000. The reformation period of Belgian Protestantism, the history of Dutch provinces abroad and the former Dutch colonies are included in the history of Dutch Protestantism. The entries in each book of the lexicon are in alphabetical order of surname. Double-barrelled names are ranked on the first name. Persons with a sixteenth/seventeenth century patronym are ranked on their first name. The letter 'ij' is listed as 'y'. The lexicon is split into six books. The first book also contains an explanation of the construction of the lexicon. The final book also contains a register of all the persons in the lexicon, and a register of who wrote each of the entries in the lexicon. Each book contains corrections for previously published books in the lexicon. The first book of the lexicon was published in 1978, the final book in 2006. See individual data-envelopes for each book for more details. The lexicon is accessible in the digital edition online (see the link in Section 2.4).The online edition makes the lexicon searchable and browsable. It can be consulted in the following ways: - by using the browse function at the top of the screen or the thumbnails in the 'Thumbnails' tab - by searching or browsing the names of persons in the Index tab - by searching the text (recognised by OCR) in the 'Zoek' tab. Note that not all the text is searchable, and that the OCR contains errors. The pages can be viewed as image, pdf or OCR (the recognised text). The list of persons was created manually by reading the lexicon. The lexicon can also be downloaded (see the Resource 'Downloadable copy of the biographical lexicon for the history of Dutch protestantism' in Section 3 for more details). To access individual books within the lexicon, see the data-envelopes for the books.

column2: Correspondence of Christofforo Suriano 1616-1623 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Correspondence of Christofforo Suriano 1616-1623 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The Correspondence of Christofforo Suriano dataset makes digitally available the content of all 725 letters sent by Suriano to the Venetian Senate. Christofforo Suriano was the first Venetian envoy to the Dutch Republic. His arrival in The Hague marks the beginning of official Dutch-Venetian diplomatic relations. The dataset contains information regarding all 725 letters sent by Suriano to the Venetian Senate between 1616 and 1623, totalling approximately 7,000 pages. His correspondence is of great importance for Dutch, Venetian, and European history in the first phase of the Thirty Years’ War. Suriano sometimes sent enclosed documents. These represent a wide range of different documents: copies of letters, speeches, pamphlets, original letters by other correspondents, notarial acts, accounts, etc. They are included in the letters. Frequently the letters were summarised when they were received in Venice, by trained secretaries. These summaries were written perpendicularly on the verso of the last folio of each letter. Parts of the letters are in code. These were decoded at the time they were received in Venice, by trained secretaries. The decoded text is included in the letter. The dataset consists of the following parts: - Transcripts of the letters (including decoded cipher but NOT including the attachments) in Italian - English summaries of the letters - Metadata describing the letters - Persons mentioned in the letters - Source code for the digital online edition. The letters (and their attachments and English summaries) can be searched and browsed via the online edition (see link in Section 2.4). The online edition makes it possible to view scans of the letters (in Italian) and their attachments (in Latin, Dutch, French, Spanish and Italian), read their English summaries and metadata, search for words or persons in letters, and view biographies of the persons mentioned in the letters. The scans of the letters and attachments are under copyright and cannot be shared. They are used in the online edition by kind permission of the Archivio di Stato di Venezia in Italy. The other parts of the online edition are included in this dataset and can be individually downloaded. See the Resources in Section 3 for more details. For more details about the correspondence, see the links in Section 2.4.

column87: Transcripts of the letters (including the attachments, decoded text and secretarial summaries) were manually created, following agreed criteria (These are described on the project page, see the link in Section 2.4). A team of native Italian paleologists created the transcripts, with the exception of attachments in Dutch, which were transcribed by the native Dutch dataset creator and research assistants. Transcripts were always checked by a second member of the team. The dataset creator also checked them and corrected oddities, in particular for Dutch person and place names. The initial check of each transcript was done against the original document itself, in situ at the state archive in Venice. Subsequent stages of proof checking were done based on the images. The project team chose not to indicate the change in scribal hands in the enclosed documents, decoded texts and the secretarial summaries. Federica D’Uonno decoded the remaining words and texts which had not been decrypted by a Venetian secretary, using a tool she created in Excel. The presence of seals or traces of seals, still visible on the last folio of the letters, are noted in the transcripts. The letters were originally bound in folders ('filze'). For the transcripts, a single file has been created for each folder, contained the transcripts of all letters in that folder.|Summaries in English were manually created by the dataset creator after reading the letters. The aim of these summaries is to provide an overview of the most important topics and themes covered by Suriano. The summaries also contain information about the various enclosed documents sent with the letters. The Excel file summaries.xlsx contains the English summaries of the letters. Note: It also contains columns with the metadata of the letters, see the resource 'Metadata of correspondence of Christofforo Suriano 1616-1623' for more details.|The dataset creator manually created metadata for each letter after reading the letter. Among other information, the Excel file summaries.xlsx contains a summary of the letter, the senders and recipients of the letters, their locations and the attachments in the letter. All columns in the spreadsheet are described in Section 3.2. For more details, see the links in Section 2.4.|The source code that was used to prepare the data for the website of the correspondence of Christofforo Suriano is publicly available on Github (see Path). This gives transparency and allows researchers to programmatically work with the corpus. Note that as the scans of the letters are not available (due to copyright) it is not possible to fully recreate the website without permission from the Archivio di Stato di Venezia. The Github repository documentation describes the steps taken to process the data in detail, including reports of errors and gaps. This includes the process by which the list of persons (see resource 'Persons mentioned in correspondence of Christofforo Suriano 1616-1623') was used to annotate each individual letter with the names of the persons mentioned in it. For more details, see the links in Section 2.4.|As much as possible, the individuals mentioned in the transcripts of the main body of the letters sent by Christofforo Suriano were manually identified, by the dataset creator and contributors. The name variants and biographical information of individuals were collected in a spreadsheet. Strings were added to find other name variants. This spreadsheet, persons.xlsx, provided the basis for automatically annotating the names in the digital edition, using TextFabric. There may still be false positives due to the variation in spellings and there may also be names which have not yet been found. All columns in the spreadsheet are described in Section 3.2. column16: : Enhancing moral residue through literature – dataset
column18: This dataset contains the responses of healthcare professionals to close-ended questions (scales) in a study designed to measure the effect of literary reading on their perceived susceptibility to moral residue (PSMR). Moral residue (MR) is the experience when individuals hold themselves morally responsible for perceived failures, even when others do not. This study explored the ways and the extent to which narratives can affect healthcare professionals’ PSMR. 417 healthcare professionals participated in the study. To explore the effect of fiction/non-fiction, one group of participants was asked to read a story and was told that the story was fiction, while a second group was asked to read the same story but was told that it was nonfiction. The dataset contains measures of three narrative engagement mechanisms: transportation, self-referencing, and identification. It also contains a novel scale for measuringPSMR , as well as other scales that were used as control variables (perceived similarity, perceived realism, prior experience with MR, age, profession, specialty, gender, tendency to rationalize).

column86: Participant responses to the MORE Study on enhancing awareness of moral residue through literature
column87: The dataset contains the responses of 417 participants to the questions in the MORE Study on enhancing awareness of moral residue through literature. The questions were designed to assess their narrative engagement (in terms of the three narrative engagement mechanisms: transportation, identification and self-referencing), to assess their perceived susceptibility to moral residue, and to assess their perceived realism, tendency to rationalize and perceived similarity to the story's protagonist. In addition, attention check questions were posed to exclude participants that e.g. did not pay enough attention, and participants were also asked some demographic questions. The study’s participants were healthcare professionals recruited via Prolific, a crowdsourcing platform. The study was conducted online using the survey software Qualtrics. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups. They were told that the study related to their emotional responses in the workplace, without mentioning a specific interest in MR. After providing informed consent, one group was told that they were to read a fiction excerpt, and the second group was told that they would read a non-fiction excerpt. The third group did not read a text. All participants in the first two groups read a short excerpt from the novel The Children Act by Ian McEwan. To assess participants' attentiveness while reading the story, we formulated three basic comprehension multiple-choice questions, each with three response options. Participants who answered more than one question incorrectly were excluded from the analysis. Additionally, we assessed participants' familiarity with the story by asking whether they had previously read it, with response options including “yes”, “no”, and “maybe/not sure”. Participants who answered “yes” or “maybe/not sure” were excluded from the analysis. To evaluate the success of the manipulation, we devised a question to ensure that participants remembered whether what they read was fiction or nonfiction. Participants who provided incorrect answers were excluded from the analysis. Participants who considered the protagonist (Fiona) blameworthy were also excluded from the study. Next, participants answered questions regarding their reading experience with the Transportation scale, the Identification scale, the Self-Referencing scale, the Perceived Similarity scale, and the Perceived Realism scale, in a random order. Next, they completed the PSMR scale. The emotional responses of this scale appeared in a random order. Participants then completed the Prior Experience with MR scale and the Rationalisation scale. Following these scales, the participants of the two experimental groups were asked to respond to this open question: “Can you imagine yourself experiencing emotions relevant to what the story's protagonist experienced? Please elaborate.” In addition, participants provided information regarding their profession, medical specialization, age, and gender. Participants in the control group only completed the PSMR scale, the Prior Experience with MR scale, and the demographic questions. Finally, participants were debriefed and asked whether they wanted to add any comments about the study. Excluded participants are not included in this dataset. The download link downloads a .zip file. Once the file has been unpacked, the data is available in data/ data.csv

column128: The participants were selected from medical professions. They had to be users of the Prolific platform. The amount of compensation paid to participants is considered enough to motivate but not to make it a profit, so they should be genuinely interested in the study. However, it could be the case that the salary or economic situation in the country of a participant influences the relative value of the compensation to them, making them more likely to participate. There were more female than male participants and more nurses than doctors. The decision to participate in the study may produce a bias towards participants who are more interested in moral residue.

column201: Jan Helge Solbakk is a medical doctor and theologian, and he holds a PhD in Ancient Greek Philosophy. Since 1996, he has been Professor of medical ethics at Center for Ethics, University of Oslo. Between 1996 and 2011, he was Adjunct Professor of medical ethics at the Center for International Health, University of Bergen. In 2007 and 2008, he was Head of the UNESCO Bioethics Programme, UNESCO Headquarters, Paris. His current research focus is on irresolvable moral conflicts and the nature of moral learning. Solbakk has coordinated and been involved in various international research and capacity building projects relating to bioethics teaching, research ethics and integrity in research, research biobanking, personalised medicine, nanomedicine, stem cell research and the ethics of organoid research. Cullin Brown is a PhD candidate in Philosophy at Binghamton University. His dissertation is on fitting affective attitudes and how they structure our personal lives and interpersonal practices (Consider: How should we think about the "Oh that is so terrible!" laugh we often have in response to morally problematic jokes? What values are at stake?). Cullin has taught and TAed courses in normative ethics, applied ethics, social and political philosophy, and logic. Frank Hakemulder is Associate Professor at Liberal Arts and Sciences, Utrecht University, and full Professor at the Reading Center (University of Stavanger) with a background in literary theory. He is specialized in the psychology of literature, with a focus on the way reading may affect attitudes toward outgroup and moral self-concept. He has supervised various research projects on the experience of being absorbed in fictional worlds, and on how such experiences affect social perception and self-concepts. At the Reading Center he and his colleagues concentrate on the nature of deep reading and its relation to deep learning and readers’ mental well-being. Nikoletta Alexandri is a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Huygens Institute (KNAW, Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences) with a background in linguistics and media psychology. She completed her PhD at Lancaster University (UK), where she specialised in the psychological effects of literary reading and the use of experimental designs. Her thesis focused on the effects of fiction and non-fiction reading and how different reading media (e-readers, tablets, audiobooks, and print books) affect readers’ empathy, absorption, and narrative comprehension. A total of 745 healthcare professionals from various parts of the world participated in the study, all of whom were over the age of 18 and fluent in English. They were paid the standard Prolific compensation of 9 British pounds per hour. Of these, 328 participants were excluded from the study according to the checks described in Section 3. The participants' ages ranged from 19 to 77, with a mean of 35.91 and a standard deviation of 12.01. Of the participants, 284 were female and 131 were male, while two participants preferred not to disclose their gender. The sample consisted of 274 nurses and 143 doctors. Participant can be from almost anywhere in the world, their country is unknown to the researchers as this is blocked by the crowdsourcing platform.

column18: The collection of broadcasts from Radio Oranje (Radio Orange) contains a selection of the radio broadcasts that were transmitted between 1940 and 1945. They were broadcasts created by the Dutch Government in exile in London, transmitted to encourage and inform the people of the Netherlands whilst under occupation. Each day at 20:15 a 15 minute long programme was broadcast via the European Service of the BBC in London. This contained news reports, anti-NSB cabaret and anti-German songs. The radio broadcaster had a strong symbolic meaning. Radio Oranje was set up on 28th July 1940 and continued until June 1945. In October 1942 it merged with Radio De Brandaris. Few broadcasts were preserved, as a weekly overview of the broadcasts shows https://wiki.beeldengeluid.nl/index.php/Radio_Oranje:_1943. Documents related to wartime radio are available at the NIOD Institute for War, Holocaust and Genocide Studies

column86: Radio Oranje audio recordings|Radio Oranje metadata
column87: The dataset contains approximately 180 digital recordings of Radio Oranje broadcasts. Many recordings were made by amateurs on various types of equipment. The sound quality is variable. German radio jamming interfered with reception, also leading to poor quality in some cases. Most recordings are of only a fragment of a broadcast. The collection can be searched and listened to via the Clariah Media Suite https://mediasuite.clariah.nl (no login required for this collection). The collection is available to listen to and download on Wikimedia Commons https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Radio_Broadcasts_by_Radio_Oranje, and via the Wikimedia Commons API. column6: Radio Herrijzend Nederland Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The collection of broadcasts from Radio Herrijzend Nederland (Radio ‘The Netherlands Revived’) contains a selection of the radio broadcasts that were transmitted between 1944 and 1946. They were broadcast by the Dutch military authorities from Eindhoven after its liberation in September 1944, to encourage and inform the people of the still occupied north of the Netherlands. There were multiple daily broadcasts. In 1945 additional masts were placed so that the broadcasts could be received in the whole of the Netherlands, and worldwide broadcasts were also made. After the liberation it continued to broadcast until January 1946. The radio station broadcast current affairs, reflections on news, and music. The content of all broadcasts was checked by the Dutch military authorities. Documents related to wartime radio are available at the NIOD Institute for War, Holocaust and Genocide Studies

column2: Radio De Brandaris Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Radio De Brandaris Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The collection Radio De Brandaris contains a selection of radio broadcasts that were transmitted between 1941 and 1942 by Radio de Brandaris. Radio De Brandaris started transmitting on July 10th, 1941, from London, commissioned by the Dutch government in exile. The broadcasts were meant for Dutch allied seafarers and other ships from Holland. They could also be received in The Netherlands and, until the Japanese occupation in March 1942, in Dutch East India and Curacao. The station broadcast for 15 minutes each day, in the morning. The broadcasts contained news. A ship's radio operator was part of the team to ensure that maritime language was used. The text was not checked by the censors, which made Radio De Brandaris initially more popular than Radio Oranje, which was subject to censorship. In the beginning of November 1942 Radio de Brandaris became part of Radio Oranje.

column2: Residential registration cards Amsterdam 1924-1989 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Residential registration cards Amsterdam 1924-1989 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Woningkaarten Amsterdam 1924-1989 Versie-1 Data-Envelope Versie-1
column18: In the 'Residential registration cards Amsterdam 1924-1989' dataset is a record of who lived in each building in Amsterdam, between 1922 and 1989. Every time someone moved in or out of the building, the index card for that building was updated by striking out the name of the person who had moved out, or adding the name of the person who had moved in. The index cards were part of the population administration of the Amsterdam City Council. Residential registration cards were introduced to replace book-style registers, which encountered problems when pages become full, particularly as Amsterdam annexed several surrounding areas in 1921. The index cards were used until the GBS automatic system for registration was introduced in 1989. The cards contain the name, birth year and gender of the main resident, and number and genders of other residents. Where residents moved out, the moving date and their new address is listed. In addition to private residences, there are cards for businesses, nursing homes, barracks, boarding houses, prisons, houseboats and caravans. Special cards per street contain information about origin of the street name and any changes to the name. This dataset consists of: - digital scans of the residential registration cards, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly - a digital index for the residential registration cards, this can be used to search for cards from a particular address. These resources are described in further detail in the Resources in Level 3. For more details about this dataset read the links in section 2.4

column86: Scans of Residential registration cards Amsterdam 1924-1989|Index of residential registration cards Amsterdam 1924-1989
column87: The digital scans of the residential registration cards show all the details of the residence information. Individual scans of residential registration cards can be viewed by searching the residential registration card index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. The scans can also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website pages for residential registration cards, see the links in Path. One building may have multiple cards, as new cards were added when old ones became full. There are two series of cards, from 1922 to 1953 and from 1953 to 1989. The layout differs between the series. When the second series was started, the most recent information was copied from the cards from the first series. On the front side of the card is the address and the family information of the main residents. The building address contains the following elements: - the house number (with any suffixes) - the neighbourhood letters (Amsterdam was divided into 50 neighbourhoods, from A to ZZ) - the name of the street. On the bottom left is a number indicating the position of this card in the sequence of cards for this building, on the bottom right is the number of the voting district. On the back is information about other residents, such as students or servants. The residents’ information contains the following elements: - Date that they moved in - Surname, first name and/or initials of the main resident - Birth year of the main resident - Gender of the main resident - Number of men and women in the family of the main resident - Date and address if moved (if moving to a different municipality then only the name of the municipality was registered). Sometimes numbers are included to indicate which main resident the family members belonged to, but this is not consistently done. At the bottom of the card is space for notes. In addition to private residences, there are index cards for businesses and for buildings without a fixed address (ships, caravans and emergency housing). These have a different format. Where there is no fixed address, there is a description of the location, for ships the ship or captain’s name is often registered. There is also an index card per street, describing: - The origin of the street name - Any earlier or later names of the street - Sometimes a reference to the decision to change the street name - Missing house numbers. Some cards have a cut-off corner, indicating that they were no longer current, for example if the building had been demolished.|The index of the residential registration cards contains the street names and numbers from the residential registration cards. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find residential registration cards. It is only possible to search on address, not on residents' names. This means that it is not possible to search for the cards without an address (such as houseboats). To find the street index cards, search by typing in [adrestype: straat] followed by the name of the street. The index can be downloaded using the OAI-PMH mechanism, see the second link in Path. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk
column94: Sensitive data about people|Data about places and object
column113: At the start of the second series of cards in 1953, the most recent information was copied from the cards in the first series. So the first registration on a card in the second series could be residents that had already been living in the house prior to 1953. Streetnames are as they were at the time. There are some spelling variations, and some streets have been renamed. When scanning the cards, the procedure for scanning person index cards was initially used. It is unknown whether this had an effect on the quality, but it is noticeable that there are many folded corners in the scans.

column127: Cards that could reveal implied special personal information (such as residence in a mental hospital) were excluded, as were cards of persons who had requested that their addresses be kept secret. Cards are removed on request by residents. The personal details are only visible on the scan of the card and are not searchable.
column128: A number of cards are not publicly available as they contain details of residents of locations whereby information about the health, criminal history or religion of the resident could be implied (for example psychiatric hospitals or prisons). Cards for sensitive locations such as women’s refuges are also blocked. Cards are also not available if residents have requested that the address information be kept private. An overview of the blocked cards is available here: https://archief.amsterdam/images/uitleg-indexen/woningkaarten/GeblokkeerdeWoningkaarten.pdf. If you have a reason to view the blocked cards then you can request an exception.|It is not presently possible to search for residential registration cards without an address (such as houseboats). The index does not include blocked addresses such as prisons, refuges, religious residences and care homes.
column131: Cards from which information about health data, criminal history or religious beliefs could be inferred have been blocked. All cards give information about name, location, gender and age. This can be read in the scan but is not searchable.

Image optimisation was applied to the scanned images, using a light and dark profile, determined by the pixel values of the scan. For details, see the 'Overbrenging Amsterdamse woningkaarten 1954-1989' report linked in Section 2.4|The index cards were digitally scanned (front and back) and the information from the records was manually entered into a digital index

column2: Common Thesaurus for Audiovisual Archives (GTAA) Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Common Thesaurus for Audiovisual Archives (GTAA) Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Gemeenschappelijke Thesaurus voor Audiovisuele Archieven (GTAA) Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The Common Thesaurus for Audiovisual Archives (GTAA) contains lists of thesaurus terms that can be used to describe audiovisual content in a common, shared way. This makes it easier to search, access and link audiovisual content. A thesaurus is a controlled word list with semantic relations and references between the termes, which makes searching a catalogue of audiovisual content easier and more focussed. The relations between the terms are hierarchical or associative. They answer questions such as: which terms are used to describe a subject in more general – or more specific – terms; which terms can I use to search that are related to this subject? The thesurus assists with finding synonyms, homonyms and spelling variations. The thesaurus is split into a number of concept schemes, categories that describe different types of terms that are relevant for audiovisual material: names, person names, genres, subjects and locations. The terms are intended for archival use and so the language used is quite formal. The thesaurus was created by a group of national Dutch audiovisual organisations. It is maintained by the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision, which adds new terms as necessary. Employees of the Dutch national broadcaster, NPO, can suggest new terms for addition. The thesaurus can be browsed or downloaded via an API or a linked data endpoint, or used via the Termennetwerk, see the links in Section 2.4. Exports can also be requested. See the Resources in Section 3 for more details about the thesaurus and how to access it.
column28: Music, theatre, performing arts and media|Film, photography and audio-visual media
column34: New terms are being continuously added. Note that the GTAA was based on an existing thesaurus so some terms come from earlier than 1994.

column80: Sound & Vision maintains the GTAA and is responsible for regularly adapting it to current affairs and making it available to external parties. The thesaurus is updated daily. New phenomena are added, for example, ‘appen’ (apping) is now a term under ‘mobiele berichten’ (mobile messaging) together with ‘SMSen’ (smsing). Larger changes, such as tackling offensive language, are conducted in projects such as ‘Words Matter’. Care must be taken that material annotated with old terms can still be found.
column86: Common Thesaurus for Audiovisual Archives (GTAA)|Names (Namen) concept scheme|Subjects (Onderwerpen) concept scheme|Subjects (Onderwerpen) B-G concept scheme|Geographical Names (Geografische namen) concept scheme|Genres concept scheme|Classification (Classificatie) concept scheme|Person names (Persoonsnamen) concept scheme
column87: The terms in the thesaurus are modelled using the widely used SKOS model, one of the standards for Dutch government, and the best practice advised for terminology sources in the Netwerk Digitaal Erfgoed (Dutch Network for Digital Heritage). The data fields are described in Section 3.2. Each term in the GTAA is a SKOS Concept with as minimum a preferred label and a HTTP URI. The URIs start with http://data.beeldengeluid.nl, followed by /gtaa/{number}, where ‘number’ is the numerical identifier for the term. An example is: http://data.beeldengeluid.nl/gtaa/123456. The GTAA has multiple parts containing terms that are grouped under the same category. Each category is a SKOS ConceptScheme. Beginning in 1995, the GTAA was developed in partnership between the creators, using the RVD-archief thesaurus as a starting point. A merger between the partners (with the exception of the Filmmuseum) on 1st January 1997 created the NAA (National Audiovisual Archive, later the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision), which continued the development of the thesaurus in cooperation with the Filmmuseum. It was agreed that Sound & Vision would maintain the GTAA. Agreements were made about how the various concept schemes in the thesaurus would be filled in. The explicit desire was that the GTAA would be usable for other audiovisual archives and collections in the Netherlands. Regular workshops were held to train employees in the use of the thesaurus. The GTAA complies with the ISO standard 2788. Expected reuse and search behaviour were the most important factors guiding the choice of thesaurus terms. For consistency and overview, extra terms are sometimes added to the hierarchical structure, e.g. ‘balsporten’ (ball sports). Due to the wide range of possible subjects in audiovisual material, terms may be placed under more than one broader term, allowing for different contexts. Terms can be suggested as candidates for the thesaurus. In particular a link to the system used by the Dutch public service broadcasters allows them to suggest new person names. These are subject to manual approval at Sound & Vision. If approved, they are added to the thesaurus. To aid maintenance, a transition was made to using SKOS-XL labels, which allow labels themselves to have an identifier that can be referred to when suggesting and tracking changes. An individual thesaurus term can be easily viewed simply by entering its full identifier in a browser, e.g. https://data.beeldengeluid.nl/gtaa/123456. It can be viewed in different formats such as RDF/XML, JSON-LD or Turtle. The entire thesaurus, or individual concept schemes, can be downloaded via the OAI-PMH or SPARQL endpoints, or a download can be requested. The thesaurus can be queried and browsed via the API and the SPARQL endpoint. An autocomplete service is also available. See Section 2.4 for the links to the APIs and endpoints. The concept schemes of the GTAA are terminology sources in the Termennetwerk (Term network) run by the Dutch Network for Digital Heritage. This allows the schemes to be searched and to be used to reconcile (match) data to the GTAA terms. For example, to match persons in another dataset to the list of person names in the GTAA. See the other Resources for specific information about the Concept Schemes within the thesaurus.|The Names (Namen) concept scheme contains proper nouns such as the names of organisations, music ensembles, wars, film titles etc. It also contains terms such as the names of Dutch festival days, e.g. ‘Sinterklaas’ (St. Nicholas) and ‘Kerst’ (Christmas. The Names concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The terms in the Subjects concept scheme are used to indicate the subjects of the audiovisual material (e.g. 'zwemmen' (swimming), 'daklozen' (homeless people), 'audiovisuele archieven' (audiovisual archives)). The Subjects concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The Subjects B-G concept scheme is a variation on the Subjects concept scheme that is used for describing audiovisual material at the level of shots and sounds. It is intended to help find material for reuse, such as stock footage. Examples are 'dorpsbeelden' (village images), 'gapen' (yawning), 'medium long shot', 'stranden' (beaches) etc. There is a large overlap with the Subjects concept scheme. Subjects B-G is often used for image material, such as photos in the Dutch National Archive or the EYE Filmmuseum. The Subjects B-G concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The Geographical Names concept scheme contains the names of towns, villages, provinces, motorways, countries etc. These are not organised into a hierarchy and there are no links between terms. The Geographical Names concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The terms in the Genres concept scheme give an indication of the type of production as a whole. They describe the form, genre or type of the programme (e.g. ‘korte film’ (short film), ‘lifestyleprogramma’ (lifestyle programme), ‘hoorspel’ (radio play), ‘politieserie ‘(police series) etc. Genres are important for forming collections. The Genre concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The Classification concept scheme organises terms from the Subjects concept scheme into sections and subsections. Each (sub)section is a SKOS Concept with labels, notes and related concepts. The Classification concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus.|The Person Names concept scheme contains the names of people who appear in or are referred to in audiovisual material. These are typically public figures, predominantly Dutch but also from all around the world. The Person Names scheme also includes contributors to the creation of a programme, such as programme makers, cast and crew. A number of persons from this scheme have been linked to persons on Wikidata. A number of persons related to music have been linked to persons in the Discogs music database or the Stichting Omroep Muziek dataset. The Person names concept scheme uses the same data fields as the main thesaurus. The skos:exactMatch property is used for the links between persons in the GTAA thesaurus and persons in Wikidata, or musical artists in the Discogs and Stichting Omroep Muziek databases.

column157: This thesaurus was created as a shared way of making the content of audiovisual material accessible. The explicit intention was that the GTAA would be usable for other audiovisual archives and collections in the Netherlands. Expected reuse and search behaviour were the most important factors guiding the choice of thesaurus terms.
column158: Safe for production use|Safe for research use
column163: The GTAA thesaurus was used at Sound & Vision for manual annotation of audiovisual archive material. Currently the Person Names concept scheme is used by Sound & Vision for automatic annotation such as speaker and face recognition. For a time, Sound & Vision used the Subjects, Names and Geographical Names schemes to extract terms from subtitles, this has been discontinued. The thesaurus is used in the system of the national public service broadcaster NPO for programme makers to describe their material. The EYE Filmmusem and Nationaal Archief (Dutch National Archive) use the GTAA to annotate their collections. There is a large number of unknown users via the APIs or the Termennetwerk.|The GTAA can be used to create links between audiovisual collections, following Linked Data principles. It can be used directly for collections that both use the GTAA, or it can be mapped to another thesaurus or vocabulary to indirectly link collections with different vocabularies. In 2016, a pilot project was conducted to connect the Sound & Vision archive to the Flemish VIAA archive by mapping the VRT thesaurus to the GTAA.|Research into linking collections via Linked Data.

column18: The ‘NSB Propaganda films’ dataset contains film material of the Film service of the Dutch National Socialist Movement (NSB). The Film service of the NSB fell directly onder the NSB. The relatively new medium of film was very important to the propaganda machine of the NSB. The films were shown in cinemas during the German occupation. The films show the ideal that the NSB wanted to present to its members: order, discipline, patriotism, putting the interests of the country before your own interests. The films show which emotions the NSB tried to appeal to: unity, togetherness and solidarity. Films were made about pro-German subjects, such as national-socialist parades and speeches, and NSB meetings. The dataset can be accessed via Wikimedia Commons, see Section 3 for more details. This dataset is a subset of the Open Images dataset. For more information about the dataset, read the links in Section 2.4.

column86: NSB Propaganda films - digitised films
column87: The NSB was founded in 1931 and headed by A. A. Mussert. At its zenith in 1943 the movement had approximately 100,000 members. In 1941 Mussert appointed the NSB member G.J. Teunissen as leader of the Film Service of the NSB. Under his supervision they started producing their own cinema news reels 'Spiegel der Beweging' (Mirror of the Movement). The cameras of the NSB were lost in the German bombing of Rotterdam, so the production companies Polygoon and Profilti were required to supply the films, first independently, then from 1940 together with the German film company ‘Tobis’. The Film service also required cinemas to show these films. Production of the films ceased in autumn of 1944, due to lack of electricity and the closure of the cinemas After the war the property of the NSB was confiscated by the Dutch state, so the films were handed over to the RVD Filmarchief (the film archive of the Dutch Government Information Service), which later merged with other archive organisations to become the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision. The films were digitised as part of the 'Beelden van de Toekomst' (Images for the Future) project. Prior to publication, a sentence was added to the metadata of each film to indicate that it is confiscated wartime material published during the WOII Open Data Depot project. The digitised films are available on Wikimedia Commons to view and download. Metadata describing the film is available on the detail page for each film, see Section 3.2 for details.

column109: Title 1 mei, dag van de arbeid - Spiegel der beweging nr. 5 (akte 1), item 2 Description NSB-propagandafilm over de viering van de dag van de arbeid. De heer Woudenberg houdt een toespraak. Gedurende zijn toespraak toont de film beelden van de 1 mei-optochten georganiseerd door de S.D.A.P. in de jaren dertig. Ook worden er beelden getoond van arbeiders die aan het werk zijn, en van arbeiders die zich ontspannen met picknicken, marcheren en zeilen. “Geconfisqueerd oorlogsmateriaal: propagandafilms van de Filmdienst der NSB vrijgegeven ihkv WOII Open Data Depot in een samenwerkingsverband met Beeld en Geluid en Netwerk Oorlogsbronnen (mei 2018)” 01.19 Tekst : 1 mei. Dag van de arbeid, over NVV-vignet geprojecteerd. 01.25 Tekst : De heer Woudenberg commissaris van het NVV. 01.29 Woudenberg achter een microfoon houdt een toespraak over de viering van 1 mei vroeger en nu. 01.42 Beelden van andere 1 mei optochten, georganiseerd door de SDAP. 01.58 Beelden van arbeiders aan het werk, o.a. glasblazers, tramconducteurs. 02.10 Het huis van de Arbeid, Amsterdam. 02.15 Kijkje in de NVV kantoren, waarna weer beelden van werkers. 02.28 Kranten worden gedrukt. 02.35 Mensen maken zich gereed om 1 mei te gaan vieren. 02.44 Lammeren in de wei. 02.50 Mensen trekken er op de fiets op uit (noot). 03.00 Andere vormen van recreatie, o.a. speeltuin, picknicken zeilen. 03.11 Ook marcheren als recreatie. 03.13 Woudenberg weer zelf in beeld, zijn stem heeft alle voorgaande beelden begeleid. 03.21 Einde Date 1 January 1941

column6: Nature Footage Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Natuurbeelden Versie-1 Data-Envelope Versie-1
column18: The Nature Footage dataset contains exceptional moving images of nature. The unedited raw footage gives a unique insight into the special nature of the Netherlands and the Dutch Antilles. It consists of over 2,000 fragments of plants, animals and landscapes. For instance, shots of white storks, European hamsters, wild boars, the Wadden Sea, butterflies, flowers etc. Recording types include overviews, close-ups, underwater footage, aerial footage and even microscope images. The clips vary in length from a few tens of seconds to more than ten minutes, with a total of more than 650 hours of footage. The collection was produced during the Natuurbeelden (Nature Footage) project. Eleven nature organisations combined to form the Stichting Natuurbeelden (Foundation for Nature Footage). Members of the foundation formed an editorial board that determined what should be filmed and checked the quality of the footage. They commissioned professional nature film makers to create the footage, also sometimes buying archive footage from them. This footage was supplemented with historical nature films from Jack P. Loorij. The film makers were required to deliver metadata describing their footage. The Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision entered the footage and the metadata into their catalogue, enriching the metadata with genres and subjects from the GTAA thesaurus. Part of the footage - primarily shots of birds - is in the public domain. The full collection is available under licence. See the Resources in Section 3 for more details. The footage can be browsed in the CLARIAH Media Suite. It can be browsed and downloaded from Wikimedia Commons (public domain only) and the Natuurbeelden website. See the links in Section 2.4.
