column18: The ‘Correspondence of William of Orange’ makes digitally available more than 13,200 letters and related documents from and to William of Orange (1533-1584). These are all the letters and documents currently known to exist. William of Orange, also known as William the Silent (posthumous nickname), is seen as a key figure in European history and the Dutch state. He became the most important spokesperson for the noble opposition party against Philip II and played a key role in triggering the Dutch Revolt, better known as the Eighty Years’ War (1568-1648). The initially Catholic prince of Orange turned to the Protestant faith from the end of 1573. The correspondence of William of Orange from the period 1549-1584 is very extensive. His network of correspondents consisted of more than 3,000 persons and organisations, in the Netherlands and abroad. These were family, friends and acquaintances, clients, political supporters, members of the nobility, kings and government organisations. The letters cover a wide range of topics, both personal and political. In addition to letters, the dataset contains commissions, petitions, directives and speeches. Images are available for most of the letters and documents. The letters and documents have been collected from almost 200 archives and libraries in the Netherlands, Belgium, Denmark, Germany, England, France, Italy, Austria, Poland, Spain, Vatican City, Sweden and Switzerland. The Royal Collections of the Netherlands (Koninklijke Verzamelingen, KV or KHA) contains by far the most correspondence. More details on the archives and libraries visited are available in the history of the collection, which gives many details about how the collection was created and by whom. See the links in Section 2.4. The letters and documents can be searched and consulted. For many letters, downloadable scans of printed editions containing transcriptions are available, alongside a brief content summary.

column87: The correspondence was collected from over 200 archives and libraries over decades. Most of the letters and documents have been photographed or scanned. The oldest images date from the 1970s, and many are of poor quality, some are scans of xerox copies. The metadata describing the letters and documents was originally stored in a card index. This was transferred to a digital database in 1984. The images were transferred to an image bank, where they are available in PDF format. The full history of the development of the collection can be read in the introduction to the collection, see the link in Section 2.4. Each letter or document can exist in multiple manifestations. For example, the handwritten original, a contemporary copy, a modern copy, a reproduction in a book etc. These different manifestations are grouped together in the record of a single letter or document, based on the date of the letter/document. For each manifestation, a single manifestation type was selected for digital availability, based on the most original form of the document. Original autographs were prioritised over draft versions. This decision was driven by the storage capacity constraints at the time of digitisation. The metadata describing the letters and documents, together with the images of the letters where available, can be accessed via the website (see Path). Each letter/document has a brief description of the content, the first words of the letter/document (the so-called 'incipit') and comments about the letter/document. The correspondence is in the original language. The following categories of documents are included: - Letters and documents - Petitions - Directives - Commissions - Speeches - Copies - Excerpts. The correspondence can be searched on date, letter number, written from/to William of Orange, name of correspondent, location it was sent from, form of manifestation and archive. It is also possible to search all text fields for words. There is an extensive search guide available on the website, see the links in Section 2.4. The overview of search results shows a limited number of metadata fields. More fields are available when you click on an individual search result. See Section 3.2 for an overview of the metadata fields. More detail about each field is available in the search guide. The scan of an individual result can be downloaded as a PDF. Multiple results can be downloaded to spreadsheet format.

Verzendplaats: Brussel (Bruxelles) Inhoud: Bericht van goede gezondheid. Hoopt de komende zomer met hem een paardrenwedstrijd te kunnen houden op het paard dat hij van Karel V heeft gekregen. column113: This dataset does not pretend to be complete. Not all letters and documents relating to William of Orange have been found. Some letters and documents could not be found even though they are referred to in scholarly editions or auction catalogues, however this is only a few tens of documents. In these cases, all available information about the letters was added to the dataset. The dataset does not include: - Documents sent by the prince’s guardians - Financial documents, such as bills, issued to the prince - Deeds in the name of the prince - Purely personal documents such as wills and marriage certificates - Documents that are only referred to from other documents but no longer exist - Documents about the prince All letters are included in the database, but some scans of manifestations of the letters are not (the metadata of the manifestations is included). See Section 3.1 for more details about the selection of manifestations. All known manifestations held in the physical archive of the Huygens Institute are preserved in analogue form, either as microfiche or as xerox copies. Due to the various manifestations, letters can occur multiple times (up to 15-20 versions). Many of the scans, in particular the German and French minutes, can be difficult for the untrained eye to read. Renumbering of the documents during the last phase of the project did not occur consistently, so at some point a re-inventory and renumbering will occur.

column152: Starting in 1974, the documents were first microfilmed and subsequently divided per letter into microfiches, which are still preserved. In addition, xerox copies were made of many letters. In 1998, the scanning of the microfilms, microfiches and xerox copies was initiated. Only one manifestation per letter was scanned, the choice of manifestation was made according to fixed criteria agreed by the Nederlands Historisch Data Archief and the Instituut Nederlands Geschiedenis. The scans were checked and if the quality was insufficient, then the scan was repeated. A watermark identifying the source of the document is automatically applied when the scan is accessed via the database.
column157: This dataset aims to provide as complete an overview as possible of the preserved correspondence sent or received by William of Orange. It supports research into his political and diplomatic activities and into the national and international networks in which he operated, including his wider family network and other dynastic, political, and administrative connections.

column18: The ‘Barend & van Dorp’ dataset contains all the episodes of this popular Dutch talk show, which ran from 1999 to 2006. Sitting around a table, the hosts Frits Barend and Henk van Dorp discussed politics, culture and sports, in short, all facets of life with their guests. Former football player and columnist Jan Mulder was a permanent guest, writer Boudewijn Büch and political reporter Frits Wester were regular guests. The 50-minute-long show was distinctive due to its remarkable guests, emotional subjects and striking statements. It became the most watched late-night show and a true competitor of the NOVA current affairs programme. In 2002 it won the ‘Zilveren Nipkowschijf’, a Dutch TV and media award handed out by a selection of Dutch media journalists and critics to the best show of the year, the first time that a programme from a commercial broadcaster received this prize. The show was broadcast by RTL 4 from 1999 to 2005. The frequency was three times a week at first but from 1st March 1999 this was increased to every weekday. Initially, the programme was recorded in a café/restaurant in Eemnes. Later, the recordings moved to Gooiland and in 2002-2006 from the Media Park in Hilversum. In its final year, the programme was broadcast by Talpa. In total 960 episodes were made.

column16: Correspondance de Guillaume le Taciturne, prince d'Orange
column18: The ‘Correspondance de Guillaume le Taciturne, prince d'Orange’ makes digitally available a collection of letters from and to William of Orange (1533-1584), compiled by Louis Prosper Gachard in the nineteenth century. This is only a portion of the correspondence of William of Orange, which is more widely documented in the ‘Correspondence of William of Orange’ database, which includes the letters collected in the ‘Correspondance de Guillaume le Taciturne, prince d'Orange’. The ‘Correspondence of William of Orange’ database is the best starting point for research into William of Orange. William of Orange, also known as William the Silent (posthumous nickname), is seen as a key figure in Dutch history and the Dutch state. He became the most important spokesperson for the noble opposition party against Philip II and played a key role in triggering the Dutch Revolt, better known as the Eighty Years’ War (1568-1648). The initially Catholic prince of Orange turned to the Protestant faith from the end of 1573. Gachard collected the letters from the Rijksarchief Brussel and other archives in Belgium (Henegouwen, Luik, Namen), France (KB Parijs, Besançon, Dijon, Lille), Austria (Vienna) and Spain (Simancas). The letters are in their original language, primarily French. Gachard also translated some letters from their original language into French. This dataset is the digitised version of the six volumes of letters published by Gachard. The books contain the content of the letters, with a reference to the original archive where it was found and additional notes. The volumes of letters are accessible in the digital edition online (see the link in Section 2.4). The digital edition makes the letters searchable and browsable. It can be consulted in the following ways: - by using the browse function at the top of the screen or the thumbnails in the 'Thumbnails' tab - by navigating through a list of the letters in the 'Brievenlijst' tab. For many letters, you can follow a link to view the originals in the ‘Correspondence of William of Orange’ databank. - by searching the text (recognised by OCR) in the 'Search' tab. Note that not all the text is searchable, and that the OCR contains errors. The pages can be viewed as image, pdf or OCR (the recognised text). Individual pages can be downloaded. The six volumes can also be downloaded in a single file (see the Resource 'Downloadable copy of the Correspondance de Guillaume le Taciturne, prince d'Orange' in Section 3 for more details). The copyright to the texts is owned by Huygens ING.

column18: The ‘Open Images Sound & Vision’ dataset contains more than 7000 videos. It consists for a large part of Polygoon films, newsreels that were shown in Dutch cinemas from 1919 to 1987, but also includes many other films from the 20th century. The videos cover a broad range of Dutch (news) topics, such as art, culture, transportation, health, education, nature, politics, sports, pets, fashion and curiosa. The metadata is provided in Dutch and English. The content of the videos is almost exclusively Dutch. The films and metadata came from the Sound & Vision catalogue. The dataset is explicitly intended for reuse and access to the material is provided under the Creative Commons licensing model. See Section 3 for more details.

column87: The Open Images collection was created during the OpenBeelden project (2009-2015). The Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision and other providers such as the EYE Film museum published portions of their collections for reuse. This material was initially published on the Open Images platform, which will be discontinued. The ‘Open Images Sound & Vision’ dataset was also published on Wikimedia, PeerTube and in the CLARIAH Media Suite, where the collection continues to be available. It can be browsed on all three platforms and downloaded from Wikimedia and PeerTube. For many years new material was added each year as material came into the public domain. Material that was sensitive or related to still-living persons was screened out. After a legal judgment in 2020 regarding the basis for determining public domain status, this regular updating ceased. Both public domain and CC-BY-SA licences are used for the audiovisual content. The licence varies per clip. The metadata is available under CC0. PeerTube and the CLARIAH Media Suite use the mp4 videos from the Open Images platform. Wikimedia Commons uses a variety of video formats. The metadata for the videos came from the Sound & Vision catalogue. This was simplified and transformed to appropriate forms for each platform that the collection was published on. column18: The ‘Diplomatic Witnesses’ dataset is a set of 94 video recordings and transcripts of interviews with approximately 40 retired Dutch diplomats and policy officials, reflecting on the history of Dutch foreign policy and diplomacy. The interviews are with Dutch diplomats active from the 1950s to the early 2020s who were participants and eye-witnesses of important events and policy developments. They cover diverse topics from security policy, geopolitics and European integration to development work, consular services, business advocacy and conflict mediation. They give unique insights into the formation and implementation of Dutch foreign policy, paint a picture of how European cooperation developed (for example through the treaty of Maastricht and the introduction of the euro), how Atlantic cooperation remained central to Dutch security policy – even when it came under pressure - and how new themes emerged on the political agenda, from terrorism to climate change and migration. The transcripts of the videos can be searched and read, and the videos viewed. Neither the videos nor the transcripts can be downloaded.
column34: The diplomats who were interviewed were active abroad from the late 1950s to the early 2020s.They were selected for their involvement in key policy developments between 1990 and 2020. The interviews were recorded in the period January 2022 to July 2023.

column87: Each interview was videoed, and a verbatim transcript was created. The interviewer annotated the interviews with countries and organisations discussed. The interviews and transcripts can be viewed on the website, see Path. Using speech recognition technology, the texts were linked to the video, so that clicking on a section of the transcript jumps directly to the relevant fragment in the video. The videos can be searched based on words in the text, and the results link directly to the relevant video fragments. It is also possible to search on name, country and organisation. The date that the interview was recorded is also visible. The interviews roughly follow the career of the interviewee. They were mostly recorded in several sessions per interviewee. Archive research was carried out in preparation for the interviews. After the interviews, interviewees were able to review the video and give permission for publication. Where interviewees requested that certain passages not be published, these requests were honoured. Some interviews were redacted in accordance with privacy laws (to protect personal data) or to remove classified information. Where this is the case, this is stated in the information about the interview.

column2: G.K. van Hogendorp, Journal d'Adrichem and Journal de La Haye Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: G.K. van Hogendorp, Journal d'Adrichem and Journal de La Haye Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column16: G.K. van Hogendorp, Journal d'Adrichem and Journal de La Haye
column18: The dataset ‘G.K. van Hogendorp, Journal d'Adrichem and Journal de La Haye’ makes the diaries of Gijsbert Karel van Hogendorp (1762-1834) from 1806-1813 digitally available. Van Hogendorp is best known for his prominent role in the transition of the Netherlands from French rule to the Sovereign Principality of the United Netherlands, the forerunner of the modern-day Netherlands, in 1813. He was an Orangist governor from Rotterdam with functions in politics and the civil service. He lost these in 1795 for the duration of the Batavian Republic. In 1805 the Van Hogendorp family moved their permanent home to ‘Adrichem’ near Beverwijk, where Van Hogendorp led a withdrawn, retired existence. In 1809 the family moved to a mansion on the Kneuterdijk in The Hague. In both locations Van Hogendorp kept a diary. The diaries are not diaries in the strict sense of the word. Van Hogendorp did not complete entries every day (often he skipped days and even weeks). The entries contain rational and contemplative reflections about the outside world, including his thoughts on the political and military events in the Netherlands and abroad, rather than descriptions of daily life. Occasionally he mentioned his own business and financial problems, and personal concerns about his fate. He also regularly added other material, such as letters. This dataset contains the complete text, as PDF scans and OCR (Optical Character Recognition) text, of book KS 51 from the series of historical books ‘Rijks Geschiedkundige Publicatiën’. In KS 51 the diary entries of the Journal d'Adrichem and Journal de La Haye were published, in the Dutch style of French in which they were written and in chronological order. The creators of the book annotated the diary entries in Dutch with background information about events and persons, and references to other works. The book opens with a Dutch introduction detailing Van Hogendorp’s life, with particular emphasis on the events mentioned in the diaries. It also contains the Van Hogendorp family trees and a subject index. An appendix contains two memoranda from 1808 about the Dutch East Indies (the colonial name for present-day Indonesia) and West Indies (the colonial name for the Dutch Caribbean islands, Suriname and the West-African colonies) The dataset is available as a digital edition (see the links in Section 2.4). Users can read the text and search the OCR for specific words. The dataset is also available to download, see Section 3 for more details.

column2: Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The ‘Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News’ makes available the daily national news bulletins broadcast on Dutch television by public broadcasters. It includes children’s news programs, but not sports news or regional news. As the focus is on television, this collection does not include cinema newsreels or web videos produced by the news broadcasters. Up until 1996, only selected fragments of TV news programs were stored. After that, selected complete programs were archived, and from 2006 onwards all Dutch TV news programs are archived in full. The longest running news program was simply called ‘Journaal’ from 1956 up until 2018, and since then ‘NOS Journaal’. The first broadcast, on 5th January 1956, is stored in the archive. From 1956 to 1969 it was broadcast by the NTS, since then by the NOS. Initially, three programs were broadcast per week, with film fragments but without a presenter. The first presenter was introduced in 1957, and the first female presenter in 1965. The number of bulletins per day changed over time. In particular, additional news bulletins were introduced when daytime television started in 1993. These were initially sporadically archived. From 2001 onwards the 8pm bulletin was archived daily, while other bulletins were archived once a month. Currently multiple editions of the news are broadcast each day, varying from 5 minutes to 30 minutes in length. Since September 2006 these are all archived in full. Repeats of the editions are not archived. Simulcasts of bulletins on other broadcasters have sometimes been archived separately, sometimes not (as they are identical in content). Editions with sign language (‘met gebarentaal/gebarentolk’) are archived, as are extra news bulletins (‘Extra NOS Journaal’). Every year, an overview of the year is produced and archived (‘Jaaroverzicht’). From 2007-2016, a news bulletin was also broadcast every weekday on another station, called ‘NOS Journaal op 3’ and later ‘NOS op 3’. In 2015 the regular bulletins were replaced with short news flashes, which ended in 2016 when the program moved entirely online. The children’s news program is called ‘Jeugdjournaal’. From 2001 onwards this was archived daily. The Jeugdjournaal is currently broadcast daily on television. It also has editions with sign language and a year overview, all of which are archived. Other important children’s news programs in the past were ‘Schooltv-weekjournaal’ (1981-2014) and ‘Zapp Weekjournaal’ (2014-2017). For all news programs, the format, program names and broadcasting schedule changed over the years. Individual exceptions also occurred, depending on the surrounding programming, holidays, or breaking news. Up until the final quarter of 2020, the main news programs also included the ‘clean feeds’, raw material used to produce the program. These clean feeds had no logos or captions and typically were more extensive than the clips used in the programs. As they are very valuable for reuse, since the final quarter of 2020 these are offered in a separate dataset, but still linked to the news programs they are used in, by referring to the season title and the broadcast date. The ‘Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News’ dataset is available on the portals listed in Section 2.4. Metadata is available to everyone, access to the audiovisual content is restricted. See Section 3 for more details. Note that there is no definitive way to search for exactly all the TV news programs. However, searching for 'journaal' or 'jeugdjournaal' in the series title in combination with filtering on 'televisie' in the publication channel will deliver a reasonable approximation. For additional help, read the guide on TV news written for researchers working in the CLARIAH Media Suite, linked in Section 2.4.

The Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News collection uses the general Sound & Vision metadata model, described in the general Sound & Vision colelction data-envelope. The 'Dutch Public Broadcaster Television News' collection uses the fields described in the data-envelope for the 'Sound & Vision Public Broadcaster Television' collection, see that data-envelope for information about enrichments such as subtitles, face and speaker detection. column201: Selection of archival items was performed by Sound & Vision archivists according to the institutional selection policy in effect at that moment in time. The aim was to preserve Dutch cultural heritage from the audiovisual media. Metadata policy determined which of the selected items were described, in how much detail and with which techniques. Attitudes about which media count as cultural heritage and how valuable certain types of archive items are have changed over time, affecting these policies. These policies were also affected by the interest of the broader media community in the reuse of archival material. Within the policies, selection and description were influenced by the particular interests, expertise and biases of the archivists. Archivists were predominantly Dutch nationals and native Dutch speakers.

column2: Diaries and notes of Willem Hendrik de Beaufort 1874-1918 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Diaries and notes of Willem Hendrik de Beaufort 1874-1918 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Dagboeken en aantekeningen van Willem Hendrik de Beaufort 1874-1918 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column16: Diaries and notes of Willem Hendrik de Beaufort 1874-1918
column18: The dataset ‘Diaries and notes of Willem Hendrik de Beaufort 1874-1918' makes the diaries of liberal politician Willem Hendrik de Beaufort (1845-1918) digitally available. He was a member of the Dutch elite but was also curious and had a broad outlook on the world around him. De Beaufort was a member of the Dutch Provincial Council, the Dutch Parliament and was minister for Foreign Affairs from 1897 to 1901. A number of important events occurred during this period, which he described in his diaries in detail: the crowning of Queen Wilhelmina, the peace conferences in The Hague and the start of the Boer War. The politics of the Netherlands, at home and abroad, is the most important topic in the diaries up until 1914, when World War I takes centre stage. The diary is not self-reflection, but a reaction to events. It contains a description and analysis of structural changes in Dutch politics (such as the ebbing of the influence of the liberals, the power takeover by the confessionalists and the growing influence of the socialists) and regular brief sketches of persons. De Beaufort mainly wrote in Dutch, with some notes in French. The dataset contains scans and OCR (Optical Character Recognition) text for the entire editions of volumes KS 73 and 74 from the series ‘Rijks Geschiedkunstige Publicatiën’, in which De Beaufort’s diaries and notes were published. The first volume opens with an introduction describing the life and work of De Beaufort, and includes a genealogical overview, a list of the offices held by De Beaufort, and an overview of the volumes, including the periodisation of the entries. These are followed by the diary entries themselves, annotated with persons, references and explanations. These are divided over the two volumes. The second volume concludes with an appendix containing a number of undated notes written by De Beaufort, a literature list, a general index on subjects, geographical locations and persons with biographical information, and references for the illustrations. A detailed description of how the diaries and notes were selected and processed for publication is included in the Introduction. This includes aspects such as how the multiple diary volumes (some of which De Beaufort used in parallel) were combined into a single chronologically ordered whole, the multiple renumbering of the entries, policies regarding dating, spelling and punctuation, and the roles of contributors. The dataset is available in the digital edition online (see link in Section 2.4), which makes the full contents searchable and browsable. The dataset can also be downloaded, see the Resource in Section 3 for more details).
column34: Volume 1 runs from 1874-1910, volume 2 from 1911-1918. 1874-1875 - loose sheets of notes. 1876-1900 - sporadic notes in a diary. 1900-1914 - more of a diary form and more frequent entries, but not daily. 1914-1918 - regular entries and, after World War I broke out, more frequent and extended entries. The diaries also discuss events prior to the period in which they were written.

column2: Personal Record Cards and Archive cards of Amsterdam 1939-1994 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Personal Record Cards and Archive cards of Amsterdam 1939-1994 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Persoons- en archiefkaarten van Amsterdam 1939-1994 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column16: Personal Record Cards and Archive cards of Amsterdam 1939-1994
column18: The ‘Personal Record Cards and Archive cards of Amsterdam 1939-1994’ dataset makes available the registration details, such as name, date of birth, address and family members, of persons living in Amsterdam at some point during that period. Information for still-living persons is blocked. From 1st January 1850 onwards, all municipalities in the Netherlands were required to register information about the population. After initially registering the information in books, Amsterdam switched to a card-based system in 1893, storing information on a card per family. In 1939 a card per person was introduced, the ‘person card’. The information from the family cards was used to create the new person cards. During this process, some information was omitted, such as previous marriages or children who had moved away from home. When a person moved away from Amsterdam, the card was sent to their new municipality, and when they died it was sent first to the CBS (National Office of Statistics) and then to the CBG (National Office of Genealogy). In all cases, before a card was sent away a copy was created, called the ‘archive card’. To create this copy, between 1939 and 1960 the information was typed onto thin pieces of paper, between 1960 and 1964 a photograph was taken, and between 1964 and 1991/94 photocopies were made. In 1994, the card system was replaced by the GBA, a national automated system. The person cards and archive cards were transferred to the City Archives of Amsterdam, scanned and indexed. A sample of original cards was preserved for historical research and exhibitions. This dataset consists of: - digital scans of the personal record cards and archive cards, these can be accessed via the index or browsed directly - a digital index for the personal record cards and archive cards, this can be used to search for persons. These resources are described in further detail in the Resources in Level 3. For more details about this dataset read the links in section 2.4
column34: Coverage during the period of the Second World War is less due to records being destroyed. Coverage is incomplete in 1991-1994 as some records were already being entered in the new computer system. See Section 3.4 for more details.

column87: The digital scans of the personal record cards and the archive cards show the person's information. Individual scans of record cards can be viewed by searching the personal record card/archive card index via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page and clicking on the search results. Individual scans can be downloaded from the search result. The scans can also be browsed on the City Archives of Amsterdam website pages for personal record cards and archive cards, see the links in Path. The cards of persons of particular interest have their own category. Note that scans of still-living persons are unavailable. The person cards, pale yellow for men and light grey for women, are divided into 35 numbered fields. These include personal information including name, birthdate and place, death, husband/wife, children, profession, address(es). Some fields may be blacked out to protect the person's privacy. See the links in Section 2.4 for a full description of all the fields and an explanation of the commonly used abbreviations.|The index contains selected key details from the cards, allowing the personal record cards and archive cards to be searched. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can search this index to find persons in the cards. The index can also be downloaded via a mechanism called OAI-PMH. See the links in Path for more information about using and downloading the index. The index was manually created by entering data read from the scans. The changes made in the period 1991-1994 were recorded in the GBA rather than on the cards. These digital files are included in the search index, but the files themselves will not be available until 2094 as it is not possible to block sensitive information in the file. The list of Data Fields in 3.2 indicates which information is available in the index. column113: All cards belonging to a person who was born less than 100 years ago, and therefore is potentially still living, are not available to search or view. In exceptional circumstances, a card may be blocked for longer if relatives object to its publications. Some cards are missing. During the Second World War some cards were destroyed, mainly those belonging to Jews. After the war, new person cards were constructed for people who came out of hiding or returned from a concentration camp. Archive cards were made for those who had died but for whom no further details of the death were known, and information from Red Cross lists was manually added later. During the period of transition to the GBA automated system, between 1991 and 1994, new records were directly created in the GBA, and changes of address or births were registered there instead of on paper. The digital files have been extracted from the GBA and archived, and are included in the same search index as the personal record and archive cards, but the digital files themselves will not be available to view until 2094 due to privacy issues. Some details may be incorrect due to changes not being reported by the persons involved. Information about profession is often incomplete and sometimes changes of address are not registered. If a person was found to no longer be residing at their given address when a check was carried out, then they were listed as ‘VOW’: Vertrokken Onbekend Waarheen (Departed, unknown where to). When a section on a card was full, for example after many changes of address, then a new card was created, and not all details were transferred to the new card, instead a comment was made such as ‘diverse adressen’ (various addresses). Administration was less reliable during the Second World War, for example people in hiding naturally didn’t register their change of address. In cases where the civil servant filling in the card didn’t know if a name was a first name or surname, then the entire name can be filled in in just one of the two fields. Where surnames were not customary in a person’s country of origin, such as Indonesia, then there is no surname on the card. For clergy who used the appellation ‘zuster’ or ‘broeder’ (Sister or Brother), the name can be filled in in both the first name and surname fields. Some details are blacked out to protect the personal information of people who may still be living. These may be accessible on special request, email stadsarchief@amsterdam.nl|All cards belonging to a person who was born less than 100 years ago, and therefore is potentially still living, are not available to search or view. In exceptional circumstances, a card may be blocked for longer if relatives object to its publications. Some cards are missing. During the Second World War some cards were destroyed, mainly those belonging to Jews.

column6: Computer Games Archive Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Computerspel Archief Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Computer Games Archive’ makes available computer games that have played a significant role in the Dutch computer game landscape. The archive includes games created by Dutch makers or that have an intrinsic link with the Netherlands (e.g. Dutch locations feature in the game), which exemplify important phenomena in the history of games in the Netherlands. The guideline of the archive is the Dutch Computer Games Canon (see link in Section 2.4), which maintains a list of culturally significant Dutch computer games. The ‘Computer Games Archive’ strives to preserve all the computer games in the canon. Games with a high profile in the Netherlands are also preserved, such as winners of the annual Dutch Game Awards. Games range from early games developed by pioneers experimenting in attics, to the first mobile games, to current games with visual effects that rival those in the cinema. In addition to the games themselves, related objects such as packaging, design documentation, promotional materials and merchandise are also archived. Metadata, photos and gameplay footage can be searched and browsed online. Access to the games themselves and the related (physical) objects is available on request. See Section 3 for more information.

column34: Temporal scope will grow as more recent games are added. There are no restrictions placed on the temporal scope. The archive includes computer games from the present day.

column157: Games are one of the most important forms of entertainment and are part of the Dutch media cultural landscape. They can also serve other purposes, such as education (e.g. games for schoolchildren to learn the topography of the Netherlands, Wii games for training heart surgeons) or therapeutic purposes (e.g. nostalgic games for dementia patients). The 'Computer Games Archive' is intended to provide a representative picture of computer game developments and the most important participants, to be able to tell the history of computer games in the Netherlands on the basis of the archived games.

column2: Sports associations, sports clubs and sports periodicals in the Netherlands until 1940 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Sportbonden, sportclubs en sportperiodieken in Nederland tot 1940 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Sports associations, sports clubs and sports periodicals in the Netherlands until 1940 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column16: Sports associations, sports clubs and sports periodicals in the Netherlands until 1940
column18: The database ‘Sports associations, sports clubs and sports periodicals in the Netherlands until 1940’ makes digitally available information about gymnastics, hockey, korfball, chess, tennis and football clubs and associations that were in existence from the introduction of organised sport in the Netherlands up until 1940. The selected six sports had the most participants in this period and are still widely practiced today. All six sports had national and regional associations to which clubs belonged. In addition to a ‘neutral’ association, football, gymnastics and korfball also had associations of a religious or political persuasion. The database contains information about the clubs/associations such as when they were started, where they were located, on what level they operated, in which periodicals they published information etc. The information was obtained from address books and yearbooks of national and regional associations, complemented by reports from sports periodicals, papers and jubilee books. More information about the clubs/associations can be found in the periodicals and other publications that are referenced in the database entries. The database gives insight into the growth of sport in the Netherlands and how this related to social developments such as ‘pillarisation’ (the vertical separation of society into groups according to their beliefs). The database can be searched on club/association name, sport type, place/province, year/period and religious/political persuasion. Search results can be downloaded as a spreadsheet file. See the links in Section 2.4 to search the database online. The website hosting the database also contains overviews of the clubs/associations. There is information explaining what is in the database, how it was created and how to search it, and an introduction per sport, describing its development from its introduction up until 1940. The sources used to create the database are listed in the literature list. See Section 3 for information about downloading a copy of the database.
column25: Directories|Surveys of literature in a subject area
column34: 1803 is the earliest known year in which an association was founded. After the First World War sport broke through to the working classes and many clubs were founded by young men who had experienced sport during their mobilisation. In 1910-1929 sport became part of ‘pillarisation’ (the vertical separation of society into groups according to their beliefs) when Protestant, Catholic and Socialist sports associations were founded. Between 1929 and 1940 the most important ‘neutral’ associations received the appellation ‘Koninklijk’ (Royal). On 1st August 1940 all football associations merged into a single organisation. This radical change was chosen as the endpoint of the database.

column113: The database contains most associations and clubs, but not all. All clubs that were affiliated with a regional or national association at some point should be included, but some clubs were never affiliated with an association, which made it harder to find traces of their existence. For most sports probably only a small number of clubs will be missing, however for football the number is probably larger. That is because there was a number of ‘wild’ associations that were not recognised by the K.N.V.B, such as clubs that belonged to factories or offices, or regional associations mainly in West-Friesland, Groningen and Friesland. Most sports placed no restrictions on the club names, so many clubs have the same name. The exceptions were the K.N.V.B from August 1911 onwards, and the K.N.K.B, who specified that names must be unique. The date on which the club/association was founded is not always reliable, in particular in the cases of mergers or clubs that 'continued' on from a club that had ceased to exist. Research has also shown that there could be a delay between a club being founded/disbanded and the club name being registered/removed from the register. Some football clubs that were disbanded in the 1920s were only removed from the register in 1941-1942. The database does not include details of championships or relegations. Nor does it include information about the board, the kit, grounds or members. These details can often be found in the periodicals, yearbooks and address books of the associations themselves.
column128: The six sports included in the database were chosen because they had the most participants up until 1940 and had a broad and diverse organisation. Football was a ‘man’s sport’, the other sports were practised by both men and women. Sport started as an elite hobby for those who had time and money to spare. After the First World War it broke through to the working classes. Football, gymnastics and korfball were ‘people’s sports’ between 1920 and 1940. Hockey, tennis and chess remained more elite sports.

column157: This database was created because, despite the importance of sport in society, it can be hard to find information on the development of the sport in the Netherlands in reference works or on the internet. The database is intended for professional historians and all other interested parties who want to research the emergence and growth of sport in the Netherlands, at local and national level. The database can also assist in researching ‘pillarisation’ (the vertical separation of society into groups according to their beliefs) in the Netherlands and the emancipation of social groups.

column18: The dataset ‘Wob/WOO Decisions Amsterdam’ makes digitally available the Freedom of Information dossiers (requests, decisions and any published documents answering the request) of the City of Amsterdam. The Wob(Wet Openbaarheid van Bestuur – Government Information (Public Access) Act) was the original Freedom of Information law in the Netherlands. It was replaced on 1st May 2022 by the WOO (Wet Open Overheid – Open Government Act). The law requires government to respond to Wob/WOO requests for existing documentation, either publishing the requested information or explaining why publication is not possible. Once the requested information is sent to the requester then it is legally available for everyone. The City of Amsterdam has chosen to immediately transfer such information to the City Archives of Amsterdam, from where it is published on both the City Archives page and the Open Amsterdam portal. The dataset consists of the Wob/Woo dossiers, each containing the original anonymised request, the decision and, if the decision is to publish information, the published documents. Very sizeable and high-profile Wob/Woo requests may be published as a separate archive.
column34: The temporal scope of the archive will change as new dossiers are published.
column52: City Archives of Amsterdam|City of Amsterdam
column80: New Wob/Woo decisions are archived every week.
column86: Online Wob/Woo Decisions Amsterdam
column87: The Wob/Woo dossiers consist of three parts: the original anonymised request, the decision to publish the information or not (in the form of an anonymised letter to the requester), and any published documents. Each dossier is given a short description clearly indicating the content of the request. The College of Mayor and Aldermen of the City of Amsterdam is ultimately responsible for deciding whether to publish information or not. It is possible that some documents or parts of documents cannot be published due to confidential business information or privacy issues. The decision describes which documents are and are not published, and for what reason. The documents are mostly published in PDF format. They contain the necessary information to answer the Freedom of Information request and may come from different departments. If more than 30 documents are released, then these are clustered. Each file or cluster gets a clear title that is listed in an inventory of the documents that indicates what is or isn't publicly available. Where a document was already publicly available, it is not included in the dossier but a link to the document is provided in the inventory. The names of aldermen are publicly visible in the documents. The names, addresses, telephone numbers etc. of other persons, such as civil servants or citizens, are blanked out for privacy reasons. This is done by the municipality administration prior to transfer to the archive. The dossiers can be browsed on the City Archives website. They can be searched via the Open Amsterdam portal. The user can search for words in the dosser description or the file names. The results can be filtered on dossier and/or date. On both websites an individual document or a whole dossier can be downloaded if the user enters their email address, which is deleted after the download link is sent. Note that downloading individual documents can lead to loss of context. It is not possible to download the entire dataset at once. See Section 2.4 for the links to the City Archive website and Open Amsterdam.

column18: This is the Public Broadcaster Radio collection maintained by the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision (NISV). It primarily contains recordings of Dutch public broadcast radio programs, with some incidental commercial radio recordings and other recordings related to historical radio, such as the first audio recording of Edison and test radio transmissions. The coverage of the archive has varied over time, see Section 3 for more details. The NISV radio collection includes items from 1877 to present. Unfortunately, neither the first experimental Dutch radio broadcast, by Hanso Schotanus à Steringa Idzerda in 1919, nor the first official Dutch radio broadcast, by the Hilversumsche Draadlooze Omroep (HDO) in 1923, were preserved because equipment for recording the live broadcasts simply did not exist. The earliest Dutch fragment in the archive is a recording of a radio tune from an Idzerda music box. Archival was fragmentary at first but steadily increased. At present all radio programs on public service broadcasters are recorded 24/7. Twice a year, an entire week of Dutch public service broadcast radio is recorded, along with the broadcasts of a number of commercial radio stations. The collection also includes interviews with radio employees who worked between 1930 and 1980, and many recordings from offshore radio. The collection consists of both the audio recordings (where available) and the descriptive metadata. Access varies for media and metadata so check the Access section. Standard access is via portals. Download of metadata, e.g. for computational analysis, requires special arrangements with NISV. Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy of the media may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis.

column87: Metadata was originally entered by Sound & Vision archivists. Increasingly, metadata was imported from the production systems of programme makers until manual annotation ceased almost completely. Currently, production metadata is enriched by detecting voices. The Sound & Vision Radio collection uses the general Sound & Vision metadata model, described in the data-envelope link. See this data-envelope for information about the general metadata fields available and about significant changes to metadata models and systems over time. In Section 3.2 we describe the metadata fields that are of specific interest to the Radio collection. The metadata is available in the open access and restricted access portals listed in Section 2.4. Note that the exact names of metadata fields may differ slightly between portals. Very few programmes are preserved from the early years of radio, and these are often fragments. Radio broadcasts were only incidentally archived until archiving of important Dutch radio fragments started in 1947. The number of archived programmes increases over time, but archival was dependent on voluntary deposits until 1997. With the introduction of magnetic tape in 1970 the deposits became more representative, and coverage increased during the 1990s when CDs made storage cheaper. From 2002 onwards the Radio 1 and Radio 5 stations were fully archived, from October 2002 onwards this was also the case for Radio 2, Radio 3, Radio 4 and Radio 6. Since 2006 all public radio broadcasts are fully archived. In 1978 NISV started to periodically archive a complete week of public service radio - including advertisements, jingles and international media. This was first done 4 times a year (March, June, October, December), then 3 times a year in 2001-2003 (week 10, week 40 and week 50). In 2003 this became biannual (week 10 and 40). In 2005, 27th October, Unesco World Heritage Day, was added. Only public service broadcast radio that is broadcast over the airwaves is regularly archived. For example, archival of the radio station FunX didn’t start until 2016 when it transitioned from being a commercial radio station to a public service station, and archival of Radio 6 stopped in 2015 when it became a purely internet radio station. Since 2005, a selection of commercial radio is stored. Up until 2001 only fragments of programs were annotated with metadata. From September 2001 onwards complete radio programs were annotated. From 2014 onwards the voices of selected speakers were detected and the names of the speakers included in the metadata. Some radio programmes have been run through a speech recogniser to generate a speech transcript. Metadata fields are not filled in for all programs. To get an idea of the coverage of a given metadata field, you can use the Inspect tool in the Media Suite|These are the actual radio recordings stored by NISV. Some are available for anyone to browse via the open access public portal. Others are restricted, and are only available to view via the restricted access portals. See Section 2.4 for a list of portals. Recordings from 2008 onwards are in one hour blocks, with both a high-resolution and a low-resolution copy stored. Recordings may not be downloaded from restricted access portals. Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis.

column201: Selection of archival items was performed by archivists at Sound & Vision and its predecessors according to the institutional selection policy in effect at that moment in time. The aim was to preserve Dutch cultural heritage from the audiovisual media. Metadata policy determined which of the selected items were described, in how much detail and with which techniques. Attitudes about which media count as cultural heritage and how valuable certain types of archive items are have changed over time, affecting these policies. These policies were also affected by the interest of the broader media community in the reuse of archival material. Within the policies, selection and description were influenced by the particular interests, expertise and biases of the archivists. Archivists and policymaker were predominantly Dutch nationals and native Dutch speakers.

column18: The 'Amsterdam Municipal Gazette 1858-2013' dataset makes digitally available the executive proposals, verbatim minutes and decisions of the Amsterdam City Council. In some cases, attachments to the decisions are also published. Sometimes publication in the gazette was necessary in order to make the decision legally binding. The gazette was started in 1858. In 2013 paper publication of the gazette ceased. Since 2014 the gazette is published online only. The gazette can be searched and browsed online, and individual books and scans can be downloaded. The dataset contains practically all of the gazettes, and consists of: - digital scans of the gazette pages - digital scans of indices of the gazette. See Section 3 for more details.
column34: The content and layout has varied over time. The indices do not cover the entire period.

column87: The digital scans of the gazette pages are available for searching and browsing. All of the gazettes were printed, but both the layout and the content of the gazette have changed over time. In particular, from 1866 onwards, the gazette was divided up into a number of sections (‘afdelingen’) to separate different types of information. During World War II the City Council was suspended, so there were no minutes, and a section was added for regulations laid down by the occupier. How the number and content of the sections changed over time is described in detail in the archive inventory, see the link in Section 2.4. The gazette was created by the 'Afdeling Gemeenteblad van de Secretarie' (Gazette department of the secretarial office), which was called the 'Bestuursdienst' (Administrative Service) after 1998. The gazette was bound in books per year. The covers and pages of the books were digitally scanned in 2022 by Picturae. In 2023 OCR (Optical Character Recognition) was carried out by Karmac to make the text searchable. Some decisions have related attachments. In the period 1870-1927 and from 2002 onwards these were included with the decision. In 1928-2001 the attachments were published in a separate book. The scans can be browsed at the bottom of the archive inventory page, see the link in Path. The scans can be searched via Transkribus, see the link in Path. In both cases, individual pages can be downloaded, in various formats. It is also possible to request PDFs of entire books. This requires an email address to be entered so that the PDF can be sent by email. The address is deleted after the PDF is sent. The results of the text recognition can be browsed via Transkribus but not downloaded.|Various paper indices of the gazette were manually created by civil servants, to help users to look up relevant information in the gazette. The digital scans of these indices are available for searching and browsing. The following indices exist: 'Overzicht der handelingen' - Alphabetical indices of the topics discussed between 1851 and 1890, with one book for 1851-1857 and thereafter a book per year. For each topic, the dates on which that topic was discussed are listed. 'Algemeen Alfabetisch Register' - Alphabetical indices of the topics discussed, per year for the periods 1890-1954, 1956-1990 and 1994. For each topic, there is a reference to the section of the gazette and the serial number under which the topic can be found. 'Algemeen register 'regelbestand' - Alphabetical indices of the topics discussed per year over the years 1995-2002. Additionally, there is the 'systematische index' which groups the topics per council department. For each topic, there is a reference to the year and the serial number under which the topic can be found, in Section 3 of the gazette. ‘Index op achternaam van raadsleden’ - Alphabetical index (by surname) of the councillors over the period 1876-1992. For each councillor, there is a summary with their name, date of birth and the period that they served as councillor. Sometimes their political persuasion is also listed (e.g. 'vrijzinnig democraat'). This is followed by a year, with cut-out excerpts from the topic index that relate to the councillor pasted in. This is repeated for all years in which there are relevant excerpts. The indices were bound in books per year. The pages mainly contain printed text, but some additions were made in handwriting, for example the years in the index of councillor names. The covers and pages of the books were digitally scanned in 2022 by Picturae. In 2023 OCR (Optical Character Recognition) was carried out by Karmac to make the text searchable. The scans can be browsed at the bottom of the archive inventory page, see the link in Path. The scans can be searched via Transkribus, see the link in Path. In both cases, individual pages can be downloaded, in various formats. It is also possible to request PDFs of entire books. This requires an email address to be entered so that the PDF can be sent by email. The address is deleted after the PDF is sent. The results of the text recognition can be browsed via Transkribus but not downloaded. The indices have been scanned as images but the information from the indices has not been entered into a digital index, so they cannot be used to search the gazette via the online search interface. Instead, you can use the search interface to search the index scans for, e.g. the name of a councillor, then use the information in the index scan to manually look up the relevant parts of the gazette.

column2: Amsterdam Municipal Police Archive Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Amsterdam Municipal Police Archive Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Archief van de Gemeentepolitie Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Amsterdam Municipal Police Archive’ dataset makes digitally available documents that span the entire history of the Amsterdam Municipal Police. The archive covers a period in which many significant events involving the police occurred in Amsterdam, such as the housing protests and riots in the 1980s and the tragic Bijlmer plane crash in 1992. The archive is far from complete as many documents were never archived, transfer of archives to the City Archives occurred irregularly, and many documents were destroyed. This dataset also includes the documents of the police volunteer corps (‘Korps Vrijwillige Politie’). The municipal police was under the control of the mayor and was responsible for enforcing municipal laws and regulations. For example, policing public events, licensing the sale of alcohol, issuing passports, registering immigrants, child protection, vice squad etc. The ‘Amsterdam Municipal Police Archive’ begins in the time of transition from French rule to Dutch independence. In 1843 the ‘Night Watch’ was merged into the municipal police. From 1851 onwards the municipal police was also legally required to assist the national police. The organisation of the police in Amsterdam was divided into cantons, later called sections and then districts, the number changing over time. These are reflected in the archive structure. On 1st April 1994 the municipal police was absorbed into the regional police Amsterdam-Amstelland. A detailed history of the municipal police, including the departmental organisation and the location of stations, is available via a link in Section 2.4. The original order of the archive was disrupted, so after an inventory in 1998 the City Archives of Amsterdam reordered it. See Section 3 for more details. The names of the files and folders can be searched via the City Archives of Amsterdam search interface. The content of the files cannot yet be searched. Many documents have been digitally scanned, and these can be browsed. Where scans are not yet available they can be requested, or an appointment can be made to view the originals at the City Archives. Not all dossiers are publicly accessible due to privacy and witness protection concerns. These have been placed under embargo and will become publicly accessible after a fixed amount of time has passed, see the link in 2.4 for more details. The dossiers may be viewable upon special request, contact the City Archives of Amsterdam. The folder structure, files and scans can also be browsed online, see details in Section 3. The dataset consists of: - the digital scans of the documents in the period 1803-1956 - the digital scans of the documents in the period 1957-1993 More information about these scans is available in Section 3.
column34: Although the municipal police begain in 1814, some correspondence from the Night Watch goes back to 1803. Few files are available between 1803 and ca. 1956. Dossiers relating to the Bijlmer disaster that occurred in 1992 contain documents from up until 2002.

column87: This collection of scans contains the archived municipal police documents from between 1814 and 1956. Early documents are handwritten, later documents are typed. Initially, the archive was organised in series of correspondence to and from various persons. From 1843 onwards, the series were divided into thematic sections. Of these, the sections ‘dienstzaken’ (service issues), ‘algemene zaken’ (general issues) and ‘comptabiliteit’ (bookkeeping) were archived from 1878 onwards, and the section ‘personen’ (persons) from 1913 onwards. From around 1920 onwards each separate police department maintained their own archive, according to their own system. Documents were stored in folders by subject. Some important documents from prior to 1920 were also included in these folders. Correspondence was no longer stored in series but in these folders. In an inventory by the City Archives of Amsterdam in 1998, it was not always possible to trace which department a document came from, so the documents from this period are now organised by subject, with within each subject folder a division into subfolders for the departments, where known. Amsterdam had police stations in various neighbourhoods. These each maintained their own archive, which is included in this archive. The police archives were transferred to the city archives at irregular intervals during the existence of the municipal police. After 2000, a number of additions were received from various sources. The scans are structured into the main archives and the additions: ‘Archieven van het hoofdbureau’ (Archives of the main police station) - These are the documents from the main police station, split into those archived before 1920 (including the Night Watch) and those archived after 1920. ‘Archieven van de wijkbureaus’ (Archives of the neighbourhood police stations) - These are organised per location. ‘Aanvulling hoofdbureau 2000’ - this is an addition to the documents from the main police station, received in 2000. ‘Aanvulling Bureau Joodsche Zaken (Bureau 11) 2009’ - these are the documents from the department dealing with Jewish Affairs during the war, received from the NIOD in 2009. ‘Aanvulling Hoofdbureau Recherchedienst 2010’ - this is an addition to the documents from the main police station, consisting of investigation documents received in 2010. ‘Aanvulling Vreemdelingendienst 2017’ - These are correspondence and annual reports from the (predecessor to) the Immigrant Service from 1858-1943, received in 2017. ‘Aanvulling Dagrapporten 2022’ - These are reports regarding important events in the period 1940-1945, received from the NIOD in 2017. For more details about the history and development of the archive, including the subject categories, departments and sources of additional material, see the link in Path. The archive can be browsed at the link in Path, at the bottom of the page. The folder structure is shown, and can be expanded to see subfolders, file names, and scans where available. For information on searching the archive, see the description in Section 2.1.|This collection of scans contains the archived municipal police documents from between 1957 and 1993. Scans of the archive of the Police Volunteer Corps (‘Korps Vrijwillige Politie’) are also included. The archive begins around the time that the Corps was set up, in 1946. The documents are typed. In this time period, each separate police department within the municipal police maintained their own archive, according to their own system. Documents were stored in folders by subject. In an inventory by the City Archives of Amsterdam in 1998, it was not always possible to trace which department a document came from, so the documents from this period are now organised by subject. The police archives were transferred to the city archives at irregular intervals during the existence of the municipal police. The scans are structured into the municipal police archive and the volunteer corps archive: - ‘Archief van de Gemeentepolitie’ - Municipal police archive, 1957-1993, divided into subfolders on subjects - ‘Archief van het Korps Vrijwillige Politie’ - Police Volunteer Corps archive, 1946-ca. 1970, divided into subfolders on subjects. The archive can be browsed at the link in Path, at the bottom of the page. The folder structure is shown, and can be expanded to see subfolders, file names, and scans where available. For information on searching the archive, see the description in Section 2.1.

Letters from the municipal police have also been found in other municipal archives, indicating that they were lost from the police archive. Police reports were routinely deleted after a certain amount of time had passed. An exception was made for high-profile cases, which were archived.

column2: Digital Bibliography of Dutch History Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Digitale Bibliografie Nederlandse Geschiedenis Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Digital Bibliography of Dutch History Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The Digital Bibliography of Dutch History (Digitale Bibliografie Nederlandse Geschiedenis, DBNG) makes digitally available the titles of more than 200,000 publications – books, journals and online publications – about Dutch history, from prehistory to the end of the 20th century. The DBNG includes the five most important historical journals from the beginning of Dutch historical scholarship (1850-1950). Subject, author, location and date information is available for the titles. The DBNG regarded the following as ‘Dutch history’: - Everything that occurred within the geographical bounds of the current Netherlands, the Kingdom of the Netherlands and former colonies. - The actions and perspectives of Dutch people, including the time before ‘the Netherlands’ existed (e.g. Erasmus.). - The actions and perspectives of people from other countries who played an important role in Dutch history (e.g. Justus Lipsius). - The involvement and influence of the Netherlands, Dutch people or Dutch culture on developments and events abroad. The DBNG was originally made available via a website, which ceased to exist in 2021. This dataset contains the underlying data on titles, persons and relationships between titles that was presented on the website.
column34: The publications deal with prehistory up until the 20th Century. The included publications were published between 1863 and 2014. When updates ceased in 2014, the bibliography was up to date for books, but was 3-4 years behind for other types of publications. Of the titles contained in the paper bibliography from between 1863 and 1940, only the most important were included in the digital version. ** for dates, here below in 2.2, use the dates that we work on the downloadable digital version **

column87: The title files of the DBNG contain the following information on the publication titles: title, author, subject, date and location. The bibliography was compiled from a number of existing sources (see Section 3.8) and was regularly added to by the DBNG team, at the rate of approximately 3000-3500 publications per year. Most Dutch publishers deposit a copy of their publications at the National Library of the Netherlands (Koninklijke Bibliotheek, KB). The DBNG team checked these publications and selected those that met the agreed criteria to add to the DBNG. In addition, the team checked a list of relevant periodicals and incidentally checked other journals. Consideration was paid to geographical coverage by including publications in the list that focus on the history of a particular region or city. Publications were also included that focused on a specific aspect of Dutch history, such as cartography. The team also examined the publication lists (called Metis lists) of historians (and related disciplines) employed by Dutch universities. Via the website, interested parties were encouraged to suggest publications, which were also checked against the criteria. The following criteria were used: - Fulfils generally accepted academic standards. - Proper citation of sources. - Embeds the subject in its historical context. - Minimum five pages of text or ca. 2000 words. - Can be consulted at a public institution. Further, there are specific criteria for certain types of publications: - Publications relating to methodology are only included if they relate to Dutch historical practices or include enough examples from Dutch history. - Publications from related domains such as archaeology are only included if written from a historical perspective. - Subdisciplines of history such as heraldry are only included if they have a broad historical context. - Publications focused on local or anecdotal history, without a broader context, are not included. - Primary sources are only included if they are edited or published for historical research. - Published interviews are included if they were conducted on the basis of transparently described research methods and include a historical context. - New editions of previous publications are not included if the content is unchanged. - Articles in compilations are included individually if the contributions are written by different authors and are clearly recognisable as different contributions. - Articles in reference works such as encyclopaedias are not individually included, the reference work is included as a whole. - Discussions or reviews of publications are only included if they are independent contributions to a broader research context. - Articles in other languages must have been transliterated to be considered for inclusion. These criteria were set up by Sebastiaan Derks, Marijcke Schillings and Ronald Snijders. Descriptions of articles from collections or magazines were created by the DBNG team. Descriptions of books could usually be obtained from external sources. The titles and their metadata were stored in OCLC/GGC, in the PICA plus format. After the DBNG website ceased operation, the files were exported and stored in the Huygens eDepot, from where they are made available in this dataset. (Note: the titles also remain available in OCLC for members of OCLC. They can be identified via the library number of Huygens, 778)|Name, roles, date and place of birth for persons featured in the digital bibliography. **where did these come from?**|**where did these come from?**

column113: The DBNG contains information from a number of different sources. As merging the sources was done automatically, for a large part, there may be some duplicates. The list of publications was reduced over time due to lack of resources.
column128: The DBNG only contains those publications that were found via the team's work process or were reported to them, and which met the specified criteria (see Section 3.1). Due to limitations of personnel and time it was impossible to include all relevant material. Priority was given to a broad spread over time, and to leading, less accessible or supra-local journals.
column140: Repertorium Geschiedenis Nederland|Short-Title Catalogue, Netherlands (STCN)|Bibliopolis|Historie in Titels (HinT)|Apparaat Nederlandse Geschiedenis|Bibliografie Nederlands Centrum voor Rechtshistorische Documentatie en Rechtsiconografie (NCRD)
column142: The national historical bibliography of the Netherlands began in 1863 and continued under various names up until 1995, when it was named the Repertorium Gescheidenis Nederland. A subsidy from the Dutch Research Council (NWO) made it possible to retrodigitise the whole paper bibliography from 1940 and later, and the most important titles from before 1940. It also enabled the conversion of the digital file containing the titles that had been input between 1987 and 1995. The digital Repertorium was made available via PICA at the Centrum voor Bibliotheekautomatisering (Centre for Library Automation) in Leiden. From autumn 1996 onwards the titles were directly input into PICA using the ‘Gemeenschappelijke Geautomatiseerde Catalogiseersysteem (GGC)’ (Common Automated Cataloguing System). For input, a basic thesaurus of keywords was used. The Repertorium was replaced by the DBNG and its titles were transferred to the DBNG.|STCN is the retrospective national bibliography of the Netherlands for the period 1540-1800. It contains descriptions of all books that appeared in the Netherlands or that were written in Dutch in that period.|Bibliopolis is the electronic national history of printed books in the Netherlands.|A bibliography of publications about West-European history from ca.1500 to 2004.|Apparaat Nederlandse Geschiedenis is a guide to the most important academic literature on the subject of the history of the Netherlands.|This is a database of 55,000 titles about Dutch law, Romain law, canonical law and other European laws in so far as these were influential for Dutch, Suriname, South African, Antillean and Indonesian law.

column152: The printed portions of the Repertorium were digitised. Other parts of the dataset were imported from digital sources or added digitally.
column157: The aim of the DBNG was to give students, professional researchers and those interested in history an overview of the annual historical publications that was as complete and balanced as possible. After the website was taken down because many people switched to searching via online catalogues, many requests were made for the information. This led to the files being published in the Huygens eDepot. While the bibliography took longer to compile than online catalogues (and therefore became less competitive compared with digital pages of contents , library catalogues etc.) it also had a higher reliability. There is an important difference between a library catalogue, that reflects the purchase policy of a library, and a domain bibliography that offers a structured, content-based and balanced registration of the publication stream. The bibliography remains a key source of information for the history of academia. The bibliography is the only systematic access to material from collections that did not appear online.

column2: Indonesian Digest Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Wordende Wereld Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Indonesian Digest Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: Indonesian Digest ('Wordende Wereld') was a weekly cinema news film about the then Dutch colony of the Dutch East Indies (now Indonesia), intended primarily for a Dutch audience but also shown in cinemas in the colony itself. It was part of the Dutch propaganda strategy, intended to show how good the Netherlands was for the colony during the struggle for Indonesian independence. An average news program lasted approximately ten minutes and discussed 3-4 subjects. Subjects included traditional crafts and culture (which dominated), politics, the military, economy and health. More than 150 editions were produced, but only a handful of Dutch cinemas showed them. Indonesian Digest ceased production in 1949 when sovereignty was handed over to Indonesia. The dataset consists of the films and the descriptive metadata. Access varies for films and metadata so check the Access section. Standard access is via portals. Download of metadata, e.g. for computational analysis, requires special arrangements with NISV. Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy of the media may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis.

column67: The ‘Indonesian Digest’ was a weekly cinema newsreel from the governmental film company ‘Multifilm Batavia’. It was a propaganda tool for the Dutch government, in particular to highlight development in the Dutch East Indies, as a reaction to the independence movement and police actions. The films originate from the period 1946-1949.

column87: These are the digitised films of Indonesian Digest. They are available to browse online via the restricted access portals. See Section 2.4 for a list of portals. Initially the footage was sent to the Netherlands where it was edited and used in the Polygoon cinema news. Towards the end of 1947 the format was changed to fully edited stand-alone news programs, which were produced weekly. The first of these was submitted to the Filmkeuring (film rating board) in November 1947. The reels were sent back to Indonesia in 1953 at the request of the Indonesian government. Prior to being sent, a selection was copied by the Rijksvoorlichtingsdienst (RVD). In the 1980s the originals were sent once more to the Netherlands to be transferred from nitrate film to acetate film. The RVD kept the films, as the National Archive in Indonesia preferred to receive copies on VHS. The entire audiovisual archive of the RVD is now part of the NISV collection. The films were originally brought out in Dutch, English and Malay. The versions in this dataset are all in Dutch.|The original films had little accompanying information. Descriptions were created by RVD employees and Dutch veterans who had served in the colony, at the request of the RVD. This metadata was edited and enriched by NISV employees. In addition, the metadata of some programs was further enriched as part of the Programma Indisch Erfgoed Digitaal (Program Digital Indonesian Heritage, PIED), in order to support the 'Ons Land' (Our Land) website https://www.onsland.nl. An important source of information when describing the films was the thesis by Gerda Jansen Hendriks, linked in section 4.1. The Indonesian Digest collection uses the general Sound & Vision metadata model, described in the data-envelope ***link***. See this data-envelope for information about the general metadata fields available and about significant changes to metadata models and systems over time. The metadata is available in the open access and restricted access portals listed in Section 2.4.
