column2: Correspondence of Willem III and Hans Willem Bentinck (1656-1702) Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Correspondentie van Willem III en van Hans Willem Bentinck (1656-1702) Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Correspondence of Willem III and Hans Willem Bentinck (1656-1702) Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column16: Correspondence of Willem III and Hans Willem Bentinck (1656-1702)
column18: The ‘Correspondence of Willem III and Hans Willem Bentinck’ dataset makes digitally available part of the correspondence between Willem III and Hans Willem Bentinck. This correspondence casts light on the relations between the houses Nassau and Orange and contains a great deal of information on the political history of the Dutch Republic and its relationship with other countries. Willem III, Prince of Orange, was stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, Utrecht, Gelderland and Overijssel, and the King of England from 1689 until his death. Hans Willem Bentinck was a general and diplomat who became a trusted advisor of Willem III, filling many high offices in the Netherlands and acting as an unofficial first minister to Willem III when he was king. Both maintained a wide correspondence, including with each other. The correspondence covers many topics from important military and political matters to hunting adventures and gossip. Due to their close friendship, the letters between Bentinck and Willem III are very open and frank, providing a unique insight into the character of Willem III. Nicolas Japikse compiled five volumes of letters from the Bentinck Archive at Welbeck Abbey and many other archives (listed in the volumes), and annotated the letters with brief notes about persons, events and place names. Only the letters that are of importance to Dutch and European history, or that provide insight into the persons Willem III and Bentinck, are included. The letters are grouped thematically, not chronologically. The volumes published by Japikse were scanned and made available as a digital edition (see the link in Section 2.4). The digital edition makes the letters searchable and browsable. In addition to the annotated letters, the edition contains an introduction to each volume and an index of person names. The appendix contains a list of letters that were not included, correspondence from Bentinck after the death of Willem III, and various documents about the subjects discussed in the letters. The pages can be viewed as image, pdf or OCR (the recognised text). Individual pages can be downloaded. The dataset is also available to download, see Section 3 for more details. The copyright to the texts is owned by Huygens ING.

column113: The preserved correspondence is not complete, in fact the largest part is missing. Of the preserved letters, only the letters that are of importance to Dutch and European history, or that provide insight into the persons Willem III and Bentinck, are included in this dataset. The excluded letters are listed in the volumes. The text of the volumes is not 100% searchable and contains OCR errors. When searching the text, users should keep in mind that the spelling varies greatly from modern spelling. Also, people are often referred to by their title or the name of their estate. These variants are listed in the index of person names. Willem III's French is, as Japikse put it, 'very puzzling'. The letters featured in the first section do not follow any chronological order and the selection criteria used in the second section are at times ambiguous. Consequently, the way in which the material has been arranged is not always clear.

column2: Correspondence of Constantijn Huygens 1608-1687 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Briefwisseling van Constantijn Huygens 1608-1687 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Correspondence of Constantijn Huygens 1608-1687 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The dataset ‘Correspondence of Constantijn Huygens’ makes digitally available the known correspondence of Constantijn Huygens, consisting of more than 9,000 letters written between 1607 and 1687. Constantijn Huygens was a prominent figure in the Netherlands in the seventeenth century. He was secretary to stadtholder Frederik Hendrik and subsequently to William II. Additionally, Huygens was counsellor and accountant to the Oranges, assisting them in managing their goods. Huygens was also part of the cultural elite. He was a poet, musician, architect and art connoisseur. His correspondence sheds light on all these aspects of his life and includes important political issues, influential people and cultural topics. The dataset is based on a book by J.A. Worp from 1911, in which he published Huygens’ correspondence as known at that time. Almost all the letters from Worp’s book in the dataset are linked back to their representation in the digitised version of the book. The book contained the text of circa 7000 letters (either completely or partially). Worp included many other letters only in a footnote, describing them as unimportant or not interesting. The printed letters and the letters referenced in footnotes have all been digitised and can be identified by the letter number followed by a letter a, b, c, etc. New letters have been added. Letters written by Huygens on behalf of the stadtholder are not included in this dataset. Images of the letters (in their original language) are available, with a short description of the letter. For some letters transcripts and translations have been added. Information is still being added so the details of some letters are not yet complete. The dataset can be searched online on date, location, correspondent, archival location, language and form of the letter. These details were manually noted by researchers. If the letter was printed in Worp’s book or a transcript has been made, then the text can be searched for specific words. A list of correspondents, created from the registers in Worp’s book and annotations of the book, is also available online. Overviews of the metadata of the letters can be downloaded as CSV. See the link in Section 2.4 for the online version. The dataset can be downloaded, see Section 3 for more details.

column2: Reform of the ground lease system in Amsterdam
column6: Vernieuwing Erfpachtstelsel Amsterdam|Reform of the ground lease system in Amsterdam
column16: Reform of the ground lease system in Amsterdam
column18: The ‘Reform of the ground lease system in Amsterdam’ dataset makes digitally available more than 16,000 documents related to the reform of the ground lease system in Amsterdam. The reforms came into effect in 2016. A large number of Freedom of Information (Wet Openbaarheid Bestuur, WOB) requests were received on this topic, so the City of Amsterdam decided to publish, where possible, all relevant documents related to the decision-making regarding the reform of the ground lease system. For publication and permanent storage, the documents were transferred to the City Archives of Amsterdam. The dataset contains (draft) minutes, (draft) decisions, reports, calculations, emails, invoices, agendas, tables of contents and other documents. Documents may not be published due to considerations of privacy, financial interests of the council, disproportionate disadvantage/judgement or internal consultation of personal views. The dataset is available to browse and download online, see Section 3 for more details.

column87: The published documents relating to the ground lease reform are available online. A special team of civil servants was set up to collect the initial pool of documents (ca. 40,000), judge if they could be published, categorise them and remove duplicates. Documents that were already published elsewhere were not published again. Personal details of civil servants or third parties are blacked out in the documents. More than 16,000 documents are published via the City Archives of Amsterdam, organised into seventeen folders: - 1 00. Organisatie en overleg (organisation and discussion). - 2 01. Bestuurlijk (administrative). - 3 02. Juridische effecten (legal consequences). - 4 03. Financiële & fiscale effecten (financial and fiscal consequences). - 5 04. Communicatie (communication). - 6 05. Planning (planning). - 7 06. Werkgroepen (workgroups). - 8 07. Dwarskijksessies (reflection with independent experts). - 9 08. Risicoanalyse (risk analysis). - 10 09. Expertsessies en consultatie (expert sessions and consultation). - 11 10. Persoonlijke mappen (personal folders). - 12 11. Inspraak (public participation). - 13 12. Emailmap A (email folder A) - 14 13. Emailmap B (email folder B) - 15 14. Emailmap C (email folder C) - 16 15. Emailmap D (email folder D) - 17 16. Emailmap E (email folder E) Each folder contains an 'inventarislijst' (inventory) of the documents in that folder. This includes information on whether the documents are already published elsewhere, published in this dataset, partially published or withheld, and on the grounds of which legal clause information was withheld. The documents can be searched via the Open Amsterdam portal (see Path). Folders, subfolders and individual documents can be downloaded. An overview of the folder structure and the documents is present on the City Archives of Amsterdam inventory page (see Path). This can be clicked open to browse the individual documents. A PDF download can be requested per document or subfolder. The documents can also be searched via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam (filter on the VES archive). Documents, subfolders and folders can be downloaded from the search results. Both the City Archives portal and the Open Amsterdam portal require an email address to be entered to download information. This is deleted after the email with the download link is sent. It is not possible to download the entire dataset in one go. The metadata of the documents (described in Section 3.2) can be viewed on both portals but not downloaded.

Many Freedom of Information requests under the previous Freedom of Information law (Wet Openbaarheid Bestuur, WOB) were received relating to the ground lease reform. Therefore, even though the WOO law had not yet been introduced at that point, the City of Amsterdam chose to actively publish this dataset.

column6: Mi Oso Es Mi Kas Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|My House is My Home Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The ‘My House is My Home’ dataset makes digitally available the archived documents of the foundation ‘Mi Oso Es Mi Kas’, which offered support to girls, young women and teenage mothers from the Surinamese and Antillean communities in Amsterdam. ‘Mi Oso Es Mi Kas’ (‘My House is My Home’ in Surinamese and Papiamento) was founded on 27th August 1986 by a group of women, girls and young mothers under the leadership of Elfriede Sinester. It maintained a centre in the neighbourhood Amsterdam Zuid-Oost for girls and women between the ages of 12 and 27 (later lowered to 23), with the aim of encouraging contact between them and providing recreation, support, help, direction and guidance. Girls could get professional help in the areas of pregnancy, abortion, parenting and relationships, and in their personal development for education and work, for example by following courses. From 1999 onwards the foundation also produced radio broadcasts. It created the Meidenfestival (Girls Festival) in 1990, to give more attention to girls, which moved to its own foundation in 1994. Mi Oso Es Mi Kas was an independent foundation that received no structural subsidy. It closed its doors in 2007 due to a lack of funds.

column18: The ‘At Home in Amsterdam’ dataset comprises the photo album and Instagram account created for the ‘Thuis in Amsterdam’ (At Home in Amsterdam) project, in which young creative makers with Asian roots were interviewed about their feelings of being at home in Amsterdam. Project manager Ayesha Standar used the adam.archief Instagram account to approach young creative Amsterdammers. Eleven makers with Indonesian, Javan or Moluccan roots joined in to search for their connection to Amsterdam. Each gave form to their memories and feelings in their own way: stories, poetry, music, photos and videos. The results were compiled in a paper and digital photo album. The photo album is now stored in the City Archives of Amsterdam (see link in Section 2.4), and the Instagram account will also be archived there. At present, it is unfortunately not possible to view the photo album online. The paper version can be viewed by appointment in the reading room of the City Archives of Amsterdam. See Section 2.5 for how to arrange access.

column6: DJ-sets van de Amsterdamse club 'De School'|DJ sets from the Amsterdam nightclub 'The School'
column16: DJ sets from the nightclub 'The School'
column18: The ‘DJ sets from the nightclub 'The School'’ dataset contains 837 DJ sets that were performed at the Amsterdam nightclub ’The School’. ‘The School’ was a club in the Amsterdam Nieuw-West neighbourhood, housed in the former Christelijke Technische School (Christian Technical School). Although always intended as a temporary venue, ‘The School’ existed for eight years, from 2016 to 2024. Both national and international artists performed there, and acts ranged from up-and-coming new talent to established performers. The music in the dataset illustrates the evolution of (electronic) music and Amsterdam club culture during this period, including both local developments and global influences. The music may be freely listened to online but may not be downloaded or copied due to copyright.

column16: COVID-19 archive of the Free University Amsterdam
column18: The ‘COVID-19 archive of the Free University Amsterdam’ dataset makes digitally available documents relating to the decision-making, policies and communication of the Free University of Amsterdam during the COVID-19 pandemic. The dataset contains documents from the board of directors, the faculties and service departments of the university and its corona crisis team. The documents include meeting reports, reports on protocols, testing facilities and other measures taken (and photos of their implementation), decision trees, planning for various scenarios and designs of communication materials such as posters, flyers and stickers for safe routes on the campus. Emails and chat messages of the crisis team are also included. The archive was donated to the City Archives of Amsterdam in 2023. It can be viewed online via the City Archives website, see Section 3 for details.

The hotspot is further divided into the folders: ‘1. Formeel vastgelegde stukken’ (Formal documents). column2: Project Office IJburg Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Projectbureau IJburg Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1|Project Office IJburg Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The dataset ‘Project Office IJburg’ consists of the archived documents of the project office that oversaw the IJburg development, a new neighbourhood of Amsterdam built by constructing artificial islands in the IJmeer lake. The aim of the project office was to conduct integral urban planning for IJburg by bundling the necessary disciplines from within the council and from external parties. Upon its creation in 1995 it had 35 employees. It included employees of the Dienst Ruimtelijke Ordening, (Urban Planning Services), the Ingenieursbureau Amsterdam (engineering firm), the Gemeentelijk Grondbedrijf (Municipal Land Management), the Stedelijke Woningdienst (Urban Housing Services) and the Projectmanagementbureau (Project Management Office). There were objections to the development on environmental grounds, so a referendum was held, in which a majority voted against the development. However, an insufficient number of votes was cast for the outcome to be upheld. Agreements were made to compensate for the building works by developing new nature reserves. Development occurred in two phases, and in November 2002 the first residents moved into homes built in the first phase. In 2013 the second phase was begun. However, Project Office IJburg ceased to exist when it merged with Projectbureau Oost in 2012. The archive of the project office contains many types of documents such as meeting minutes, plans, investigations and evaluations. Topics include island construction, home building, energy supplies, public spaces, public transport, infrastructure, water, nature and environment, and security, among others. The referendum is documented along with other information about public reaction such as newspaper cuttings, letters from residents and satisfaction surveys. The documents have not yet been digitised but the archive inventory can be searched and browsed, see Section 3 for details.
column34: The Project Office IJburg was officially created in 1995. However, the underlying concept of the IJburg development had existed for a lot longer, and so the archive includes documents from predecessors of the Project Office.

column87: The documents in the archive are not yet available online, however the folder structure and filenames can be viewed in the online inventory. The archive of the Project Office IJburg was brought into order by the Stedelijk Archiefbewerkingsteam (the Municipal Archive Processing team). It was handed over to the City Archives of Amsterdam in August 2022. The archive was reorganised on a geographical basis, per island. Documents that were duplicated in other archives were removed. The archive is organised into four folders: 1 Organisatie (Organisation) - documents relating to the organisation: work programmes, way of working, meetings, progress reports, finances and communication. 2 Taakuitvoering (Task execution) - documents relating to the planning and construction, organised into ‘Eilanden-overstijgend’ (relating to all islands), ‘IJburg 1e fase’ (the first phase), ‘IJburg 2e fase’ (the second phase) and ‘Afzonderlijke eilanden’ (Individual islands) for documents per island. 3 Voorlopers van projectbureau IJburg (Predecessors of Project Office IJburg) - documents from the project prior to the creation of the project office. 4 Gedeponeerde archieven (Deposited archives) - documents from related projects that the project office participated in. Two metres of the archive were destroyed. These are documented in the lists: “Lijst van voor vernietiging in aanmerking komende stukken in Gemeentearchieven” (Staatscourant, 20th December 1983) and "Selectielijst archiefbescheiden gemeentelijke en intergemeentelijke organen vanaf 1 januari 1996" (Staatscourant 20th December 2005, nr. 247). The archive now comprises 27 metres of documents. These have not yet been digitised. The folder structure and the names of the documents can be viewed on the inventory page of the City Archives of Amsterdam (see link in Path). Via the search interface of the City Archives it is possible to search the archive (filter on the Projectbureau IJburg archive) on words in the folder/filenames and dates of creation of the documents. Scans of the documents can be requested (scanning on demand) or an appointment can be made to view the paper originals in the reading room at the City Archives.

column18: The ‘Overview maps of Amsterdam’ dataset makes digitally available maps that give an overview of the entire city of Amsterdam, from the sixteenth century up until the present day. The collection includes topographical maps, expansion plans, city divisions and thematic maps. The collection gives a complete picture of the topographical development of the city. Of particular importance are maps of the third and fourth major expansions of Amsterdam in 1613 and 1662, the maps with plans for expansion by J.G. van Niftrik (1866), J. Kalff (1875) and C. van Eesteren (1935), and the famous map by Cornelis Anthonisz (1544). The thematic maps give a wide range of information about the city, from health to pollution, art, public transport, advertising, religion, tourism and many more. All of the maps can be searched online and many can be viewed online. See Section 3 for details.

column2: Diary of Willem de Clercq 1811-1844 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Diary of Willem de Clercq 1811-1844 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Dagboek van Willem de Clercq 1811-1844 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Diary of Willem de Clercq 1811-1844' dataset makes digitally available the scans and transcripts of the diary of Willem de Clercq. De Clercq worked in trade, first in his family firm, then as secretary and later director at the Nederlandsche Handel-Maatschappij. He was a poet and one of the leaders of the Dutch Réveil, a social Christian movement. His diary is unique in the Netherlands, not only for its length but also for the depth and breadth of the entries, which give insight into the social, cultural and political history of the first half of the nineteenth century. The diary contains many different elements, such as travelogues, lists of literature (that he read), notes about religious topics, important events and his memoirs. De Clercq described the events around him in detail, but also his personal state of mind and his faith, his most important source of inspiration. The end of the Batavian Republic and the birth of the Kingdom of the Netherlands are discussed, along with the revolution and separation of Belgium. Important people such as King Willem I and Isaac da Costa (poet, historian and leader of the Réveil) also feature. The diary is divided up into thirty sections, each corresponding to a time period (usually a year). Each section is divided into topics. An additional section before the start of the diary contains a childhood notebook of De Clercq. De Clercq wrote in French until 1813 when he switched to Dutch. Some passages are in other languages. The scanned images of the diary pages and transcripts of the text are available in the online edition, where the transcripts can be searched and downloaded, and additional contextual material is also available. See link in Section 2.4. The dataset can be downloaded, see Section 3 for details.

column198: The aim was for the transcription to be verbatim as far as possible. The transcription stayed true to the original text even when this contained errors. Where corrections or additions were necessary, for example for incorrect dates, these were placed between square brackets. Unreadable sections were indicated with '[onl.]'. Corrections by De Clercq while writing are not transcribed, but 'censored' sections are transcribed surrounded by square brackets and a minus '[- text]'. Minor changes were made in spelling and punctuation to assist readability. See https://resources.huygens.knaw.nl/dagboekdeclercq/tekstverantwoording for more details.
column201: Willem de Clercq came from a wealthy trading family. He worked in the family company, had a broad range of interests and was a keen observer. He became involved in the Réveil social and religious movement and became one of the leaders. He came to the conclusion in 1818 that a diary should not only be an objective representation of events but also a subjective report of the writer's own feelings, joys and sorrows.

column2: Royal Theatre Carré Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Royal Theatre Carré Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Koninklijk Theater Carré Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The dataset ‘Royal Theatre Carré’ makes available the archive of this iconic Amsterdam theatre. The dataset consists largely of correspondence with artists, suppliers and members of the public. It also contains diverse documents such as posters, contracts, floor plans, photo albums, scrapbooks and drawings. Circus Carré existed for many years prior to 1887. The archive begins around the time that the permanent Carré theatre was built in Amsterdam. The building was also suited to other sorts of performances, and gradually the circus performances made way for variety. After the Carré family sold the theatre in 1919 the emphasis shifted to operettas, revues and theatre productions, and boxing was introduced. In the years that followed there were many directors, including Alex Wunnink, who introduced global stars, and his son Karel Wunnink, who introduced musicals in the 1960s. After the building was threatened with demolition in 1968 (the archive contains many letters of support received by the theatre during this period) the city of Amsterdam first hired and later bought it. When the theatre reached its hundredth anniversary, it became the 'Royal' Theatre Carré. The theatre was closed for renovation in the 2000s and reopened in 2004, achieving financial independence in 2009. In 2012 it received its first female director, Madeleine van der Zwaan. All of the items can be searched online and many can be viewed online. See Section 3 for details.

column86: Royal Theatre Carré
column87: The items can be searched online (for words in the folder-/filename and on date) via the search interface of the City Archives of Amsterdam (select ‘30864, Archief van het Koninklijk Theater Carré’ as the archive). A list of all the documents in the dataset and their images (where available) can be viewed on the inventory page (see link in Path). The original paper archive has a length of approximately fifty metres. A small part relates to the operation and organisation of the theatre, but the largest part consists of dossiers about performances, both Carré productions and external productions, including correspondence with the artists involved. The performance dossiers are divided into three sections: dossiers from when Alex Wunnink was the director, dossiers from when Karel Wunnink was director, and general dossiers. There is some overlap between these three sections. Dossiers are organised alphabetically by artist name but also by production name. For practical reasons, posters are stored in their own folder. Audiovisual material was originally kept in separate folders but has been added to the performance dossiers where possible. From 1986 onwards, Carré productions fell under a separate foundation, the Stichting Carré Theaterproducties, and so the dossiers are stored under the name of the foundation. Besides the performance dossiers, there is a lot of additional material about performances, groups and artists, stored in separate folders. Photo albums were mostly created by Carré employees, although some came from fan groups. The items are divided into folders: Organisatie (Organisation): Statutes, policies, meeting notes, anniversary celebrations, correspondence, photos and recordings related to the directors, administration. Middelen (Resources): Accommodation, financing, PR, property, HR and archive (lists pf AV material stored at the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision) Exploitatie (Operation): Performance dossiers, including artists, correspondence, programmes, posters, photos and audio(visual) recordings. Operational documents such as contracts and permits. Documentatie (Documentation): Various publications, including news articles, TV broadcasts and scrapbooks. Stichting Carré Theaterproducties (Foundation Carré Theatre Productions): The documents of this legal entity set up for Carré's own productions. Includes the organisation (meeting notes, annual reports etc.), the productions (brochures, photos, recordings etc.), and educational activities. Stichting Vriendenkring Carré (Foundation Friends of Carré): Meetings, publications and other documents. Overige archiefvormers: directeuren en medewerkers van Carré in andere functies (Other archive creators: directors and employees of Carré in other positions). Foto-albums en documentatie afkomstig van Willy Walden ('Snap') (Photo-albums and documents from Willy Walden ('Snap'): Photo albums and photos of the revue performer Willy Walden. Many items can be viewed online, however some are unavailable due to copyright issues ('auteursrechtelijk beschermd'). Many have not yet been digitised, in which case you can request a scan or make an appointment to view the paper original in the reading room of the City Archives of Amsterdam. Note that digitisation cannot be requested for most of the performance dossiers due to copyright. Items that are viewable online can be downloaded. An email address is required for the download. This is deleted after the email with the download link is sent.

column2: Polygoon Dutch News Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Polygoon Dutch News Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Polygoon Hollands Nieuws/Neerlands Nieuws Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Polygoon Dutch News’ dataset makes digitally available Dutch cinema news films about Dutch topics. For decades, the weekly Polygoon news was the definitive cinema news in the Netherlands. The films give an iconic view of the Netherlands in the 20th century and have been declared UNESCO heritage. As the film archive was carefully preserved, fragments are often reused in TV programmes, films and on the radio. The Polygoon news is characterised by humour and a positive perspective, particularly in the post-war period. The films were intended to show important current events and technical innovations, but also to educate the public by pointing out where things were going wrong. The films show traditions, public institutions, modernisation and events such as the North Sea Flood of 1953 and the independence of Surinam in 1975, but also everyday items such as toy trends, vaccinations and language lessons. The films are regarded to be of cinematographic quality. Polygoon started producing news in 1922. In 1924 Polygoon began a silent twice-weekly news film under the name ‘Hollands Nieuws’. Four cameramen travelled around the Netherlands to film material. From 1932 onwards the news was produced weekly and with sound. In 1940, at the start of the German occupation, Polygoon was forced to work together with Profilti and the German company Tobis to produce news under the name ‘Tobis Nieuws’. In 1944, production ceased due to a shortage of electricity and the closure of most cinemas. After the war, Polygoon and Profilti merged, and the news changed its name to ‘Neerlands Nieuws’. When TV was introduced in the Netherlands in the 1950s, the Polygoon films became a welcome source for documentary makers. When reduced revenues threatened the closure of Polygoon in 1963, a public outcry led to government subsidies. Polygoon cinema news stopped in 1987 when it was overtaken by TV news. Many films are in the public domain and can be freely viewed and downloaded. Others are under copyright and are available via restricted access portals. See Section 3 for details.

column18: The ‘Polygoon World News’ dataset makes digitally available Dutch cinema news films about world news. Polygoon World News started after the Second World War. It was produced weekly using material obtained via an exchange program with news sources abroad such as Pathé-Journal, Gaumont Actualité and Eclair Journal in France, and Fox Movietone News from the US. The Polygoon World News was a symbol of the post-war Netherlands. When reduced revenues threatened the closure of Polygoon in 1963, a public outcry led to government subsidies. Polygoon cinema news stopped in 1987 when it was overtaken by TV news. Many films are in the public domain and can be freely viewed and downloaded. Others are under copyright and are available via restricted access portals. See Section 3 for details.

column6: Notary Archives of Amsterdam 1578-1915 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Notariële Archieven Amsterdam 1578-1915 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Notary Archives of Amsterdam 1578-1915' dataset makes available several million notary acts, created by 670 Amsterdam notaries. These offer a great deal of information about international and national trade, shipping, slavery, personal possessions and inheritances, warrants, etc. Acts can also document events of a more personal nature, such as hardships at sea, neighbourhood disputes, bar fights and parents acknowledging or disowning their child. The original paper copies of the acts occupy almost three and a half kilometres of shelf space. A large proportion (ca. 80% as of December 2025) are available as digital scans. The dataset consists of: - digital scans of the acts. - an index for searching a subset of the scans. - transcripts for searching a subset of the scans. See Section 3 for more details.

column87: The digital scans of the acts show the pages of the act, including seals and attached notes. About 80% of the acts have been scanned (as of December 2025). Acts that have been indexed can be searched via the City Archives of Amsterdam search page (select the filter ‘Akte’ and the Index ‘Notarieel Archief’) and clicking on the search results. Acts that have transcripts can be searched via the Transkribus page. In either case, the scans can be downloaded from the search result. See the resources 'Index of the Notary Archives of Amsterdam 1578-ca. 1811' and 'Transcripts of the Notary Archives of Amsterdam 1578-1915' for more details. All scans can be browsed and downloaded on the City Archives of Amsterdam website page, see the link in Path. On this page it is also possible to request scans for acts that have not yet been scanned, these are listed by name/number and date. In addition to the acts themselves, the scanned pages include paper indexes created by the notary offices. The acts are organised under the name of the notary. Within each notary they may be organised by type of act and/or chronological period. In addition to the acts per notary there are four special categories: - 732 STUKKEN WAARVAN NOG NIET DUIDELIJK IS WELKE NOTARIS HET BETREFT: these are acts for which it is not clear who the notary was. - 733 OVERBRENGING ARCHIEVEN NOTARISSEN NAAR MINISTERIE VAN JUSTITIE 734 Akten uit 5372 overgebracht vanuit het Rijksarchief in Noord-Holland: these are acts that were found in the provincial archive and returned to the City Archives of Amsterdam. - 735 Stukken waarvan het verband met het archief niet duidelijk is – these are documents for which it is unclear what their relationship is with the notary archive. For a description of the available metadata fields, see section 3.2 of the resource 'Index of the Notary Archives of Amsterdam 1578-ca. 1811'. See the link in Section 2.4 for links to publications that can assist in using the dataset.|The index contains selected key details from the acts, allowing them to be searched. Via the search page of the City Archives of Amsterdam, you can use this index to find acts based on persons, dates, locations etc. The acts have been indexed on: - Type of act. - Names of persons mentioned in the act. - Date of the act. - Notes (languages, subjects and organisation names). - Locations. The name of the notary is automatically included in the index for the acts belonging to that notary. The index contains acts that were indexed during a test project in 2015, and the current ‘Alle Amsterdamse Akten’ project, which started in 2016. Volunteers index the acts by hand, by reading the digital scans. About 40% of the scans have been indexed (as of December 2025). As more acts are indexed, they are added to the index. The project focuses on notaries who started work prior to 1811. As a result, the bulk of the indexed acts are between 1578 and 1811, although acts from later are present if the same notaries were still working or if these were indexed during the test project. The existing paper indexes were not used to create the digital index, however some of these index cards have digital scans and transcripts available as part of the notary archive. The index cannot currently be downloaded. See the link in Path for more information about using the index. The list of Data Fields in 3.2 gives more details about the information available in the index. The index metadata conforms to the City Archives of Amsterdam metadata model https://github.com/Stadsarchief-Amsterdam/blauwdruk|Transcripts have been created for a subset of the notary archive. A transcript allows the text of the act to be searched. About 40% of the scans have been transcribed (as of December 2025). More transcripts will be added in future. The transcripts can be used to search the notary archive (see link in Path) and viewed in search results. The transcript for a search result can be downloaded per page. In the project ‘Crowd leert Computer Lezen’ (Crowd teachs computer to read), 2018-2023, volunteers manually create transcripts for acts. These are used to train the HTR (handwritten text recognition) model of Transkribus, which is being run on the scans to automatically create transcripts. Both manually created transcripts and automatically created transcripts are included in the search engine. The model is frequently updated so the quality of the transcripts is variable. Note that the aim is to have sufficient quality to allow searching the acts, not to provide a digital version of the text in the act.

Where there are too many persons in the document, the participant is advised to choose the most important persons. Indexed acts are checked by a controller and, if necessary, by a domain expert. Full instructions can be read in https://velehanden.nl/img/uploads/projecten/amsterdam_notarieel_2/invoerinstructie.pdf|The annotators were instructed to stick as closely as possible to the characters on the page. column6: Tastes of the Past Version-2 Data-Envelope Version-1
column18: The 'Tastes of the Past' dataset makes digitally available the recipe corpus and research results of the 'Tastes of the Past' project, which developed an experimental methodology to reconstruct and evaluate historical tastes and to connect historical and contemporary taste evaluations. Reconstruction aims to gain greater understanding of tastes, not to reproduce exactly 'how it was' in historical times. The dataset contains the results of applying this methodology to research in which a taste panel assessed the taste of apple pie baked according to a historical recipe selected from a recipe corpus compiled by the project. The taste panel made use of taste descriptors extracted from the recipe corpus when describing how they experienced the taste of the apple pie. The data was used to publish an article, which is linked to in Section 4.1. The dataset consists of three parts: - The historical recipe corpus. - Results of the text analysis of the recipes for taste descriptors. - Results of the taste panel. These parts are described in more detail in Section 3.
column20: Graphs and tables used in the article were translated to English
column29: reconstruction research|recipes|historical tastes|History of Knowledge|Early Modern History
column34: The recipe books were selected from the early modern period as this is when printed cookbooks started to appear. After 1800 the close relationship between cooking and medicine ceased to exist.

column87: The digital texts of the recipe corpus (see Resource ‘Recipe book corpus’) were analysed for the presence of taste descriptions using a combination of close reading and AntConc, a standalone open software package for linguistic analysis of texts https://www.laurenceanthony.net/software/antconc/. There were small mistakes in the transcriptions, but these were negligible for AntConc, as the program allows wildcard search. Taste descriptions were found in what would now be categorised as medical or botanical texts. They were rarely found in what would now be categorised as culinary texts. These only indicated if a dish was considered good (‘goed’) and tasty (‘smakelijk’) or delicious (‘kostelijk’). Further textual analyses were carried out on these words. The data and graphs of these analyses are available in the folder AntConc_Research in the dataset in Zenodo, or via the link in Path.|A taste panel was recruited to assess the tastes of a dish cooked according to a historical recipe from the corpus. Based on the corpus analysis, a selection of dishes was made that were potentially consumed by people from all social classes. From this selection an apple pie recipe was chosen as it fitted the criteria for availability of historically accurate ingredients, time, expense, feasibility and reasonable familiarity to modern consumers. A questionnaire was developed to assess how the taste panel experienced the apple pie. This included general questions, such as what kind of dish they thought they had been given (before and after eating), and their opinions of the appearance, smell, taste, and texture of the apple pie. They were asked to describe the taste using multiple-choice and open questions, including a multiple-choice question allowing them to choose from the taste descriptions that were found in the recipe corpus. The session was held in Amsterdam in April 2024. Taste panel participants approved the use and disclosure of their answers. This resource contains the recipe and ingredients list used when baking the apple pie and wordclouds, graphs and tables produced from the questionnaire results, which were used in the article linked to in Section 4.1. A subfolder contains the questionnaire form and the answers that participants filled in, saved in various formats. The resource is available in the subfolder ‘TasteLab_data’ of the Zenodo dataset or via the link in Path.|The corpus of recipes was intentionally broad, including culinary texts but also botanical or medical texts, as the distinction between these categories was much less clear in the early modern period than in the present day. The corpus consisted of nineteen editions of printed seventeenth- and eighteenth-century Dutch recipe books and three transcribed seventeenth- and eighteenth-century Dutch manuscript recipe books. The recipes were processed by Transkribus (a program designed for HTR (Handwritten text recognition) but also used for OCR (optical character recognition) of printed texts) to generate text files suitable for analysis. The corpus is divided over three database files.

column157: This dataset was produced as part of experimental research to see how a taste panel and reconstructions of historical recipes could help to understand tastes of the past. column185: Tasting and assessing the apple pie
column189: The taste panel consisted of volunteers with a reasonable spread over age and gender. They were not questioned about their background. As they volunteered, they were self-selecting. The only criteria was that they should be able to read and write Dutch. For health and safety reasons, people were excluded who had food intolerances, allergies, autoimmune diseases, or who were pregnant. The test panel members were recruited via newsletters and social media. They were asked to sign a liability waiver and a data processing agreement.
column190: Participants received a small thank-you gift and a card with the apple pie recipe.
column201: Positionality as stated in the article "Vera Eising, Manon Henzen, Merit Hondelink, Judith Konsten & Marieke M. A. Hendriksen (12 Aug 2025): Tasting the Past? Developing a Methodology for Researching Historical Tastes, Global Food History, DOI: 10.1080/20549547.2025.2523728": "Vera Eising received her MA in history from the University of Amsterdam. In the 2023-2024 academic year, she was the junior researcher on the NWO-funded project “Tastes of the Past” at the Huygens Institute for Dutch History and Culture, part of the Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences (KNAW). Manon Henzen is a culinary historian who holds degrees in History and Middle East Studies. After a career in project management, she founded the company EetVerleden in 2011 to share her love for food and cooking history with a broad audience. EetVerleden offers (online) historical cooking courses, workshops, trips, lectures, and books. Merit Hondelink is a PhD candidate in Archaeobotany at the University of Groningen, where she is currently finishing her NWO-funded project, titled A taste of historic cookery: a reconstruction of the daily meal as prepared by common burghers of Early Modern Dutch cities, AD 1500-1850. Judith Konsten is an independent researcher and professional flavorist. Specialized in tasting coffee and wine, Judith is broadly interested in tasting as a research practice, and in connecting contemporary tasting methods to historical research. Marieke M.A. Hendriksen is senior researcher and head of the department of Knowledge and Art Practices at the Huygens Institute for Dutch History and Culture. She is the PI of the NWO-funded project “Tastes of the Past” (2023-2024) and of the European Research Council-funded project “PRESERVARE: Large-scale conservation of perishable foodstuffs in the Low Countries, 1600-1800” (2024-2029)."

column2: Diaries of Vice-Admiral F. Pinke, navy commander in the Dutch East Indies 1914-1916 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1
column6: Diaries of Vice-Admiral F. Pinke, navy commander in the Dutch East Indies 1914-1916 Version-1 Data-Envelope Version-1|Dagboeken van vice-admiraal F. Pinke, commandant zeemacht in Nederlands-Indië 1914-1916 Versie-1 Data-Envelop Versie-1
column18: The ‘Diaries of Vice-Admiral F. Pinke 1914-1916’ dataset makes digitally available the diary entries of Vice-Admiral Frederik Pinke, naval commander and head of the Marine department in the Dutch East Indies 1914-1916. During the First World War the Netherlands remained neutral, and Pinke was responsible for the maintenance of this neutrality in Indonesia. This included ensuring communications between the warring parties in the Indonesian region. It was feared that a breach of neutrality could lead to the Netherlands being forced to join the war. From the start of the First World War up until December 1916, when Pinke left his post as vice-admiral, he recorded diary entries about the situation and politics in Indonesia under Dutch colonial control, in particular regarding neutrality. Pinke wrote the entries retrospectively, initially reflecting on events after two weeks or a month, in later entries after a longer period. He described the period with reference to sent and received letters (which are included in the entries), which show how communication between high-ranking officials occurred. Neutrality was a big subject, as was news about the Dutch squadron of warships. His notes discuss negotiations with other naval contacts, the Dutch government and the consuls and governments of other countries. Pinke also describes shipping movements and secret reports. He gives his personal opinion but does not discuss personal matters. The dataset contains scans and OCR (Optical Character Recognition) text for volume 60 from the series ‘Rijks Geschiedkunstige Publicatiën’, which published the diary entries. The diary entries are completely transcribed, with Pinke’s marginal notes added between brackets at the place that was starred in the text. They contain footnotes with contextual information, obtained from various collections in the Dutch National Archives, the Dutch Foreign Office and the Documentation Centre for the History of Dutch Protestantism (VU Amsterdam). These sources are described further in the introduction to the diary entries, which also gives background information about the navy in the Dutch East Indies at that time. In addition, there are three appendices, with indices of names of persons, names of ships and geographical locations, and a map of the Indonesian archipelago. The dataset is available as a digital edition (see the links in Section 2.4). Users can read the text and search the OCR for specific words. The dataset is also available to download, see Section 3 for more details.

column18: The dataset ‘Active and passive Woo publications’ makes digitally available all documents published on the Open Amsterdam webportal by the City of Amsterdam, as prescribed by the Woo (Wet Open Overheid – Open Government Act). It consists of documents published after a Woo-request (passive publication) as well as documents selected for publication by the City of Amsterdam itself (active publication). Documents published after a Woo-request are further described in the dataset 'Wob/Woo Decisions Amsterdam'. Based on the Woo, institutions have to actively publish documents in various categories, ranging from documents used in political meetings to covenants, research reports, pieces of advices or documents used in civic engagement. The City of Amsterdam has chosen to immediately transfer such information to the City Archives of Amsterdam, from where it is published on both the City Archives page and the Open Amsterdam portal.
column52: City Archives of Amsterdam|City of Amsterdam
column80: New Woo documents are archived every week.
column86: Active and passive Woo publications
column87: The Wob(Wet Openbaarheid van Bestuur – Government Information (Public Access) Act) was the original Freedom of Information law in the Netherlands. It was replaced on 1st May 2022 by the Woo (Wet Open Overheid – Open Government Act). Woo-documents are published in various categories, on the Open Amsterdam portal you will find document in these categories: - Pieces of advice - Disposals - Administrative documents - Civic engagement - Covenants - Year plans and reports - Complaints - Research reports - Preventive frisking - Shell - ‘Spijtoptantenregeling’ (documents relating to the City of Amsterdam's decision to offer a second chance of applying for a permanent leasehold to tenants who had not applied when the reform was introduced) - Renewal leasehold system - Changes of Environmental Act - Wob and Woo dossiers. Some of these categories are prescribed by the Woo, some of them are based on issues chosen by the City of Amsterdam. For example, in the case of preventive frisking the city wanted to be more transparent about the decision making process behind a decision made by the mayor. The documents are mostly published in PDF format. The names of aldermen are publicly visible in the documents. The names, addresses, telephone numbers etc. of other persons, such as civil servants or citizens, are blanked out for privacy reasons. This is done by the municipal administration prior to transfer to the archive. The publications can be searched via the Open Amsterdam portal (see Path) and the City Archives of Amsterdam website. The user can search for words in the dossier description or the file names. The results can be filtered on dossier and/or date. On both websites an individual document or a whole dossier can be downloaded if the user enters their email address, which is deleted after the download link is sent. Note that downloading individual documents can lead to loss of context. It is not possible to download the entire dataset at once.

column155: Transparency of government decisions and activities
column157: The City of Amsterdam wants to be more transparent about its decision making. Documents that have been made public actively may be viewed by everyone. Anyone is entitled to request information from the City of Amsterdam. If access is granted, then these documents may also be viewed by everyone. Archiving both the passively and actively published documents in perpetuity and publishing them from the archive simultaneously answers the needs of long-term archival and of providing the general public with access to the published information.

column18: This dataset was developed as part of a Dutch HOSAN research program https://www.clicknl.nl/en/pps-projecten/hosan/ exploring the feasibility of utilising heritage datasets from the Netherlands to create speech models that represent all Dutch voices. The dataset contains a large quantity of Dutch audio data from Dutch television in the period 1972-2022, stored at the Netherlands Institute for Sound & Vision. The audio files add up to a total of 81k hours of audio, with most audio files having a length of 30 minutes to 1 hour. The selection was made from TV broadcasts (excluding international news) of specified genres. The dataset consists of the following parts: - the audio files (only available to listen to under login due to copyright) - metadata about the selected programs

column87: An initial selection was made of material from the period 1972-2022 that met the following criteria: - TV broadcasts excluding international news. - Radio broadcasts from the radio station NPO Radio 1. - Excluding music-related genres. - Broadcast program material only (no rushes etc.). - Program duration available in the metadata. - Digital carrier available. This initial selection contained approximately 184k hours of TV and 128k hours of radio. For training speech models, only the TV data was selected. The set was further reduced by selecting specific genres (see genres.txt file in the Resource 'Metadata of of NISV 81k Dutch TV Speech Dataset '), and by removing audio with a length longer than three hours. Only a single broadcast per day of any given series (e.g. one single edition of the Dutch public broadcaster's news program per day) was selected, as it was a requirement for training the speech models that the set contained as little duplication of audio fragments as possible. Low-resolution versions of the MXF carriers were downloaded, the audio (in AAC format) extracted and this dataset delivered to the researchers under secure conditions with strict non-disclosure agreements in place regarding both the data and the resulting models. Initial use of the data revealed that eighty-eight audio files contained a virtually flat audio signal. Investigation of a sample at Sound & Vision revealed that these came from videos for which the original analogue carriers contained no audio signal. The carrier IDs of these files are contained in the metadata, see Resource 'Metadata of NISV 81k Dutch TV Speech Dataset'. The audio files themselves are under copyright. They can be listened to via the restricted access portals of Sound & Vision by users with a login (see Section 2.5 for access information). They can be found by searching on their identifier (see Resource 'Metadata of NISV 81k Dutch TV Speech Dataset '). Depending on the rights situation of the individual recording a copy may be ordered from NISV for a fee. Reuse is subject to restrictions on a per-item basis. Note that audio files in the archive can be changed, so the versions available now are not necessarily identical to those delivered at the time the dataset was compiled.|The metadata describes the selected programs (see Resource 'Audio of NISV 81k Dutch TV Speech Dataset' for the selection process). The metadata contains the following files: genres.txt - a list of the genres selected (in Dutch). no_audio.txt - a list of the carrier identifiers without significant audio content. filtered_any_genre_cc0 - A dataframe containing the IDs of the programs and non-copyrighted metadata about the program such as title and broadcast date. See Section 3.2 for more details. More metadata on the programs is available via the restricted access portals of Sound & Vision, see Section 2.5 for access information.

column18: De afdelingen Dialectologie en Volkskunde hebben (ca. 5000) opnamen gemaakt van o.m. dialectsprekers en verhalenvertellers. Van de ingesproken teksten zijn transcripties gemaakt. De collectie is digitaal en bestaat uit scans van een selectie van de transcripties van de vrije gesprekken (soundbites), opgenomen in Nederland en Vlaanderen. Er is zowel een geanonimiseerde versie van de transcripties als een niet-geanonimiseerde. In totaal gaat het om meer dan 11.000 scans. De transcripties zijn beschikbaar als JPEG (geanonimiseerd) en als TIFF. De geanonimiseerde versie is web based raadpleegbaar (net als de geluidsbanden) via de website van het Meertens Instituut. In het kader van het KNAW-project Stimulering Open Access (2012) zijn de scans ook bij DANS opgeslagen (zowel geanonimeerd als niet geanonimiseerd). Voor de website, zie: http://www.meertens.knaw.nl/ndb/. column16: ZEFYS2025: A German Dataset for Named Entity Recognition and Entity Linking for Historical Newspapers
column18: Historical newspaper collectons were amongst the first materials to be scanned in order to preserve them for the future. To expand the ways in which specific types of information from digitised newspapers can be searched, explored and analysed, appropriate technologies need to be developed. Named entity recognition (NER) and entity linking (EL) are such information extraction techniques aiming at recognising, classifying, disambiguating and linking entities that carry a name, in particular proper names. However, large annotated datasets for historical newspapers are still rare. In order to enable the training of machine learning models capable of correctly identifying named entities and linking them to authority files such as, e.g., wikidata entities, we provide a corpus of 100 German-language newspaper pages published between 1837 and 1940. The machine learning task for which this dataset was collected falls into the domain of token classification and, more generally, of natural language processing.

column87: The 100 historical newspaper pages were selected from the newspaper information system of Berlin State Library ZEFYS (an abbreviation of ZEitungsinFormationssYStem) with the aim to provide a sufficiently large and homogeneously annotated dataset. For ZEFYS2025, pre-existing named entity datasets from two distinct projects – Europeana Newspapers and SoNAR were combined with additional newly annotated newspaper pages into a single coherent resource. Out of the 100 pages, 84 pages were produced automatically by OCR. No normalisation or modernisation of the OCR was performed; however, post-OCR correction was done for the named entities in the dataset in order to present them in a normalized, consistent form. Moreover, 16 of the pages were annotated on the basis of manually transcribed ground truth. The output of the OCR process performed for the provision of the newspapers in the ZEFYS system was transformed into .tsv format using a page2tsv tool (https://github.com/qurator-spk/page2tsv); tokenization and sentence splitting were performed using SoMajo (https://github.com/tsproisl/SoMaJo). For initial entity recognition and linking, the tool described in Labusch, K., & Neudecker, C. (2022). Entity Linking in Multilingual Newspapers and Classical Commentaries with BERT. Conference and Labs of the Evaluation Forum. 1079-1089. https://ceur-ws.org/Vol-3180/paper-85.pdf was used. The complete training dataset consists of 100 OCRed historical newspaper pages where the full text has been converted into .tsv format. The first column indicates token position and sentence splits, where the beginning of a new sentence is indicated with a 0. Tokens can be found in the second column with one token per row (tokens may be split across multiple rows due to OCR/segmentation errors). Two columns are used for named entity tags at two different annotation layers, the first one indicating the surface tag, while the latter is used for embedded entities. The column ID contains Wikidata QIDs, if available or otherwise NIL. Four columns are dedicated for the page image coordinates, if available. For the annotation of tokens, the IOB chunking scheme has been used. Each token has received one of the following labels: Inside, Outside or Beginning. Tokens that do not represent entities are tagged O (outside). B-ent (begin-of-entity) indicates that the token forms the beginning of an entity; I-ent (inside-of-entity) is used if the token is part of the entity but does not form its beginning. Three entity types are annotated: Locations (LOC), persons (PER), and organisations (ORG). Embedded entities were annotated in the same way. Entities which can be found on Wikidata are represented via their QID, which can be complemented with https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/ to receive the full link to the entity. If an entity could not be found on Wikidata (but it has been searched for), a NIL was inserted. The annotations in the .tsv files of the dataset conform to the ‘one word per line’ standard described in Tjong Kim Sang, E., & Meulder, F.D. (2003). Introduction to the CoNLL-2003 Shared Task: Language-Independent Named Entity Recognition. In Proceedings of the Seventh Conference on Natural Language Learning at HLT-NAACL 2003, 142–147. https://aclanthology.org/W03-0419.pdf The tags are encoded according to the IOB standard as described in Ramshaw, L. A., & Marcus, M. P. (1995). Text Chunking Using Transformation-Based Learning. In S. Armstrong, K. Church, P. Isabelle, S. Manzi, E. Tzoukermann, & D. Yarowsky (Hrsg.), Natural Language Processing Using Very Large Corpora (Vol. 11, 157–176). https://doi.org/10.1007/978-94-017-2390-9_10. column99: 100 historical newspaper pages. The whole dataset comprises 348,307 tokens. Altogether, 14,072 entities were annotated, namely 4,389 PER, 6,049 LOC and 3,223 ORG entities. To determine these absolute numbers, the B-tags were counted. The number of distinct entities amounts up to 8,000. The dataset comprises 10,341 links to Wikidata. This German-language dataset is characterised by the historical context of the temporal range from which it was collected; therefore, entities like "Berlin", "Paris", "London", or "Deutschland", "England", "Preußen" and "Rußland" can be found amongst the most frequently used LOC entities, "Beethoven", "Bismarck" and "Poincaré" amongst the PER entities and "Reichstag", "Reichsregierung" or the German political party "Zentrum" amongst the ORG entities.
column119: Human annotators added annotations and entity links or corrected the suggestions provided by the entity recognition and linking systems.
column128: As the 100 newspaper pages were more or less randomly selected from the digitised newspaper collections of Berlin State Library, the emphasis of this digital collection can be regarded as a bias: The focus is on newspapers published in Prussia. The historical worldviews and preferences reflected in such newspapers within the time span between 1837 and 1940 can clearly be understood as biases that reflect the role of Prussia as a great European power and core of the German Empire after 1870/71. However, given the long time frame, this dataset also reflects linguistic change as well as the change of preferences of what is newsworthy and what should therefore be reported in newspapers. This linguistic change and shift of focus is especially evident in the newspapers printed during the Weimar Republic. Though the newspapers were selected to represent a broad range of types and content, there is an emphasis on financial papers due to accordingly themed digitisation projects. 21 pages or one fifth of the whole corpus has been taken from the Berliner Börsen-Zeitung published between 1872 and 1930, and 17 pages have been taken from the Berliner Tageblatt und Handels-Zeitung published between 1877 and 1934; this emphasis on financial papers clearly forms a bias. DDR-Presse newspapers were not included due to rights issues.
column131: The dataset does not contain personal or sensitive information beyond what is available on Wikidata anyway. It does not contain any sensitive data in the sense of contemporary privacy laws.

For a part of the newspapers presented in ZEFYS, full texts are provided. The 100 newspaper pages which form the dataset therefore represent a selection of the selection of newspapers available in ZEFYS.
column157: The intention to provide a large corpus (> 100k tokens) in German language from historical sources including named entity tags as well as links to corresponding knowledge base entries (where applicable) for the purpose of historical NER/EL motivated the creation of this dataset. This dataset was established for the training of machine learning models capable of correctly identifying named entities and linking them to wikidata entries. The tasks of named entity recognition and entity linking are conceived to enable these information extraction techniques especially in historical newspapers. However, they might work well on other digital assets as well if they come from a comparable time span. The machine learning task for which this dataset was collected falls into the domain of token classification and, more generally, of natural language processing. In this dataset, persons, locations and organisations are identified and linked that have been mentioned in German-language newspapers published before 1940. Most probably, the social impact of the dataset is therefore very low. However, advancing information extraction techniques facilitates the creation of knowledge and furthers research as well as the discovery of new sources.

column185: Text annotation
column189: Ulrike Förstel, Jörg Lehmann, Clemens Neudecker, Sophie Schneider, Markus Bierkoch. During the time when the annotations took place, all annotators were employed at Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. Socio-demographic information on the annotators is not available, but see the information provided in section 5.2.
column198: The neat (named entity annotation tool) tool developed by one of the project collaborators was used. Three entity types (persons, locations, organisations) were annotated. Annotating nested named entities was allowed with a limit of depth one, but nested named entities are not considered in the entity linking annotations. Annotations mainly followed the annotation guidelines developed for the Impresso project, see Ehrmann, M., Watter, C., Romanello, M., Clematide, S., & Flückiger, A. (2020). Impresso Named Entity Annotation Guidelines. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3585749. Using the annotation tool, all automatically generated annotations have been intellectually checked and revised by a group of German native speakers. This included the verification of the NE tags assigned previously as well as the addition or deletion of tags if needed. For NEs confirmed in this step, existing links were checked for the most precise and correct option for linking, and missing links to entities were inserted if available. To flag ambiguous or uncertain cases arising during annotation for later discussion, an extra NE-TAG class TODO was used in this process. Consensus about challenging cases collected throughout the annotation was reached in regular discussion meetings, and the set of instructions was expanded iteratively, adding further rules or examples when deemed necessary. Since the revision was carried out by one expert per page only, it was not possible to calculate inter-annotator agreement or similar measures to assess consistency between multiple annotators. Instead, we employed computational methods to assist in localizing and reducing inconsistencies within extensively annotated datasets. The automated analyses identified reappearing tokens throughout our dataset with a high divergence in how they were transcribed, tagged or linked. In additional correction loops, these inconsistencies were systematically reviewed once again by the annotators.

column18: The dataset is a result of the project "Digitization of 19th Century Children's and Youth Literature" conducted at the Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library between 2021 and 2025, which is part of the collaborative project "Colibri – Corpus Libri et Liberi". Within this project, Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library has digitized about 5,600 historical children's books and youth literature, from which the illustrations were extracted using a machine learning model. This data publication comprises 53,533 illustrations extracted from 3,412 children's and youth books published between 1800 and 1925, accompanied by metadata and annotations and example images for each annotated class. The scope of this dataset is limited to works printed in Germany during the 19th century and further German-language countries.
column20: The language of the children's and youth books is usually German (ger). A minority of the collection is in French (fre), English (eng), or Low German (nds). However, this applies only to a few dozen books. If the digitised works contain languages besides German, this is documented as well in the .csv file, e.g., German may be accompanied by French (fre), Latin (lat), Italian (ita), English (eng), Dutch (dut), Spanish (spa), etc. Languages are specified in the accompanying .csv file in ISO 638-2/B format. However, since the images mostly contain visual elements, the indication of language is in this case secondary.
column34: with a clear emphasis on works published in the middle of the 19th century. Publication peaks were formed by the years 1830, 1850, 1860 and 1890.
column36: Publication places are in the vast majority of cases located in Germany; occasionally works have been printed outside Germany, e.g. in Vienna, Prague, Zurich, etc.

column87: This data publication builds on top of the collection of historical German children's and youth books digitised within the project "Colibri". Preprocessing and cleaning of the dataset was performed in a series of steps. The first step consisted in collecting all images of digitised works in the collection of historical children's books "Colibri", which can be found in the digitised collections of Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. In the next step, illustrations and other visual elements were extracted from this corpus of images using the image extraction model developed by Berlin State Library within the Eynollah suite. This resulted in 90,688 images contained in 3,412 digitised works. This corpus was further reduced by adding annotations to the images, thus establishing a classification system and training a classifier, and finally excluding images that were sorted into classes that seemed not to be of interest, namely Übriges (Other), Gesetzter_Text (printed text), Einband (binding), Stempel (stamp), Farbtafel (table of colours), and Signature (shelf mark). Only images that had received the classifications Abbildung (illustration), Ornament (ornament), Vignette (vignette), Noten (musical notation), Initiale (capital letter), Exlibris (bookplate), Druckermarke (printers' mark), Karte (map), or Photo (photography) were kept, thus reducing the dataset to 53,533 images. The image dataset consists of a single compressed zip file with directories which are named using the unique identifier PPN of each digitised item ("ColibriImagesDataset.zip"). MD5 and SHA256 hashes of the file ColibriImagesDataset.zip: MD5: 4909c7981a5f640e24feb16c6647c1c5 SHA256: a3b9307d079684077e2525845b7b4422cf960b22a93fe77e10daed6b6dc529da|A .csv file containing metadata and annotations for each image file is provided "ColibriImagesMetadataAnnotations.csv"). MD5 and SHA256 hashes of the file ColibriImagesMetadataAnnotations.csv: MD5: d0b9a1c63708adfe5c96bc2121f2740d. SHA256: 9f8373e7107ad4ed698903bd5a9044fe7c2e169ac7557e15ccac384a9be0493f. Annotations were added with the aim of training a classifier which can be used on other digital collections as well. The file contains the PPN of each individual digitised work. The metadata for each image have been extracted from METS/MODS files describing the contents and structure of a digital object published in the digitised collections. Furthermore, each of the 53,533 image files is listed with its filename, followed by two more columns which result from the annotation and extraction process by which the image files were filtered (see below). The column "groundtruth" contains labels added manually according to a classification scheme, the column "classification" contains labels added automatically using the same scheme, resulting from a multiclass classifier applied to the image dataset. The metadata published as part of this data publication follow the METS / MODS standards. Full metadata for each individual work can be retrieved using the OAI-PMH interface provided at https://oai.sbb.berlin/. The metadata are a collaborative product of the people performing scanning and of trained librarians. The annotations were not part of the initial data collection; however, they are being published alongside the metadata describing the digitised works from which the image files were extracted; depending on the use made of this dataset, metadata may as well be added as labels to the images. The metadata provided contain classifications according to the DDC notation system and genre fields according to the MARC Genre Term List. The classifications were performed by the librarians who created the bibliographic record.|A .pdf file with example images for each annotated class has been added to the data publication ("ColibriExamplesForTheImagesInTheDataset.pdf").

The PPNs of the individual works form the name of the folder in which the images extracted from the work are contained; the images themselves have received individual names within each folder.
column119: Ground truth annotations were provided by members of the research project "Human.Machine.Culture" or by trained librarians participating in a workshop. On this basis, a classifier was trained and applied to the full dataset. The results of the classification were then evaluated, further annotations were added to broaden the groundtruth and the classifier was retrained. This process was repeated until the classifier reached an accuracy of 90% or more for each class. This process resulted in 33,397 manually annotated images or 37% of the 90,668 images contained in the full dataset.
column125: The dataset contains more than 40,000 illustrations extracted from digitised children's and youth books during the 19th century. As such, the illustrations reflect the pedagogical standards of the time as well as the worldview which was typical for the target group of these publications, the German bourgeois readership. At this time, corporal punishment was accepted as a means of education, and such depictions of violence can be found amongst the illustrations. Furthermore, the illustrations bear testimony to the biologistic thinking and dismissive consideration of specific population groups which has to be regarded as typical for the last third of the 19th and early 20th century. In the illustrations, it happens that people coming from non-European continents are depicted in a stereotypical and racist manner, and especially inhabitants of countries located in Africa are presented from a colonial point of view.
column128: The dataset contains more than 40,000 illustrations extracted from digitised children's and youth books during the 19th century.
