column131: The dataset fully complies with the European General Data Protection Regulation GDPR|The dataset does not contain personal or sensitive information beyond what is available in international authority files anyway. Since the youngest titles contained in this dataset were printed in the early 20th century, the dataset does not contain any sensitive data in the sense of contemporary privacy laws.
column140: "Colibri" collection
column142: All data were obtained from the collection of the department of children's and youth books of the Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. The historical books were digitised as part of the cooperative project "Colibri", funded by the German Research Foundation. The segment of children's and young adult literature selected for the "Colibri" project ties in with the digitization strategy for 18th century prints extensively funded by the German Research Foundation (DFG) as part of the Directory of 18th century prints published in the German-speaking world (VD 18) and at the same time closes the gap to the children's and young adult books digitized in the Europeana Collections 1914-1918 project. In addition, the 1,600 non-fiction books for children and young people from the fields of science and technology digitized by the Staatsbibliothek in a 2013 project (WegehauptDigital) funded by the Federal Government Commissioner for Culture and the Media (BKM) complement the collection. The selection process for "Colibri" was guided by both the demands of the relevant research community and the existing collections of children's and young adult literature in the participating libraries. In this context, both the Berlin State Library and the International Youth Library in Munich stepped in with their extensive holdings to round out the picture and represent the diverse production of children's and young adult books from the long 19th century as comprehensively as possible. The total of 15,000 bibliographic units was made possible by contributions from the University Library of Bielefeld (1,000 titles), the University Library of Braunschweig (4,000 titles), the International Youth Library of Munich (5,000 titles) and the Berlin State Library (5,000 titles). The aim was to create a representative sample out of the total population of all children's and young adult literature titles of the period under consideration, which can now be examined quantitatively and qualitatively, numerically and hermeneutically. This dataset exclusively contains illustrations from the digital copies of the Berlin State Library. The provenance of the image files is the internal file storage system of Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. Alternatively, the images could have been retrieved via the IIIF interface, the use of which is described here. Metadata have been retrieved from the OAI-PMH interface provided at https://oai.sbb.berlin/. All the digitised works from which the images published in this dataset were extracted are available under a Public Domain Mark 1.0 licence.
column152: The motivation for digitisation resulted from the participation of the Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library in the collaborative research project "Colibri". The latter project performed a selection of works from the comprehensive collection of children's books and youth literature held at the library; this collection has to be seen as one of the major and most prominent of its kind in Europe. The works digitised within this research project form but a small part of the entire collection of children's books and youth literature at Berlin State Library, which today comprises about 200,000 volumes (roughly 56,500 of which are dating back to the times between 1507 and 1945).
column157: This data publication was created with the intent to provide a single large annotated computer vision dataset for research purposes and the development of AI applications. The primary intention for this data publication was the provision of a large computationally amenable dataset exclusively consisting of historical image files containing illustrations of digitised books to stimulate research and development of AI applications. In 2025, large (meta-)datasets from the field of historical cultural data are still missing. In this respect, the data publication provided here aims to fill a gap. The study of visual elements in historical printed books is still a desideratum which may be tackled by collaborations between the digital humanities and the machine learning communities, especially when it comes to the analysis of large amounts of data: Illustrations and ornaments provide information about (the change of) printing techniques and artistic styles, decorative elements are formative for the (genre) perception of books and the development of reading habits, and book plates are relevant for provenance studies. As children's and youth books are especially rich in illustrations and since non-textual elements form an understudied aspect in the history of print, this data collection aims at stimulating research and development of AI applications in relevant fields. The dataset contains extracted images and metadata of historical titles that have been published between 1800 and 1925. Most probably, the social impact of the dataset is therefore very low.
column163: The files are suitable for the computational use of digitised collections according to the Collections as Data principles. There are at least three tasks for which the image dataset may be used: Task: "object detection in historical images". See as an example Kim et al. 2024. Task: "evolution of the presentation of printed books over time (ornaments, initials, number of illustrations per book, colouring etc.)". See as an example Megg's History of Graphic Design or Eisenstein's Printing Press as an Agent of Change. Task: "re-use of illustrations across printed publications". See for children's books Czernow et al. 2025. This dataset was not part of a shared task. Example use, evolution of iconography over time: https://leuris.uni-leipzig.de/portal/details/forschungsprojekt/4959. As children's and youth books are especially rich in illustrations, further possible uses of this dataset are the (automated) identification of artistic styles (e.g., art nouveau) and the formation of reading habits via visual elements. Finally, as this dataset contains several hundred printers' marks and bookplates, the dataset may contribute to studies on book history and on provenance as well. People using this dataset are welcome to provide feedback on this dataset and of the use made of it to the dataset curators. Type of Cultural Heritage Application: Stylistic Analysis / Machine Learning.
column176: This dataset does not contain data splits which can be used for machine learning tasks. It is advisable to first analyse the dataset and then perform the split(s), for example to split off portions of the dataset relative to the numbers of works published over time.
column184: Members of the research project "Human.Machine.Culture"|Trained librarians participating in a workshop
column185: Classifying the images
column189: Ground truth annotations were provided by members of the research project "Human.Machine.Culture" or by trained librarians participating in a workshop. All annotators were trained employees of a large cultural heritage institution accustomed to deal with assets coming from cultural history; however, no socio-demographic information on the annotators is available.
column198: Annotations were added with the aim of training a classifier which can be used on other digital collections as well. A description of the tool used (https://github.com/qurator-spk/sbb_images/blob/master/doc/annotator.md) as well as the annotation guidelines explaining the 16 classes (https://github.com/qurator-spk/sbb_images/blob/master/doc/Annotation-Guide.md) can be found on GitHub. On this basis, a classifier was trained and applied to the full dataset. The results of the classification were then evaluated, further annotations were added to broaden the groundtruth and the classifier was retrained. This process was repeated until the classifier reached an accuracy of 90% or more for each class. This process resulted in 33,397 manually annotated images or 37% of the 90,668 images contained in the full dataset. Annotations were not aggregated; rather, the iterative annotation process included mutual cross-checking of the labels provided by several annotators. The limitations of the annotation process lie in the fact that some of the classes cannot be conceived of in an unambiguous way. While some classes are clearly distinct (like table of colours or musical notations), classes like ornament, vignette and capital letters tend to overlap. However, multiple annotations of the same image were avoided for the sake of achieving strong classification results. Finally, it has to be noted that classes like Druckermarken (printers' marks) and Exlibris (book plates) were not reliably recognised by the classifier due to the multiple forms and colours they can take; with regard to these latter classes, all results have been checked and corrected by human annotators.
column201: The data publication was created by two librarians who participated in the project “Digitization of 19th Century Children’s and Youth Literature”, and a member of the team of the research project “Mensch.Maschine.Kultur – Künstliche Intelligenz für das Digitale Kulturelle Erbe” at Berlin State Library (SBB). Dr. Sebastian Schmideler, head of the department for children's and young people's literature at Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. As a scientist at the University of Leipzig, he initiated this project and supported it with great commitment until the application was successfully submitted. Dr. Schmideler was a member of the project's scientific advisory board and today, as head of the children's and young adult book department, is working on a follow-up application to enable the digitization of children's and young adult journals. Sigrun Putjenter, deputy head of the department for children's and young people's literature at Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. She was responsible for the selection of relevant titles from the State Library's collection and managed the project for the State Library as a participant in the Colibri project. Dr. Jörg Lehmann, project collaborator in the "Human.Machine.Culture" project at Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin – Berlin State Library. Jörg Lehmann has studied history and comparative literature. He was responsible for the selection and annotation of the illustrations contained in the dataset as well as for preparing the data for publication. The metadata are a collaborative product of the people performing scanning and of trained librarians.
